name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage that loads dynamic cheese museum content and routes visitors to visit planning, exhibitions, timeline, learning resources, tours & workshops, and shop sections, as well as to featured detail pages.

core_features: On page load, call getHomepageContent to retrieve hero metadata, primary navigation sections, and featured exhibits, programs, articles, and products.
Display a functional hero/action area using the hero object and expose at least one primary action that leads to planning a visit, based on the primary_sections data.

Render a grid/list of primary sections from primary_sections, each acting as a navigation entry point to visit.html, exhibitions.html, timeline.html, learn.html, tours_workshops.html, or shop.html according to section.target_page.
Show a featured exhibits list using featured_exhibits (Exhibit entities) with navigation to exhibit_detail.html?id={Exhibit.id}, plus a generic call-to-action to the full exhibitions.html page.
Show a featured programs list using featured_programs (Program entities) with navigation to program_detail.html?id={Program.id}, plus a call-to-action to tours_workshops.html.
Show a featured articles list using featured_articles (Article entities) with navigation to article_detail.html?id={Article.id}, plus a call-to-action to learn.html.

Show a featured products list using featured_products (Product entities) focused on shop items (e.g., books), with navigation to shop.html?category=books and optional product-focused parameters for deeper browsing.
Provide header-level navigation back to index.html (logo/home link) and to cart.html (cart icon) from the homepage.
Interpret optional URL parameters (e.g., section) to focus or scroll to a specific homepage section, using the IDs or target_page values from primary_sections for matching.
user_workflows:
Plan a visit from the homepage:
1. User lands on index.html.
2. homepage-data-loader component triggers getHomepageContent and populates hero, primary_sections, and featured_* data.
3. hero-action-panel identifies the primary_sections item whose target_page corresponds to the visit section and renders a primary action button with the section's dynamic label.
4. User clicks this primary action or the corresponding card in primary-section-grid.
5. primary-section-grid maps that item to visit.html and navigates there with a simple URL (no additional parameters required).

Explore a featured exhibit:
1. User lands on index.html and data is loaded via getHomepageContent.
2. featured-exhibits-list renders a list of Exhibit-based cards from featured_exhibits.
3. User clicks a featured exhibit card.
4. The component reads the Exhibit.id and navigates to exhibit_detail.html?id={Exhibit.id} using URL parameters.
5. The exhibit detail page can then use the id parameter to load detailed information (outside the scope of this page).

Browse all exhibitions from the homepage:
1. After getHomepageContent returns, featured-exhibits-list displays a secondary action such as a link based on the section whose target_page corresponds to exhibitions (resolved via primary_sections if needed).
2. User clicks this 'view all exhibitions' style action.
3. The component navigates to exhibitions.html without additional parameters.

Book a guided tour or workshop starting from a featured program:
1. User lands on index.html and homepage-data-loader retrieves featured_programs.
2. featured-programs-list displays Program-based cards showing key metadata (title, program_type, duration, etc.).
3. User clicks one of the program cards.
4. The component navigates to program_detail.html?id={Program.id}.
5. The program detail page then handles session selection and booking (not part of this page).

Access learning articles from homepage:
1. After loading content, featured-articles-list renders featured_articles as clickable items.
2. User clicks an article tile or title.
3. The component navigates to article_detail.html?id={Article.id}.
4. Alternatively, user clicks the 'all articles/resources' style action, which navigates to learn.html for broader browsing.

Go from homepage to shop books:
1. After getHomepageContent, featured-products-list shows featured_products.
2. User clicks a generic call-to-action associated with the shop section (e.g., derived from primary_sections where target_page='shop').
3. The component navigates to shop.html with URL parameters ensuring category=books (e.g., shop.html?category=books).
4. Optionally, if the user clicks a specific featured product card, the component can navigate to shop.html?category=books&highlight_product_id={Product.id} so the shop page can emphasize that product.

Use URL parameters to jump to a specific homepage section:
1. User arrives at index.html with a query string such as index.html?section={value}.
2. homepage-section-router parses the section parameter.
3. Once getHomepageContent resolves, homepage-section-router compares the section parameter value with available primary_sections[i].id and primary_sections[i].target_page values to determine which section should be active.
4. The router updates local state (e.g., activeSection) and requests a scroll/focus to the corresponding content block (such as exhibits, programs, articles, or shop products).
interactions: Click on logo or Home link in the header: navigates to index.html without additional query parameters, resetting any activeSection derived from the URL.
Click on cart icon in the header from the homepage: navigates directly to cart.html.

Click on a primary section card in primary-section-grid: reads that section's target_page field and navigates to the corresponding page (visit.html, exhibitions.html, timeline.html, learn.html, tours_workshops.html, or shop.html).
Click on the primary visit action in hero-action-panel: invokes the same navigation logic as clicking the visit-related primary section card, routing the user to visit.html.
Click on a featured exhibit card: navigates to exhibit_detail.html?id={Exhibit.id} where Exhibit.id comes from the clicked card's bound Exhibit entity.
Click on a featured guided tour or workshop card: navigates to program_detail.html?id={Program.id} using the bound Program entity.
Click on a featured article card or title: navigates to article_detail.html?id={Article.id} using the bound Article entity.
Click on the exhibitions call-to-action within the exhibits section: navigates to exhibitions.html without additional parameters.
Click on the tours & workshops call-to-action within the programs section: navigates to tours_workshops.html.
Click on the learn/articles call-to-action within the articles section: navigates to learn.html.
Click on the shop/books call-to-action within the products section: navigates to shop.html?category=books using the category value specified in the outgoing navigation definition.
Click on a featured product card: navigates to shop.html with at least category=books and may append highlight_product_id={Product.id} in the query string so the shop page can emphasize that product.

Page load of index.html with a section query parameter: homepage-section-router interprets URLSearchParams.section and, once data is loaded, sets the matching section as active and scrolls or focuses to its content.
Page load of index.html without parameters: homepage-data-loader still fetches all homepage content, and homepage-section-router defaults to a neutral state with no specific activeSection preference.
page_functionality.state_logic: The homepage does not persist state beyond the current page load and uses URL parameters for any stateful behavior. Supported patterns include: (1) index.html?section={value}: When present, the homepage-section-router reads this value from the query string. After getHomepageContent resolves, it matches the value against primary_sections[i].id and primary_sections[i].target_page. If a match is found, it sets activeSection to that identifier and requests that the corresponding section component (e.g., featured-exhibits-list, featured-programs-list, featured-articles-list, or featured-products-list) becomes focused or scrolled into view. If no match is found, the homepage behaves as if no section parameter was provided. (2) Navigation to detail and downstream pages always passes identifiers via URL parameters: exhibit_detail.html?id={Exhibit.id}, program_detail.html?id={Program.id}, and article_detail.html?id={Article.id}. (3) Navigation to the shop always passes a category query parameter for books: shop.html?category=books, optionally extended by this page to include highlight_product_id={Product.id} when a user clicks a specific featured product. (4) Navigations out of the homepage (to visit, exhibitions, timeline, learn, tours_workshops, and cart) do not rely on any hidden or client-side storage; all necessary context is conveyed through standard URL parameters when needed. The homepage itself uses only the getHomepageContent interface for data and recalculates its state on every page load based on the current URL and the API response.

components.functionality: On initial page load, call getHomepageContent once, handle loading and error states, and expose the resulting hero, primary_sections, featured_exhibits, featured_programs, featured_articles, and featured_products data to child components via a shared in-memory state or context.

Provide global navigation controls including a logo or home link that routes to index.html and a cart icon that routes to cart.html. Highlight the Home entry as the current page and do not depend on dynamic data.

Use the hero object from getHomepageContent to display contextual introductory information and determine one or more primary actions based on the primary_sections array, prioritizing the section that corresponds to planning a visit. When the user clicks the primary action, navigate to the appropriate target page derived from the selected primary_sections item.

Render all entries from primary_sections as interactive tiles or list items. Each entry uses its dynamic label and description from getHomepageContent. When a user clicks an entry, map section.target_page to its corresponding page: 'visit' -> visit.html, 'exhibitions' -> exhibitions.html, 'timeline' -> timeline.html, 'learn' -> learn.html, 'tours_workshops' -> tours_workshops.html, 'shop' -> shop.html?category=books (respecting the provided outgoing navigation for shop).

Display a list or grid of Exhibit-based cards using featured_exhibits from getHomepageContent. Each card binds its Exhibit.id for navigation and can show other Exhibit fields (title, region, era_label, etc.) for context. Clicking a card navigates to exhibit_detail.html?id={Exhibit.id}. The component also exposes a secondary action that navigates to exhibitions.html for users who want to browse all exhibitions.

Render featured_programs as a list of Program-based cards indicating attributes such as program_type, duration_minutes, language, and audience_type. Clicking a card navigates to program_detail.html?id={Program.id}. The component also includes a general call-to-action that routes to tours_workshops.html for a complete listing.

Render featured_articles as a list of clickable article items showing metadata such as title, topic, and estimated_reading_time_minutes. When a user clicks an article tile or title, navigate to article_detail.html?id={Article.id}. Provide a secondary navigation control that routes to learn.html for broader exploration of educational content.

Render featured_products as a list or grid of Product-based cards, focusing on items in the books category as provided by getHomepageContent. Each card uses Product.id, Product.category, and related fields such as price and currency for display. Clicking the section-level call-to-action navigates to shop.html?category=books. Clicking an individual product card navigates to shop.html with category=books and may append highlight_product_id={Product.id} to the URL so that the shop page can optionally emphasize that product.

Interpret query parameters from window.location.search on page load. If a section parameter is present, store its value and wait for getHomepageContent to complete. After data is available, try to match the section value against primary_sections[i].id and primary_sections[i].target_page. If a match exists, set activeSection to that identifier and request scroll/focus to the corresponding featured-* component. If no match exists, leave activeSection unset. The component does not fetch data itself; it only coordinates URL-derived state with already-loaded homepage content.

Expose a dedicated, always-visible trigger (cart icon or link) in the header that navigates directly to cart.html when clicked. This component does not inspect or modify the cart; it only handles navigation.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width grid similar to a 12-column system with a main content column and an internal 4-column image grid. Each image/caption acts like one column of four within the content width.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom narrative: a full-width global navigation/header at the very top, followed by a single large, centered content card that contains all homepage content. Inside this card, the sequence is: (1) hero-action-panel acting as the page heading and introductory narrative with key action, (2) primary-section-grid as a full-width row (or two) of main section tiles, visually echoing the mockup’s image row, (3) stacked featured lists (exhibits, programs, articles, products) as subsequent bands within the same content column. Non-visual components (data-loader, section-router) wrap and orchestrate this flow but do not disrupt the linear arrangement.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom narrative: a full-width global navigation/header at the very top, followed by a single large, centered content card that contains all homepage content. Inside this card, the sequence is: (1) hero-action-panel acting as the page heading and introductory narrative with key action, (2) primary-section-grid as a full-width row (or two) of main section tiles, visually echoing the mockup’s image row, (3) stacked featured lists (exhibits, programs, articles, products) as subsequent bands within the same content column. Non-visual components (data-loader, section-router) wrap and orchestrate this flow but do not disrupt the linear arrangement.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The navigation bar occupies roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height and spans 100% width. Below it, the main content card is centered and spans about 70–80% of the viewport width (max-width ~1100–1200px), with generous left/right margins of light grey background. Inside the card, the hero-action-panel takes approximately the top 35–40% of the card’s height, the primary-section-grid occupies the next 25–35%, and the remaining vertical space is for the stacked featured lists. Each featured list reads as a horizontal band within the same card, separated by 24–32px vertical spacing.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain moderate density: readable line lengths (60–75 characters), 1.4–1.6 line height, and clear 24–32px vertical spacing between major blocks (hero, section grid, each list). The primary-section-grid and each featured list use a tighter grid with relatively small horizontal gutters (~8–12px) between items to create a dense row of tiles, similar to the mockup’s historical photo band, while still preserving thin borders and clear separation.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from left to right across the top navigation (logo → primary navigation → search/language → cart), then down to the bold red hero title and introductory text. From there, the eye drops to the strong horizontal band of section tiles (primary-section-grid), which acts as a visual anchor. Below, the user scrolls through sequential featured lists, each introduced by a clear heading. A subtle scroll-to-top affordance can sit at the lower right of the viewport, visually secondary. Overall flow is vertical and orderly, with each band aligned to the same central column, reinforcing a traditional, editorial reading rhythm.

layout.overall_description: The homepage presents as a calm, editorial museum page: a full-width, light background with a solid, slightly old-fashioned navigation bar anchored at the top. Centered beneath this is a single, large white content card with gentle 24–32px padding on all sides. At the top of the card, a red serif page title and a concise block of descriptive copy set the tone, accompanied by one or two understated action buttons. Immediately below, a full-width row of four evenly sized tiles showcases the main site sections (Visit, Exhibitions, Timeline, Learning, Tours & Workshops, Shop), behaving much like the photo strip in the mockup; these tiles sit close together with small gutters, forming a strong horizontal band. As you scroll, the same central column continues downward into distinct blocks for featured exhibits, programs, articles, and products—each with a clear heading and a grid of items aligned to the same internal 4-column structure. The whole page feels structured, content-first, and slightly museum-like, with the focus on clear reading and exploration rather than flashy hero imagery.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual, structural component that underpins the entire page. Conceptually, it sits behind all other components, occupying the full page (100% width and height) but rendering no visible UI. It initializes and feeds data into the hero-action-panel, primary-section-grid, and all featured lists. In layout terms, it does not alter spacing or visual flow; it behaves like an invisible layer that wraps the entire viewport, ensuring that when content appears inside the centered main content card, it is already populated.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Background, non-visual process with no direct spatial footprint; logically wraps the whole page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation bar spans the full width of the viewport at the very top of the page. It has a fixed height that approximates 15–20% of the initial viewport height and contains a subtle but strong red branding accent (for example, a red bottom border or a red logo lockup on the left). Inside, content is aligned to the same centered max-width container as the main content card (about 70–80% of viewport width, max ~1100–1200px). Within this container, the museum logo sits at the far left, followed by primary navigation links in a horizontal row just to the right of the logo. On the far right side, in a single line, sit the language options, search field/icon, and the header-cart-link. Vertical padding is roughly 16–24px to keep the bar visually substantial but not bulky. The navigation’s background is solid (white or very light grey), with the rest of the page background slightly darker, making this bar a clear top frame for the content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky at the top on scroll (or fixed if desired), always occupying the top edge of the viewport and pushing the main content card down below it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart link is positioned within the primary-navigation-bar, aligned to the top-right portion of the centered navigation content area. It appears in the same horizontal line as the language selector and search icon/field, placed as the rightmost element to signify a transactional action. It uses a compact pill or icon-plus-text treatment with minimal padding (4–8px vertical, 8–12px horizontal), ensuring it feels like a utility rather than a primary call-to-action. There is a small horizontal gap (8–16px) between the cart link and the search control to keep utilities distinct while maintaining a tight, conservative header row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the navigation bar’s right-aligned utility cluster; its position is fixed relative to other header elements and moves with the sticky header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The hero-action-panel is the first visible section inside the main content card, directly below the navigation bar’s bottom edge with a vertical gap of about 32–48px between the header and the top of the card. The card is centered on the page, spanning roughly 70–80% of the viewport width with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. At the top left of this panel, a large page title in red serif type is set as a single line (or two at most), occupying the full width of the text column. Immediately beneath the title is one or two paragraphs of descriptive text about the museum and its cheese history, left-aligned and running across the central column (not exceeding ~65–75 characters per line). Within the paragraph, occasional colored links (e.g., green) and small inline icons can punctuate the text. Below the paragraph, aligned to the left, a row of one or two understated primary/secondary action buttons (e.g., “Plan Your Visit” as primary) sits with about 16–24px vertical spacing from the last line of text. This whole hero block occupies roughly the top 35–40% of the content card height before leading the eye down to the primary-section-grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored to the top of the main content card; it expands vertically with text but always precedes the grids and featured lists.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary-section-grid sits directly below the hero-action-panel within the same main content card, separated from the hero by approximately 24–32px of vertical whitespace. It stretches the full usable width of the card’s inner area (minus the 24–32px horizontal padding), aligning perfectly with the text column above. On desktop, it is arranged as an internal 4-column grid: four tiles per row, each tile occupying roughly one quarter of the content width. Small horizontal gutters of about 8–12px separate the tiles, mimicking the tightly packed photo row from the mockup. The first row might include the most important sections (e.g., Plan Your Visit, Exhibitions, Timeline, Learning), with any additional sections (Tours & Workshops, Shop) flowing onto a second row directly below, separated by a 16–24px vertical gap. Each tile is a simple flat card with a thin grey border, a small icon or thumbnail at the top, the section title in serif or strong type beneath, and a short caption or single-line description. Overall, this grid occupies approximately 25–35% of the content card height and forms a strong horizontal band anchoring the central content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, fluid grid that reflows responsively (e.g., collapsing to 2 columns on tablet, 1 column on narrow mobile) while maintaining the same centered alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-exhibits-list is the first of the stacked featured bands that appear below the primary-section-grid within the same main content card. There is a clear vertical gap of about 32px between the bottom of the section grid and the top of this list. A section heading such as “Featured Exhibitions” appears left-aligned at the top, set in a medium-weight serif or small caps style, using the same text column width as the hero copy. Immediately below the heading, a horizontal rule or subtle top border can separate the heading from the items. The list items then form a 3–4 column grid aligned to the same internal grid as the primary-section-grid (ideally 4 columns on large screens), with each exhibit card consisting of a small image thumbnail at the top, the exhibit title, and 1–2 lines of descriptive text. Item gutters are again 8–12px horizontally and 16–24px vertically. This band occupies roughly the next 20–25% of the card’s vertical space. The overall feel is a dense but orderly row (or rows) of exhibit teasers, clearly grouped under their heading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the vertical stack; expands vertically based on the number of featured items, always aligned to the central content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-programs-list sits directly below the featured-exhibits-list, separated by about 24–32px of vertical whitespace to create a distinct visual band. It follows nearly the same structure: a left-aligned heading such as “Programs & Workshops” at the top, using the same typographic style and column width as the other section headings, followed by a grid of program cards. Cards align to the same 4-column system as other grids and maintain the same gutters. Each card might show a small icon or date badge, a program title, and a short description or age group. This section is visually more about utility than drama, so it maintains the restrained, content-first approach: no oversized imagery, just consistent small thumbnails and text within the tiles. It uses a similar vertical height to the exhibits list, but may be slightly shorter if fewer items are featured.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and stacked, inheriting the central column alignment; reflows responsively into fewer columns on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-articles-list is placed beneath the programs list, again with 24–32px of vertical spacing. It keeps the same left-aligned section heading pattern (e.g., “From the Cheese Archive” or “Featured Articles”) and full-width alignment within the card. Below the heading, the layout can be slightly more editorial: items may be arranged in 2–3 columns to allow for longer article titles and excerpts, while still respecting the underlying 4-column grid (e.g., each article card could span 2 of the 4 columns). Each card contains a small thumbnail or icon at the top or left, the article title in a slightly larger serif, and a 2–3 line excerpt. Vertical spacing between article cards is 16–24px. This section feels more text-heavy, echoing the informational, museum publication tone, and can occupy a bit more vertical space than the previous lists if needed, but still bounded within the same central card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, text-oriented band that fits into the vertical stack; cards may span multiple grid columns but remain aligned to the same central grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-products-list is located toward the lower portion of the main content card, beneath the featured-articles-list, separated by another 24–32px of whitespace. Its heading (e.g., “Shop Highlights”) follows the same style as the others, left-aligned and occupying the text column width. Beneath the heading, a grid of product tiles aligns with the core 4-column structure—four products per row on desktop, with the familiar 8–12px horizontal gutters and 16–24px vertical gutters. Each tile is a compact product card with a small product image, name, and price; optional micro-copy (e.g., “Aged 24 months”) can appear in a smaller line. Unlike a bold e-commerce hero, this section stays understated and integrated with the museum aesthetic—thin borders, flat backgrounds, no overly large buttons. A subtle “View all products” text link can be positioned right-aligned under the last row of items, maintaining a conservative look. This section likely takes 15–20% of the card height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and stacked, part of the same central column; reflows to fewer columns as screen width decreases.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The homepage-section-router is an invisible organizational layer that conceptually wraps the hero-action-panel, primary-section-grid, and all featured lists within the main content card. In layout terms, it sits directly beneath the navigation bar and defines the single vertical column that all visible sections follow. It does not render any visible UI, borders, or spacing of its own, but it controls which subsets of sections (e.g., featured lists) are loaded or displayed based on state (such as seasonal content or user preferences). Visually, users only experience a continuous, uninterrupted stack of sections within the same centered card; the router simply governs which segments of that stack appear at any given time.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual orchestration layer that spans the full width of the main content card; it dictates the order and presence of sections without occupying its own visual space.

name: Visit & Tickets
title: Visit & Tickets
description: Provides practical visit information (opening hours, location, access, visitor tips) and lets visitors select a date, choose a ticket type, view available admission timeslots, select ticket quantities, and create a booking that can be added to the cart.

core_features: Load and display visitor information (opening hours, location, access details, visitor tips) using getVisitorInfo.
Display a Tickets & Hours section with a visit date picker that initializes from the URL “date” parameter when present, and triggers availability loading via getAdmissionAvailability.

List available ticket types for the selected date using data from getAdmissionAvailability.ticket_types and allow the visitor to choose an active ticket type, optionally preselected via the URL “ticket_type” parameter.
Display admission timeslots returned by getAdmissionAvailability.timeslots, filtered by the selected ticket type, and allow the visitor to select a timeslot.

Allow the visitor to set quantities per ticket category (adult, child_6_12, child, senior) aligned with the pricing fields exposed in getAdmissionAvailability, validating against the timeslot’s available capacity.
Create an admission booking for the chosen timeslot and quantities via createAdmissionBooking, passing add_to_cart=true to add it to the unified cart/visit summary.
Provide a clear navigation action to cart.html (Visit Summary / Cart) after a successful booking, so visitors can review their booking in the cart.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Check opening hours and basic visit info:
1. User opens visit.html (with or without URL parameters).
2. On page load, the system calls getVisitorInfo.
3. Visitor information (opening hours list, location, access info, visitor tips) is displayed in dedicated sections.
4. The user can read this information without interacting with the Tickets & Hours section.

Workflow 2 – Book tickets starting from another page with a preselected date:
1. User clicks a call-to-action on index.html/about.html/contact.html that links to visit.html with a “date” URL parameter.
2. On visiting visit.html, the page parses the “date” parameter and sets the initial visit date picker value to this date if it is valid.
3. The page calls getAdmissionAvailability(visit_date=parsed_date).
4. When availability data loads, the page determines a default ticket type (or uses the “ticket_type” URL parameter if present and valid) and highlights it in the ticket-type selector.
5. The timeslot list shows all timeslots for the selected ticket type on that date.
6. The user selects a timeslot from the list.
7. Ticket quantity controls become enabled; the user sets desired quantities per category while the interface validates that total requested tickets do not exceed the timeslot’s available_capacity.
8. The user submits the booking form (e.g., “Add tickets to cart”).
9. The page calls createAdmissionBooking with the selected timeslot_id, ticket counts, and add_to_cart=true.
10. If the response indicates success, the UI shows a confirmation state and enables a “View Cart / Visit Summary” action linking to cart.html.

Workflow 3 – Change date and ticket type to find better availability:
1. User arrives on visit.html (from any source) and the page loads availability for the initial date.
2. The user changes the visit date using the date picker.
3. The page updates the URL “date” parameter to the newly selected date and calls getAdmissionAvailability for this updated date.
4. The ticket-type selector refreshes to reflect ticket_types returned for the new date.
5. The user can switch between ticket types; each time the selected ticket type changes, the timeslot list filters to only show timeslots whose ticket_type_id matches the selected type.
6. The user selects a timeslot and sets quantities as in Workflow 2, then submits the booking and navigates to cart.html if desired.

Workflow 4 – Return from cart and adjust booking:
1. User goes from visit.html to cart.html after adding a booking.
2. If the user wants to adjust their visit time, they use browser back navigation or a navigation link to return to visit.html.
3. Because visit.html manages visit date and ticket type in the URL (date and ticket_type parameters), returning to visit.html with those parameters restores the same date and ticket-type context.
4. The page re-calls getAdmissionAvailability with the date from the URL and re-selects the ticket_type from the URL if valid.
5. The user selects a different timeslot and/or adjusts quantities and submits again; createAdmissionBooking is called to create another booking and add it to the cart.

Workflow 5 – Handling no-availability scenarios:
1. User selects or lands on a date for which getAdmissionAvailability returns no available timeslots (either the timeslots array is empty or all entries have is_available=false or available_capacity=0).
2. The page shows an informational message within the timeslot list component indicating that no admission times are available for that date/ticket type.
3. The timeslot selection and quantity controls remain disabled until the user selects a different date or, if available, a different ticket type.
4. The user changes the date or ticket type; getAdmissionAvailability is called again and the UI updates accordingly.
interactions: On page load: parse URL parameters (date, ticket_type); call getVisitorInfo once; determine the initial visit date; call getAdmissionAvailability with that date; initialize internal state for selected ticket type and timeslot selection.

Visit date picker interaction: when the user changes the date, the component updates the internal visit date state, updates the “date” URL parameter (using history.replaceState or equivalent), clears any selected timeslot and quantities, and triggers a new getAdmissionAvailability call for the new date.

Ticket type selector interaction: when the user selects a ticket type, the component updates the internal selected_ticket_type_code state, updates the “ticket_type” URL parameter, clears any selected timeslot and quantities, and filters the timeslot list to only show timeslots that match the selected ticket type.

Timeslot selection interaction: when the user clicks/selects a timeslot row or card, the timeslot list component marks it as selected, stores its timeslot_id and available_capacity in state, and enables the ticket quantity form for editing.

Ticket quantity adjustment interaction: for each supported category (adult, child_6_12, child, senior) indicated by presence of corresponding price fields in the selected ticket type data, the user can increment/decrement a numeric input; the form validates that all counts are non-negative integers, that at least one ticket is selected, and that the sum of all categories does not exceed the selected timeslot’s available_capacity.

Booking form submission: when the user submits the form (e.g., clicks an 'Add tickets to cart' button), the component validates inputs, then calls createAdmissionBooking with timeslot_id from the selected timeslot and the chosen category counts; add_to_cart is set to true. While waiting for the response, the component shows a loading state and prevents duplicate submissions.

Booking response handling: if createAdmissionBooking returns success=true, the UI displays confirmation text based on the message field, may display summary data from booking or cart_item, and enables a 'View Cart' button linking to cart.html. If success=false, the UI displays the message as an error and keeps the form data, so the user can adjust and retry.

Navigation to cart: when the user clicks the 'View Cart' / 'Visit Summary' button or link, the browser navigates to cart.html without additional parameters (cart context is assumed to be handled globally by the system).

Error/reload interactions: if getVisitorInfo or getAdmissionAvailability fail (e.g., network error), the responsible components show an error message with a 'Retry' action; on retry click, they re-call the same interface with the last used parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: URL parameters are the primary mechanism for preserving and sharing state across navigation. The visit.html page reads and writes: (1) 'date' – a string in YYYY-MM-DD format representing the selected visit date. On initial load, if 'date' is present and parsable as a valid date, it is used as the initial visit date; otherwise, the component falls back to a dynamically determined default date (for example, the current date from the client environment). When the user changes the date in the date picker, the page updates this parameter (using query string modification) and triggers getAdmissionAvailability with the new date. When the user navigates away and later returns to visit.html with a 'date' parameter, the previously selected date context is restored. (2) 'ticket_type' – a string corresponding to a ticket type code such as 'general_entry'. After getAdmissionAvailability returns, the page checks whether any ticket_types in the response have ticket_type.code equal to the 'ticket_type' parameter. If so, that ticket type is selected; if not, the page selects a default ticket type from the returned list (for example, the first active entry). When the user changes the selection in the ticket type selector, the page updates the 'ticket_type' parameter accordingly. Together, these two parameters fully define the context for availability loading and filtering; they are always synchronized with the UI whenever the user interacts with the date picker or ticket type selector. Other state such as selected_timeslot_id, total_requested_quantity, latest_booking_result, and error flags for each interface call are kept in in-memory page/state variables and are not persisted to URL parameters or localStorage. Whenever the visit date or ticket type changes, the page clears dependent state (selected timeslot and quantities) to avoid inconsistent combinations. No assumptions are made about static values (such as fixed opening hours, currencies, or prices); all such data is read from the respective interface responses at runtime.

components.functionality: On page initialization, calls getVisitorInfo to retrieve opening hours, location, access information, and visitor tips, then passes this data to downstream components for display.

Displays visitor information (opening hours list, location details, access information, and visitor tips) based on the data provided by visitor-info-loader, including handling loading and error states.

Orchestrates the Tickets & Hours workflow: manages the selected visit date and ticket type, triggers availability loading via getAdmissionAvailability, and coordinates the date picker, ticket type selector, timeslot list, and ticket quantity form.

Provides a controllable date input for selecting the intended visit date. Initializes from the URL 'date' parameter or a dynamic default, and notifies the tickets-and-hours-section of changes so it can reload availability and update the 'date' URL parameter.

Displays the list of available ticket types for the selected date using getAdmissionAvailability.ticket_types. Lets users choose a ticket type, applies the URL 'ticket_type' parameter on initialization when it matches a returned type, and updates the URL when the selection changes.

Shows the list of admission timeslots returned by getAdmissionAvailability.timeslots, filtered by the currently selected ticket type. Each item displays its start and end time, availability status, and note. Users can select a single timeslot, which then enables the ticket quantity form.

Displays informational messages related to timeslot availability, such as when no timeslots are available for the selected date/ticket type or when all capacity is booked. Reacts to the timeslots data, selected ticket type, and availability flags.

Allows the user to specify the number of tickets per supported category (adult, child_6_12, child, senior) based on which pricing fields are present for the selected ticket type. Validates that quantities are non-negative, that at least one ticket is selected, and that total requested quantity does not exceed the selected timeslot’s available capacity.

Submits the current selection (timeslot and quantities) to create a booking and add it to the cart by calling createAdmissionBooking with add_to_cart=true. Disables itself while a request is in progress and relays the response to the booking-feedback component.

Encapsulates the createAdmissionBooking interface call. Receives timeslot_id and ticket category counts from the ticket-quantity-form and admission-booking-submit components, calls createAdmissionBooking with add_to_cart set to true, and exposes the resulting booking and cart_item data along with success or failure state.

Shows feedback after a booking attempt, using the success flag and message from createAdmissionBooking. On success, it may display key booking details derived from the booking object and reveals navigation actions such as a 'View Cart' button. On error, it displays the message and offers a retry action.

Provides a clear navigation path to the visit summary/cart page. It becomes active once at least one booking has been successfully added to the cart (as indicated by a successful createAdmissionBooking response). Clicking it navigates the user to cart.html.

Shows loading and error states specifically for the getAdmissionAvailability call. Displays when availability is being fetched for the current date and hides when data or an error is returned; on error, it offers a retry that re-calls getAdmissionAvailability for the current visit date.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width grid similar to a 12-column system with a main content column and an internal 4-column image grid. Each image/caption acts like one column of four within the content width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom, centered layout inside a fixed-width main content card. Above (outside this spec) is the global red brand/header bar. Inside the white content card, the order is: (1) the page title 'Visit & Tickets' (template, not listed), (2) the visitor-info-loader / visitor-info-panel occupying the upper narrative portion as a full-width text block, (3) the tickets-and-hours-section as a structured sub-section beneath the text. Inside tickets-and-hours-section, content is arranged in a two-column internal grid: the left column stacks visit-date-picker, ticket-type-selector, and ticket-quantity-form; the right column stacks timeslot-availability-message, timeslot-list, and availability-loading-indicator. Below these columns sit admission-booking-submit, booking-feedback-banner, and cart-link-button, aligned to the grid. Data-loader and data-mutator components do not appear as permanent visual elements; they only influence loading and feedback states via placeholders and overlays.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom, centered layout inside a fixed-width main content card. Above (outside this spec) is the global red brand/header bar. Inside the white content card, the order is: (1) the page title 'Visit & Tickets' (template, not listed), (2) the visitor-info-loader / visitor-info-panel occupying the upper narrative portion as a full-width text block, (3) the tickets-and-hours-section as a structured sub-section beneath the text. Inside tickets-and-hours-section, content is arranged in a two-column internal grid: the left column stacks visit-date-picker, ticket-type-selector, and ticket-quantity-form; the right column stacks timeslot-availability-message, timeslot-list, and availability-loading-indicator. Below these columns sit admission-booking-submit, booking-feedback-banner, and cart-link-button, aligned to the grid. Data-loader and data-mutator components do not appear as permanent visual elements; they only influence loading and feedback states via placeholders and overlays.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content card is centered and spans about 70–75% of the viewport width, with soft grey page background margins on both sides. The global header above occupies roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height; the content card fills the remaining 80–85%. Inside the card, the top narrative section (visitor-info-panel) consumes roughly the upper 35–40% of the card’s height, while the tickets-and-hours-section occupies the lower 60–65%. Within tickets-and-hours-section, the internal two-column layout gives about 40% of the width to the left column (date/ticket controls) and 60% to the right column (timeslot list). Vertically, each control block (date picker, ticket type, quantity, timeslot list) is separated by ~24–32px, following the base 8px spacing scale. The booking-feedback-banner, when visible, stretches full width beneath the interactive area, with ~24px margin above and below.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a moderate, editorial density. Text in the visitor-info-panel is set in comfortable line lengths (approximately 60–75 characters per line), with 1.4–1.6 line-height and 24–32px spacing between paragraphs. Controls inside the tickets-and-hours-section are not overly spread out: labels are close to their fields, and related controls stack with 16–24px gaps. The two-column layout in the booking area slightly increases density, echoing the mockup’s multi-column image row while staying readable. Gutters between the left and right booking columns are narrow (around 16–24px), reminiscent of the tight image gutters in the mockup, to create a cohesive block of practical information and controls.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the strong red brand bar and logo (global), down to the bold red page title at the top of the card, then directly into the visitor-info-panel where practical visiting details are read first. From there, the visual flow drops into the tickets-and-hours-section: the left column’s visit-date-picker is the first interactive focal point, then the eye moves right to the timeslot-availability-message and timeslot-list, mirroring the horizontal sweep of the mockup’s image row. After selecting times and types, the eye is drawn downward to the prominent red admission-booking-submit button. Below that, the booking-feedback-banner—using subtle colored backgrounds—provides confirmation or error status, and the cart-link-button in a quieter style offers a secondary path to the cart. The availability-loading-indicator appears centrally within the timeslot-list area, momentarily capturing attention without disrupting the overall vertical reading flow.

layout.overall_description: The Visit & Tickets page feels like a calm, editorial museum information sheet with a practical booking tool embedded into it. A centered white content card floats against a soft grey background, framed by the museum’s red header above. Inside the card, a large red serif title announces ‘Visit & Tickets’, followed by a generous block of well-spaced text explaining opening hours, location, access, and tips—this is the narrative anchor of the page, just like the descriptive text in the mockup. Beneath that, a subtle divider introduces the tickets-and-hours-section, which behaves like the mockup’s horizontal image band, but here it is a two-column booking grid: the left side gathers date and ticket choices in a vertical stack, while the right side shows available admission times as a dense, scrollable list. The components sit close enough to feel like one cohesive booking console, yet have consistent 24–32px breathing room. A strong red booking button anchors the bottom of this block, with status banners and a reserved cart link tucked neatly below. The entire layout reads from top to bottom with a clear, steady rhythm: information first, then structured choices, then decisive action, all inside a traditional, trustworthy visual language.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visitor-info-loader occupies the same region as the visitor-info-panel at the top of the main content card, directly below the page title and above the tickets-and-hours-section. While data is loading, it presents as a pale, full-width placeholder block centered within the card, spanning roughly 80–90% of the card’s width (aligned with the text column) and about 25–30% of the card’s height. Within this block, a few horizontal grey skeleton lines (simulating two or three paragraphs) are stacked with 8–12px gaps, echoing the editorial text rhythm. There is a 24–32px vertical gap between the page title and this loader block, and another 24–32px gap between the loader and the top edge of the tickets-and-hours-section. Once loading completes, this placeholder collapses completely and is visually replaced by the fully rendered visitor-info-panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears only while data is being fetched; occupies static space in the content flow and then collapses to zero height once content is ready.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visitor-info-panel is positioned in the upper part of the main content card, directly beneath the page title. It spans almost the full inner width of the card (about 80–90% of the card width), centered, matching the main text column. This panel consists of several paragraphs of serif body text, optionally including inline colored links and small icons, formatted in a single column. There is approximately 24–32px of white space above it (from the title) and around 32px of white space below it before the tickets-and-hours-section begins. Vertically, the text panel occupies roughly 35–40% of the card’s height when content is typical. The edges of this panel align with the content grid used below, so the left edge of the first character and the left edge of controls in the tickets-and-hours-section visually line up, reinforcing the single editorial column feeling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level content that retains its position and spacing regardless of interaction; grows vertically as text length increases.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tickets-and-hours-section starts approximately 32px below the bottom of the visitor-info-panel and occupies the lower half to two-thirds of the main content card. It is presented as a clearly defined sub-section with a subtle top divider (a fine grey line or slight top padding) to distinguish it from the narrative text above. Inside, the content is organized into a two-column grid within the same fixed content width: the left column uses about 40% of the card’s width, the right column around 60%. A vertical gutter of about 16–24px runs between them. The top edges of both columns align horizontally, creating a unified band of booking controls that echoes the mockup’s tight horizontal image row. The left column stacks visit-date-picker, ticket-type-selector, and ticket-quantity-form from top to bottom with 24px gaps. The right column stacks timeslot-availability-message at the top, followed by timeslot-list with the availability-loading-indicator appearing within this zone as needed. Below the two columns, aligned with the left column, is the admission-booking-submit button with about 24px top margin. The booking-feedback-banner, when present, spans the full width of the section beneath the button, and the cart-link-button is aligned to the right side of the section’s content width, either on the same horizontal row as the banner or just below it, maintaining a tidy lower edge. Overall, this section reads visually as a single, dense booking console inside the larger editorial card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container that defines a local two-column grid; its height expands based on the content of its children, but its lateral alignment within the card remains fixed and centered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visit-date-picker is the first element inside the left column of the tickets-and-hours-section, sitting at the top-left corner of the booking grid. It aligns vertically with the top of the timeslot-availability-message on the right. The control’s label and field span the full width of the left column, with the label in a serif style above and the calendar input or date field below, separated by about 8px. The overall block (label plus control) is about 64–80px tall, depending on control style, with 24px of vertical space below it before the ticket-type-selector begins. Left and right padding inside the column is modest (about 16–24px from the card edge), keeping it aligned with the editorial text above. This makes the date picker the primary interactive element users encounter after reading the descriptions, anchoring the left side of the booking area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form control occupying the top of the left column; remains in place while other right-column elements and banners update around it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ticket-type-selector sits directly below the visit-date-picker in the left column of the tickets-and-hours-section, with approximately 24px of vertical space between them. It occupies the full width of the left column, mirroring the date picker’s alignment and padding. The label appears at the top of this block with 8px spacing before the actual selection UI (e.g., a dropdown or a vertical list of radio options). If multiple ticket categories are shown, radio or pill options are stacked with about 8–12px spacing, but the entire group does not extend beyond the left column’s width. There is a further 24px gap below this component before the ticket-quantity-form, forming a clearly segmented yet visually continuous vertical flow of controls in the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static selector that expands vertically based on the number of ticket types but remains constrained to the left column’s width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timeslot-list occupies the central lower portion of the right column within the tickets-and-hours-section. It sits directly below the timeslot-availability-message with about 16px of vertical gap. The list spans the full width of the right column, with modest horizontal padding (16–24px) to align with the section’s grid. Vertically, it can grow to fill most of the right column’s height, roughly 60–70% of the tickets-and-hours-section’s vertical space. Time slots are presented in a compact vertical list or grid of rows, each row taking a small height (32–40px) with 8–12px spacing between rows. If slots exceed the visible area, this zone may scroll internally, but its top and left edges remain locked to the right column grid. During loading states, the availability-loading-indicator appears centered within this list area, slightly dimming or overlaying the existing content while preserving the same spatial footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrollable or expanding content region confined to the right column; during loading, a semi-opaque overlay and spinner may appear centered within this area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timeslot-availability-message is placed at the very top of the right column in the tickets-and-hours-section, horizontally aligned with the top of the visit-date-picker on the left. It behaves like a short, single-line or two-line informational text block summarizing availability (e.g., ‘Available time slots for your selected date’). This text spans the full width of the right column but remains visually light—smaller type or subtle color—so it doesn’t overpower the main controls. There is a 8–12px gap between this message and the upper boundary of the right column, and a 16px gap below it before the timeslot-list begins. This placement guides the user’s eye from the left side (date) across to the right side (availability context) before they dive into the details of the timeslot list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static informational text at the top of the right column; remains fixed in place while the timeslot list and loading indicator beneath it update.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ticket-quantity-form is positioned in the left column of the tickets-and-hours-section, directly below the ticket-type-selector, with about 24px of vertical space between them. It occupies the full column width, with any internal fields (e.g., Adult, Child, Senior quantity steppers) arranged either in a vertical list or a simple two-column mini-grid within this area. Labels for each kind of ticket are left-aligned, with numeric inputs or steppers to their right, maintaining 8–12px spacing between label and input. Rows within this mini-form have about 8–12px vertical spacing, but the entire block is kept visually compact to echo the moderate density of the page. Beneath the ticket-quantity-form, there is a 24–32px vertical gap before the admission-booking-submit button, giving the user a small pause before the primary call to action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form component that grows vertically if many ticket categories exist but remains fully contained within the left column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The admission-booking-submit button is placed below the left and right columns of the booking grid, visually anchored to the left column. It sits about 24–32px beneath the ticket-quantity-form, left-aligned with the column’s content edge. The button is a strong red rectangular element, about 44–48px tall, with horizontal padding of 16–24px on each side, making it noticeably wider than individual form fields but still within the left column width. On larger screens, it does not stretch all the way to the right column; instead, it maintains the two-column structure above while providing a clear focal point at the bottom-left of the booking area. On smaller screens, this button can expand to nearly full card width to remain prominent. There is about 16–24px of space beneath the button before any booking-feedback-banner appears, so the button feels anchored yet not cramped against other elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static primary action button; its width may be responsive (column-width on desktop, near full-width on mobile) but its vertical position remains beneath the form controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The admission-booking-api is not directly visible to users. It operates behind the scenes, driving the state of the admission-booking-submit action and updating the booking-feedback-banner and availability-loading-indicator. Spatially, it does not claim any dedicated area in the layout. Its only visual manifestation is through state changes: disabling/enabling the submit button, showing a brief inline loading state on the button, or triggering the display of the booking-feedback-banner beneath the button.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No inherent visual footprint; influences other components’ states and thus indirectly affects their layout visibility and dynamic height (e.g., showing/hiding banners).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-feedback-banner appears directly beneath the admission-booking-submit button when a booking attempt has resulted in an error, warning, or success that needs to be communicated. It spans the full width of the tickets-and-hours-section’s content area (matching the overall content width of both columns combined), creating a horizontal band that visually ties together the left and right sides of the booking grid. The banner has a slightly tinted background color (e.g., pale green for success, pale red or amber for error/warning) and 12–16px of inner padding on all sides. There is about 16–24px of vertical space between the button and the top of the banner, and another 24px below it before the bottom margin of the section. This placement ensures feedback is prominent but still subordinate to the main flow and does not push the entire booking area too far down the page when not needed (the banner collapses completely when hidden).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically; when visible, it pushes page content below it downward, expanding the tickets-and-hours-section vertically. When hidden, it collapses to zero height without leaving extra spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-link-button is positioned within the lower part of the tickets-and-hours-section as a secondary, subtle navigation control. It aligns to the right edge of the section’s content width, typically on the same horizontal line as the bottom edge of the booking-feedback-banner or, if the banner is not visible, about 16–24px below the admission-booking-submit button. The cart link is styled as a text-forward button or a low-emphasis ghost button (outline only), about 32–40px tall, with modest horizontal padding. This right-aligned placement mirrors the mockup’s peripheral elements (like a scroll-to-top arrow), making the cart link easy to find without competing with the main booking action. On smaller screens, it remains horizontally aligned to the right within the card’s width, maintaining a clean lower-right utility area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static alignment to the right inside the section; may only appear when a cart is non-empty, in which case its appearance slightly extends the section height downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability-loading-indicator sits within the right column of the tickets-and-hours-section, occupying the same visual zone as the timeslot-list. When active, it appears centered both horizontally and vertically within the list’s visible area—typically a small spinner or circular progress indicator with a short ‘Loading availability…’ text below it. The rest of the timeslot-list area may be lightly dimmed or replaced by skeleton lines, but the overall footprint of the right column remains unchanged. This ensures that while availability is being fetched, the user’s eye remains anchored in the same region where time slots will appear, maintaining continuity in the visual flow from left controls to right results, without shifting other elements up or down.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlays or temporarily replaces the timeslot-list content within the right column without altering the column’s size or the position of surrounding components.

name: Exhibitions
title: Exhibitions
description: Browse museum exhibits focused on cheese history, with filters, sorting, and options to build a self-guided route.

core_features: Load available exhibit filter and sort options using getExhibitFilterOptions and initialize the filter controls based on URL parameters (region, era, sort) when present.
Display an 'Explore Exhibits' call-to-action that scrolls the viewport to the main exhibits list section.

Provide filter controls for region and era/time period (including any option corresponding to 'before_1500' when returned by getExhibitFilterOptions), and any additional filter/sort options exposed by the interface.
List exhibits using listExhibits with the currently selected filters, sort option, and pagination parameters, showing key exhibit details and actions.
Allow users to open a dedicated exhibit detail page (exhibit_detail.html) by clicking on an exhibit’s title or image, passing the exhibit.id as a URL parameter.
Allow users to add an exhibit directly to their self-guided route from the list by calling addExhibitToRoute, and show inline summary feedback based on the returned route and stop data.
Provide a 'View Self-Guided Tour' navigation action to my_route.html once at least one exhibit has been added to the route.
Support pagination of exhibits using listExhibits.page, listExhibits.page_size, and listExhibits.total_count, synchronized with URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Browse exhibits using incoming presets from another page:
1) User clicks a link to exhibitions.html from another page (e.g., index.html, about.html, my_route.html) that includes one or more URL parameters: region, era, and/or sort.
2) On page load, the page controller parses the current URL’s query string to extract region, era, sort, and page (if present).
3) The controller calls getExhibitFilterOptions and populates the filter and sort controls; it preselects the controls whose values match the URL parameters, if those values exist in the returned options.
4) The controller then calls listExhibits with filters built from the URL (e.g., filters.region = region, filters.era = era), sort_by = sort (if present), and a resolved page and page_size.
5) The exhibits list renders according to these filters and sort order.
6) The user clicks the 'Explore Exhibits' CTA, which scrolls to the exhibits list section where the filtered results are visible.

Manually refine filters and sorting:
1) User adjusts the region filter using the region control populated from getExhibitFilterOptions.regions.
2) The filter component updates the region value in the URL query string (adding or replacing the region parameter and resetting the page parameter to the first page).
3) The page controller observes the change (or handles it directly in the event handler) and calls listExhibits with the new filters and existing sort option.
4) The exhibits list updates to reflect the new region filter.
5) User changes the era filter using options from getExhibitFilterOptions.era_options (including any option whose value corresponds to 'before_1500' when provided).
6) The era parameter in the URL is updated, the page is reset to the first page, and listExhibits is called again with the updated filters.
7) User selects a different sort option from getExhibitFilterOptions.sort_options; the sort parameter in the URL is updated and listExhibits is called with the new sort_by value.

Paginate through exhibits:
1) After a listExhibits call, the pagination component computes the total number of pages from total_count and page_size.
2) User clicks a pagination control (e.g., next page, previous page, specific page index).
3) The pagination component updates the page parameter in the URL query string while preserving region, era, and sort.
4) The page controller calls listExhibits with the same filters and sort_by but a new page value.
5) The exhibits list re-renders with results for the selected page while retaining the applied filters and sorting.

Add exhibits to a self-guided route and view the route:
1) User scans the exhibits list and clicks an 'Add to Route' action on a specific exhibit entry.
2) The exhibit list item component calls addExhibitToRoute with the exhibit_id of the selected exhibit.
3) On success, the route summary component updates using the returned SelfGuidedRoute object and the list of RouteStop entries, including updated total_estimated_visit_time_minutes, total_estimated_walking_time_minutes, and total_estimated_duration_minutes.
4) The UI shows confirmation feedback (e.g., updated route totals) and indicates that the exhibit is now part of the route (for example, disabling or changing the state of the 'Add to Route' action for that exhibit).
5) If this is the first exhibit added to the route, the 'View Self-Guided Tour' navigation control becomes visible or enabled.
6) User clicks 'View Self-Guided Tour', which navigates to my_route.html (without adding or modifying URL parameters; the page will rely on the current self-guided route state managed elsewhere).

Open an exhibit detail page:
1) User clicks on the title or main image of an exhibit in the list.
2) The exhibit list item component reads the exhibit.id from its bound Exhibit object.
3) The browser navigates to exhibit_detail.html with ?id=<exhibit.id> in the query string.
4) The detail page can then load and display the specific exhibit based on this id.
interactions: Click 'Explore Exhibits' button: Scrolls the page to the exhibits list section anchor and does not alter filters or sorting; the list is already loaded based on current URL parameters and filter controls.
Change a region filter control: Sets or updates the region parameter in the URL; resets the page parameter to the first page; triggers a new listExhibits call with updated filters.
Change an era filter control: Sets or updates the era parameter in the URL; resets the page parameter; triggers a new listExhibits call with updated filters.
Change a sort option: Sets or updates the sort parameter in the URL; keeps the current filters; resets the page parameter; triggers a new listExhibits call with the new sort_by value.
Use pagination controls: Updates the page parameter in the URL; calls listExhibits with the same filters and sort_by but the new page, then refreshes the list.
Click exhibit title or image: Navigates to exhibit_detail.html?id=<exhibit.id> using the id from the bound Exhibit object.

Click 'Add to Route' on an exhibit: Calls addExhibitToRoute with the exhibit_id; on success, updates the local representation of the current route state (route and stops) and route summary display; adjusts the button state for that exhibit.
Click 'View Self-Guided Tour': Navigates to my_route.html without altering exhibit filters or route content.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Exhibitions page uses the URL query string as the single source of truth for its browse state. Recognized URL parameters include: region (string), era (string), sort (string), and page (number). Additional internal parameters such as page_size may be read from the URL if present, otherwise an application-level default is used when calling listExhibits.

On initial load: The page controller parses the current URL. It calls getExhibitFilterOptions first to obtain regions, era_options, and sort_options. Then it aligns the current state with the URL: if region, era, or sort parameters match values present in the returned options, those options are marked as selected in the filter and sort controls; otherwise, the controls fall back to a default option (such as an 'all' option) defined by the returned configuration or by application rules. The current page is derived from the page parameter if it is a valid positive integer; otherwise, the first page is used. The controller then constructs a filters object (e.g., including region and era values drawn directly from the URL when present) and calls listExhibits with filters, sort_by (from the sort URL parameter when present), page, and page_size.

On user interaction: Whenever the user changes filters or sorting through the UI, the corresponding URL parameters (region, era, sort) are updated using browser history APIs or a full reload. Each change also resets the page parameter to indicate the first page. Pagination interactions update only the page parameter. After any change to these parameters, listExhibits is called again with filters and sort_by derived from the updated URL, ensuring that the rendered list always matches the query string.

Route state: The addExhibitToRoute interface manages the underlying SelfGuidedRoute and RouteStop records. The Exhibitions page does not add route identifiers to the URL; instead, it uses the return value of addExhibitToRoute to show in-page route-related feedback (such as total estimated duration) and to determine whether to show or enable the 'View Self-Guided Tour' navigation. The actual route content is not encoded in the Exhibitions URL but in the backend state associated with the agent.

Navigation: Navigating to exhibit_detail.html preserves the current Exhibitions page URL, but the detail page is reached with a new URL that includes id=<exhibit.id>. Navigating to my_route.html from the 'View Self-Guided Tour' control opens the route overview page without passing exhibit filters; the route page independently loads the current self-guided route for the agent.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, state synchronization between filters/sorting/pagination and the exhibits list, and delegates to getExhibitFilterOptions, listExhibits, and addExhibitToRoute via child components.
Displays a prominent 'Explore Exhibits' button that, when clicked, scrolls the page to the exhibits list section so users can begin browsing results.

Renders the region and era filter controls (and any other filter options provided) using options from getExhibitFilterOptions, initializes their selected values based on URL parameters, and updates the URL and exhibits list when the user changes filters.

Displays sorting options for the exhibits list using sort_options from getExhibitFilterOptions, sets the initial selection based on the sort URL parameter, and updates the URL and triggers listExhibits when the sort order changes.
Displays the current page of exhibits returned from listExhibits, including titles, key metadata, navigation to exhibit_detail.html, and access to the 'Add to Route' action for each exhibit.

Represents a single exhibit from the exhibits array returned by listExhibits, exposing its id, title, summary, and other key information, and providing controls for opening the detail page and adding the exhibit to the self-guided route.

Uses listExhibits.total_count, listExhibits.page, and listExhibits.page_size to render pagination; when the user changes the page, updates the page parameter in the URL and triggers a new listExhibits call via the page controller.

Shows a concise summary of the current self-guided route after addExhibitToRoute is called, including aggregated visit and walking time based on the returned SelfGuidedRoute and RouteStop data, and indicates that at least one exhibit is in the route.

Provides a 'View Self-Guided Tour' link/button that becomes visible or enabled once the route-summary-inline component indicates there is at least one RouteStop; when activated, navigates to my_route.html.

Calls getExhibitFilterOptions on initialization, passes the resulting regions, era_options, and sort_options to the exhibit-filter-bar and exhibit-sort-control components, and ensures these components only render after options are available.

Builds the filters, sort_by, page, and page_size parameters based on the current URL query string and invokes listExhibits; then forwards the resulting exhibits, total_count, page, and page_size data to the exhibit-list and pagination-controls components.

Receives 'Add to Route' actions from exhibit-list-item components, calls addExhibitToRoute with the exhibit_id, and updates the route-summary-inline and view-route-cta components with the returned route and stops.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width grid similar to a 12-column system with a main content column and an internal 4-column image grid. Each image/caption acts like one column of four within the content width.
layout.layout_pattern: Preserve the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom narrative flow inside a single centered content card. The global museum header (logo, language, search) remains at the very top. Directly beneath, a large white 'Exhibitions' page card (the page controller) is centered on the page. Inside the card, the content flows as: page title and descriptive paragraph at the top, followed by a primary call-to-action, then the inline route summary, then a single horizontal band of filters and sorting, and finally a multi-row grid of exhibit cards with pagination at the bottom. Each exhibit card echoes the mockup’s image-plus-caption pattern, adapted into a grid of repeated items rather than a single row.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Preserve the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom narrative flow inside a single centered content card. The global museum header (logo, language, search) remains at the very top. Directly beneath, a large white 'Exhibitions' page card (the page controller) is centered on the page. Inside the card, the content flows as: page title and descriptive paragraph at the top, followed by a primary call-to-action, then the inline route summary, then a single horizontal band of filters and sorting, and finally a multi-row grid of exhibit cards with pagination at the bottom. Each exhibit card echoes the mockup’s image-plus-caption pattern, adapted into a grid of repeated items rather than a single row.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height. Below it, the white exhibitions card takes up the remaining central area, spanning about 70–80% of the viewport width and centered with light grey page margins. Within this card, the introductory heading and description (plus explore CTA and route summary) together take around the top 30–40% of the card’s vertical space, the filter and sort controls occupy a compact horizontal strip just beneath, and the exhibit list grid consumes the lower 50–60% of the card area. Each exhibit card uses roughly one third (or up to one quarter on very wide screens) of the inner content width, aligning to an internal 3–4 column grid with narrow gutters that mirror the mockup’s 4-column image row.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain moderate density: comfortable line lengths, clear paragraph spacing, and a calm editorial feel, but no excessive whitespace. The introductory text and route summary have slightly more breathing room (24–32px vertical gaps), while the filter/sort bar and exhibit cards are more tightly packed to allow many items to be visible at once. Exhibit cards follow the mockup’s denser image row: small but consistent gutters between cards, thin borders, and compact captions. Overall, the page feels information-rich but not cramped.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual journey begins with the strong red museum header bar and logo at the top, then drops into the centered white 'Exhibitions' card. The eye first hits the bold red page title, then moves down through the descriptive paragraph with highlighted inline links. From there it encounters the primary 'Explore Exhibits' CTA, then the subtle but important route summary with a right-aligned 'View Route' link. Next, attention slides into the horizontal filter and sort strip, and finally rests on the grid of exhibit cards, which act like a repeating band of image + caption elements. At the very bottom, pagination closes the vertical flow. Supporting elements like loaders and action handlers appear only within the bounds of these primary areas and never disrupt the linear, top-to-bottom reading order.

layout.overall_description: The page sits within a classic museum-style frame: a red global header with the logo on the left and utilities on the right spans the full width at the top. Beneath it, centered in the viewport, a large white card defines the 'Exhibitions' area, occupying about three quarters of the page width and surrounded by soft grey background margins. Inside this card, a bold red serif title 'Exhibitions' anchors the top left, followed by one or two lines of descriptive body text that introduce cheese-history exhibits and the self-guided route concept, with the occasional green-toned text link punctuating the paragraph. Just below, an 'Explore Exhibits' button sits left-aligned as the primary CTA. A lightly shaded strip directly underneath summarizes the visitor’s current route, with concise text starting at the left and a 'View Route' link at the far right.
