Below this narrative section, a compact horizontal bar of filters stretches across the card: filter controls cluster on the left, while a sort dropdown or control rests on the right, aligned to the same grid. As the user scrolls, the lower portion of the card is dominated by a grid of exhibit cards—each showing a small bordered image on top with a caption area beneath—arranged in three columns on desktop. The cards stack into multiple rows with minimal gaps, echoing the mockup’s photo grid density. At the very bottom of the card, pagination controls form a low-profile row of page pills and arrows, centered or left-aligned within the content width, signaling the end of the content stream. Throughout, loaders appear in place of filters or exhibits when needed, and invisible handlers handle interactions, keeping the experience visually consistent and calm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The exhibitions-page-controller manifests as the main white content card centered beneath the global museum header. It spans approximately 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop, with equal light grey margins on the left and right, and is vertically offset from the header by about 48px (6 base units). The card has 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. From top to bottom inside this controller: the page title sits at the top left, full-width; immediately below are the introductory paragraphs; directly under those, the explore-exhibits-cta is left-aligned; the route-summary-inline occupies a thin full-width band beneath the CTA; the exhibit-filter-bar and exhibit-sort-control share a horizontal row below the summary; beneath that, the exhibit-list fills the majority of the remaining vertical space; and finally, the pagination-controls are anchored at the bottom of the card with extra padding before the card edge. The entire card scrolls as a single column, maintaining a strong vertical reading path.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered container within the main page flow; it scrolls with the page and defines the fixed-width grid for all child components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The explore-exhibits-cta is a prominent red button positioned inside the exhibitions-page-controller, directly beneath the introductory descriptive text. It is left-aligned with the page title and body text, sitting about 16–24px below the last paragraph line. The button itself is modest in size—roughly as wide as its label plus padding—using 6–8px vertical and 12–16px horizontal internal padding, creating a compact but noticeable control. There is 24px of vertical space between the bottom of the button and the top edge of the route-summary-inline, ensuring it feels clearly connected to the intro yet distinct as an action. On smaller screens, the button still sits on its own line, centered within the content column if space is tight.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline-block element within the vertical flow of the header text area; it does not stick or float and simply moves up as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The exhibit-filter-bar appears as a full-width horizontal strip within the exhibitions-page-controller, just below the route summary area. It is aligned to the same left and right insets as the text above, spanning nearly the entire inner width of the card. The bar’s top edge sits about 24–32px below the bottom of the route-summary-inline (or below the explore-exhibits-cta if there is no route summary visible). Filter controls (such as dropdowns, pills, or checkboxes) begin at the left edge and extend horizontally, occupying roughly the left 60–70% of the bar on desktop. The controls are arranged inline with 8–12px gaps between them, and the total height of the bar is compact—about 48–64px—echoing the moderate density and flat styling of the mockup. On narrow screens, these controls can wrap to a second line but maintain the same left alignment, preserving the editorial column feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static bar within the content card that scrolls normally with the page. It sits directly above the exhibit list and is visually grouped with the sort control as a single control band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The exhibit-sort-control sits on the same horizontal band as the exhibit-filter-bar, anchored to the right side of the content width inside the exhibitions-page-controller. On desktop, it occupies roughly the rightmost 20–30% of the row and is aligned so that its right edge matches the card’s internal right padding. Vertically, it is centered within the height of the filter bar, with about 8–12px breathing room above and below. This creates a clear left-right tension: filters on the left, sorting on the right, similar to classic list controls. On smaller screens where horizontal space is limited, the sort control drops to a second line directly beneath the filters, right-aligned with the card content, separated by 8–12px vertical spacing to keep the band visually unified but readable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control that shares its row with the filter bar; it reflows under the filters on smaller widths but always remains adjacent to them in the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The exhibit-list fills the lower portion of the exhibitions-page-controller, beginning roughly 24–32px below the filter/sort band. It spans the full inner width of the card (minus padding) and is organized into a multi-row grid of exhibit-list-item cards. On typical desktop widths, items are arranged in three columns, each card occupying about 30–32% of the inner width, with 16px gutters between columns and 24px vertical spacing between rows. On wider screens, this can expand to four columns that align with the underlying 12-column grid pattern suggested by the mockup. The grid extends downward as the main scrollable content, echoing the mockup’s dense row of image cards but expanded into several rows. The list stops approximately 32px above the pagination-controls, leaving a clear separation that marks the end of the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid within the content card that grows vertically as more items are shown; it scrolls normally and reflows responsively from 3–4 columns on desktop down to 1–2 columns on narrow screens while maintaining centered alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each exhibit-list-item is a vertical card within the exhibit-list grid, mirroring the mockup’s image-with-caption structure. The card occupies one grid column and has a thin, light grey border with a subtle shadow or none, keeping the flat, editorial feel. At the top of the card is a rectangular thumbnail image (approximate 4:3 aspect ratio) spanning the full card width, with a small 8px margin above the border or directly touching it. Below the image, within 16px of internal padding, the exhibit title appears in a slightly larger serif type, followed by a short 2–3 line description in body text. Underneath, smaller meta information (duration, era, room) may appear in a single line or two, and the 'Add to route' interactive element (tied to the add-to-route-handler) is placed at the bottom-right within the card content area. There is about 16–24px vertical spacing between the bottom of one card and the card below in the next row, and 16px horizontal gutters between adjacent cards in the same row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static tiles inside the grid; they expand vertically to accommodate variable text length but maintain consistent width, image aspect ratio, and alignment with neighboring cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls form a low-profile control row at the bottom of the exhibitions-page-controller, centered horizontally within the card or slightly left-aligned to match the text column, depending on the overall site convention. They sit about 24–32px below the last row of exhibit-list items, with additional 24–32px padding between the controls and the bottom edge of the card, establishing a clear end-of-content zone. The controls themselves appear as a series of small numbered pills and previous/next arrows styled similarly to the language selection tags from the mockup: compact, with 4–6px vertical and 8–12px horizontal padding, separated by 8px gaps. The line height is modest, matching the conservative editorial style and not competing visually with the exhibit cards above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the bottom of the content card; it scrolls into view after the exhibit-list and does not remain fixed when the user scrolls back up.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The route-summary-inline appears as a subtle, full-width band within the exhibitions-page-controller, directly beneath the explore-exhibits-cta. It spans the same inner width as the paragraph text and is offset from the button by about 16–24px. The band uses a very light background tint (e.g., a light grey or cream) to differentiate it from the white card while remaining understated. Inside, a single line or two of text starts at the left, summarizing the current route (e.g., number of exhibits, estimated duration). The height of the band is about 40–56px depending on content. The view-route-cta is positioned on the same horizontal line, right-aligned within this band, with about 16px padding from the right edge. There is about 24px vertical space between the bottom of this band and the top of the exhibit-filter-bar, visually grouping it with the introductory text while still clearly preceding the filtering area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline section in the vertical flow. It appears or disappears based on whether a route exists but does not change the behavior of surrounding elements beyond shifting them up or down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view-route-cta is a text-based navigation link placed inside the route-summary-inline band, aligned to the far right. It sits on the same baseline as the route summary text or slightly above if the text wraps, maintaining a visual linkage between summary and action. The link uses a branded color (e.g., a deep red or green accent) and may be underlined or styled as a discreet textual button. It has about 8–12px of horizontal padding around the text for touch targets on smaller screens, but remains visually light so that it doesn’t overpower the summary. There is minimal vertical padding above and below, matching the band’s internal spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline link anchored to the right within the route-summary-inline; it scrolls with the page and has no fixed or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filters-and-sorting-loader occupies the exact visual footprint of the exhibit-filter-bar and exhibit-sort-control whenever filter or sort data is being fetched. Instead of the usual controls, a set of horizontal placeholder bars or a subtle loading shimmer spans from the left padding to the right padding of the card at the same vertical position. The height of this loading area matches the combined filter/sort band (roughly 48–64px). There is no extra spacing added; it maintains the same 24–32px gap above (from the route-summary-inline) and below (before the exhibit-list) as when the filter and sort controls are visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Transient overlay state within the filter/sort region; it replaces those controls while loading but remains static within the card’s vertical flow, disappearing once data is ready.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The exhibits-data-loader appears in the space normally occupied by the exhibit-list when exhibit data is being fetched. It is centered horizontally within the exhibitions-page-controller and begins at the same vertical position where the first row of exhibit-list-item cards would appear—about 24–32px below the filter/sort band. Visually, it may be a skeleton grid echoing the layout of 3–4 exhibit cards per row, or a single centered loading indicator, but in all cases it stays within the card’s inner width and preserves the same top and bottom margins. The pagination-controls remain below this area and may be hidden or disabled while loading, but their spatial position (at the bottom) is not altered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Transient state occupying the exhibit-list’s space; it scrolls normally as part of the page and disappears seamlessly when real exhibit cards are rendered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-route-handler has no direct visual representation; instead, it is attached to the 'Add to route' control inside each exhibit-list-item. That control typically appears at the bottom-right of the card’s caption area, aligned with other meta information and spaced about 8–12px above the card’s bottom padding. The handler dictates interaction behavior but does not change the card’s spatial dimensions; the button or link itself fits naturally within the existing content density of the card, using similar padding and type scale as other minor actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, event-driven behavior with no spatial footprint beyond the interactive element it powers; it moves only as the exhibit-list-item cards themselves scroll within the content card.

name: Exhibit Detail
title: Exhibit Detail
description: Displays full information for a single cheese history exhibit and lets the visitor add it to their self-guided route, with navigation to related exhibits, the exhibitions list, and the My Route page.

core_features: Load and display a single exhibit using the URL parameter `id` via `getExhibitDetail(exhibit_id)`.
Show key exhibit metadata: title, region label, country, era display, and narrative content (summary and description).
Display exhibit media using the `images` array from the exhibit data.
Present practical visit information: gallery location display and estimated visit time in minutes.
Allow the visitor to add this exhibit to their self-guided route using `addExhibitToRoute(exhibit_id)`.
After adding to the route, display a concise summary of the updated route using the returned `route`, `stops`, and total estimated time fields.
Provide a navigational link back to the exhibitions list (`exhibitions.html`).
Offer a shortcut link to the My Route page (`my_route.html`) after adding the exhibit.
Display a list of related exhibits from `related_exhibits`, each linking to its own Exhibit Detail page.
user_workflows: View an exhibit from the exhibitions list: (1) User clicks an exhibit card on `exhibitions.html`, which links to `exhibit_detail.html?id={exhibit_id}`. (2) On page load, the Exhibit Detail page extracts `id` from the URL and calls `getExhibitDetail` with that value. (3) While loading, the page shows a loading state. (4) When data is returned, the page populates the exhibit header, images, description, practical info, and related exhibits list.

Add the current exhibit to My Route: (1) After viewing the exhibit, the user clicks the "Add to My Route" action. (2) The page calls `addExhibitToRoute` with the current exhibit’s `id`. (3) While the request is in progress, the button is disabled and an in-progress state is shown. (4) On success, the page updates a route summary panel using the returned `route`, `stops`, and total duration fields, and confirms that this exhibit is now in the route. (5) On error, the page shows a non-blocking error message and re-enables the button.

Navigate to My Route after adding an exhibit: (1) Once `addExhibitToRoute` returns successfully, the route summary panel exposes a "View My Route" control. (2) The user activates this control, which navigates to `my_route.html` (no additional URL parameters required).

Return to the exhibitions list: (1) At any time, the user can click a "Back to Exhibitions" link or breadcrumb in the navigation component. (2) The page navigates to `exhibitions.html` using a standard link. (3) Any filtering on the exhibitions page is handled by that page’s own logic when the user arrives.

Navigate to a related exhibit: (1) In the related exhibits list, the user selects another exhibit. (2) The link opens `exhibit_detail.html?id={selected_exhibit_id}`. (3) The Exhibit Detail page re-runs the load workflow, calling `getExhibitDetail` for the new `id` and updating all sections accordingly.
interactions: On page load, parse the `id` query parameter from the URL and invoke `getExhibitDetail` with that value.
If `id` is missing or empty on page load, show a user-facing error state indicating the exhibit cannot be loaded and do not call `getExhibitDetail`.
If `getExhibitDetail` returns successfully, populate exhibit metadata, narrative content, images, practical info, and related exhibits in their respective components.
If `getExhibitDetail` fails (e.g., network or not found), display an error state with a suggestion to go back to the exhibitions list.
When the user clicks the "Add to My Route" button, call `addExhibitToRoute` with the current exhibit `id` and show a loading state on the button.

If `addExhibitToRoute` returns `success: true`, update the route summary panel with the returned `route`, `stops`, and total duration fields, and mark the current exhibit as added (e.g., button state updated).
If `addExhibitToRoute` returns `success: false` or errors, show an inline error message and re-enable the "Add to My Route" button without changing the route summary.
When the user clicks "View My Route" in the route summary panel, navigate to `my_route.html` via a standard link or programmatic navigation.
When the user clicks "Back to Exhibitions", navigate to `exhibitions.html` via a standard link.
When the user clicks a related exhibit item, navigate to that exhibit’s detail page using its `id` in the URL (`exhibit_detail.html?id={related_exhibit_id}`).
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is derived from the URL query parameter `id`. On every page load or URL change, the page reads `id` from `exhibit_detail.html?id={exhibit_id}`. If `id` is present and non-empty, the page calls `getExhibitDetail(exhibit_id=id)` and enters a loading state until data is returned or an error occurs. If `id` is missing or invalid, the page skips the API call and enters an error state indicating that no exhibit can be displayed. The exhibit data returned from `getExhibitDetail` becomes the in-memory state for all detail components (header, description, images, practical info, related exhibits). The "Add to My Route" feature does not change the URL; it manipulates only in-memory UI state. When `addExhibitToRoute` is called with the current `id`, the page tracks a temporary 'adding' state for the button. On success, the route summary area is updated with the returned `route` and `stops`, and flags that this exhibit is now included in the route. This flag is kept in memory for the current page instance; if the user reloads the page, the server truth would need to be rechecked by other pages as this page has no dedicated interface to fetch the route. Navigation to `exhibitions.html` and `my_route.html` uses simple links without extra URL parameters; any additional state on those pages is handled by their own URL parameters and logic.

components.functionality: Reads the `id` URL parameter, calls `getExhibitDetail` with that exhibit ID, manages loading and error states, and distributes the resulting data object to child components.
Provides basic navigation controls for the page: back to the exhibitions list and quick access to the My Route page.
Displays the primary exhibit header information such as title, country, region label, and era display.
Shows the exhibit’s images in a gallery format, if any are provided. Handles image selection and basic media interaction.
Presents the narrative content of the exhibit, including summary and full description where available.
Displays practical visit information for the exhibit, including gallery location and estimated visit time as returned by `getExhibitDetail`.
Enables the user to add the current exhibit to their self-guided route using `addExhibitToRoute` and shows a concise summary of the updated route on success.
Displays a short summary of the self-guided route after the exhibit is added, using the route and stops returned by `addExhibitToRoute`.
Shows related exhibits based on the `related_exhibits` array from `getExhibitDetail`, each item linking to its own Exhibit Detail page by setting `id` in the URL.
Displays user-friendly error messages when the exhibit cannot be loaded (missing `id` or `getExhibitDetail` failure) and offers a link back to the exhibitions list.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width grid similar to a 12-column system with a main content column and an internal 4-column image grid. Each image/caption acts like one column of four within the content width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow with a fixed global header at the very top and a single, centered main content card below it. Inside the card, content is arranged as one main vertical stack: exhibit header/title at the top, followed by the descriptive text, then a horizontal image gallery band (4-column grid on large screens), then practical information, followed by the add-to-route action panel, the inline route summary, and finally a related exhibits band. Error and loading states take over the main card area rather than appearing as separate layers elsewhere.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow with a fixed global header at the very top and a single, centered main content card below it. Inside the card, content is arranged as one main vertical stack: exhibit header/title at the top, followed by the descriptive text, then a horizontal image gallery band (4-column grid on large screens), then practical information, followed by the add-to-route action panel, the inline route summary, and finally a related exhibits band. Error and loading states take over the main card area rather than appearing as separate layers elsewhere.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global navigation/header spans the full viewport width and occupies about 15–20% of the initial viewport height. Below it, the main content card is centered and constrained to roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, framed by light grey side margins. Within the card, the top ~40% of the above-the-fold area is primarily the exhibit header and descriptive text, while the image gallery row visually dominates the next ~40–60%. The practical info, route panels, and related exhibits occupy the lower portions of the card in distinct but moderately spaced sections, each separated vertically by 24–32px. The card itself uses internal padding of approximately 24–32px on all sides.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate and text-first. Paragraphs use a readable line length (around 65–80 characters per line) with comfortable line spacing, and section spacing is based on 24–32px vertical gaps. The image gallery and related exhibits grids are denser, with relatively tight 8–12px gutters between images/cards to maximize the number of items in a row while still preserving thin grey borders and legible captions. Buttons and interactive pills use compact padding (about 4–8px vertically and 8–12px horizontally) to feel efficient rather than oversized.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow begins at the strong red brand/navigation bar at the top, then moves left-to-right across the logo and utility links (language, search, route access). From there, the eye drops down to the large, red-styled exhibit title at the top of the content card, then naturally flows into the descriptive text where highlighted links and occasional inline icons punctuate the copy. Below the text, the horizontal image gallery band functions as a visual anchor. After the gallery, attention moves down through the practical info (key details), then to the add-to-route call-to-action panel and the route summary inline block. Finally, the gaze lands on the related exhibits grid at the bottom before exiting the page or scrolling back up via the site’s scroll-to-top control.

layout.overall_description: The Exhibit Detail page is framed by a full-width, red-accented museum header at the top and a single, centered white content card below, sitting on a light grey background. The header anchors the brand and utilities; beneath it, the main card appears as a solid, rectangular panel aligned to the center of the viewport, with generous 24–32px padding inside and around it. At the top of this card, the exhibit’s name is presented in a traditional serif typeface in red, spanning most of the card’s width, with smaller metadata aligned beneath. Immediately below, a block of descriptive text runs full-width within the card, with comfortable line length and vertical rhythm, including subtle green links and small inline icons where needed. As you scroll, this narrative leads directly into a horizontal image gallery: a row of equally sized image cards arranged as a four-column grid on desktop, each with a thin grey border and a caption below, forming a dense but orderly visual strip across the width of the content card. Under the gallery, the layout returns to single-column content: a clearly labelled practical info section, then a horizontally arranged add-to-route panel with a brand-red button, followed by a quieter, bordered inline route summary. At the very bottom of the card, a related exhibits section mirrors the visual language of the gallery with a small grid of related exhibit thumbnails and captions. Everything scrolls as one continuous vertical story, with consistent spacing and conservative styling that prioritizes readability and trust over visual spectacle.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-loader occupies the exact space reserved for the main content card, directly below the global navigation bar. When active, it renders a centered white panel with the same width as the content card (approximately 70–80% of the viewport width, capped at a fixed maximum) and the same 24–32px internal padding. Within this panel, a loading indicator (spinner or skeleton rows) is vertically and horizontally centered in the available space, with faint placeholder lines mimicking the future positions of the exhibit title, text blocks, and image gallery row. The surrounding page chrome (red header at the top, light grey background at the sides) remains visible, but all exhibit-specific content within the card area is replaced by this loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlays or replaces the main content card region during data loading; positioned statically in the normal document flow below the header, scrolling with the page when content height exceeds viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation bar sits at the very top of the page, spanning the full viewport width. It forms a horizontal red band that is approximately 15–20% of the initial viewport height. Within this band, content is centered horizontally to align with the main card below: a fixed-width inner container matches the card’s width (around 70–80% of the viewport). The museum logo and site title are aligned to the left within this inner container, while utility elements such as language selectors, a compact search input/icon, and a link to the visitor’s route are aligned to the right in a single horizontal row. Vertical padding of roughly 16–24px above and below the nav items ensures the bar feels substantial but not bulky. There is no sidebar; the nav bar is a single clean strip, and the main content card begins directly beneath it with a slightly larger vertical gap (about 48–64px) to distinguish global chrome from page content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed at the top of the page in the visual hierarchy but scrolls away with content if the page is long; it is not sticky. Provides a stable top anchor for all other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The exhibit header section is the first content block inside the main white card, positioned directly below the navigation bar with a top outer margin of about 48–64px and card padding of 24–32px. It spans the full internal width of the card. The exhibit title appears as a single, left-aligned line (or two lines if necessary) in a large serif font, styled in the brand red, occupying roughly the top quarter of the card’s visible area on first load. Immediately beneath the title, there is a smaller metadata row or short subtitle—such as the exhibit’s era, origin, or room location—set in a muted grey or black serif or simple sans-serif, still left-aligned. Vertical spacing between the title and metadata is modest (around 8–16px) to keep them visually grouped. The entire header block occupies roughly the top 20–25% of the content card’s initial height, clearly establishing the exhibit’s identity before any supporting text or images.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content card, forming the anchor at the top of the scrollable exhibit content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The images gallery is placed below the primary descriptive text section, forming a horizontal band that visually spans the full internal width of the main content card. A vertical gap of about 24–32px separates it from the preceding text. On large screens, the gallery follows a 4-column grid: four equally sized image cards appear in a single row, each occupying approximately one quarter of the card’s width, with 8–12px gutters between them. Each image card consists of a photo with a thin light-grey border and a subtle drop shadow or delineating line, with the caption directly beneath the image, left-aligned and taking the same width as the image. Captions are set in small, readable type and limited to 1–2 lines where possible to maintain a tidy row height. On medium screens, this grid gracefully collapses to two images per row; on small screens, it collapses to a single column, but the row still reads as a coherent gallery band. The overall gallery block typically occupies around 40–60% of the card’s height once in view, acting as a strong visual anchor in the mid-section of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the main vertical scroll. The grid is responsive, adjusting column count based on viewport width while remaining centered within the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The description section sits directly beneath the exhibit header section and above the images gallery, occupying the full internal width of the content card. A vertical gap of approximately 16–24px separates it from the header. This section contains one or more paragraphs of body text set in a traditional serif typeface, left-aligned and running in a single column with a comfortable line length (65–80 characters). Vertical spacing between paragraphs follows the 16–24px scale, keeping the narrative continuous but airy. Inline emphasis is provided by colored links—typically a muted green—and occasional small inline icons (e.g., for audio guide availability) that sit on the text baseline and break up the copy subtly. The description block takes up most of the top half of the main content card above the gallery, roughly 20–30% of the overall page height before scrolling further, reinforcing a content-first reading experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolling with the page as an integral part of the main content card’s vertical narrative.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The practical info panel is placed below the images gallery, separated from it by a clear vertical gap of about 24–32px. It spans the full internal width of the main card but is visually grouped as a distinct subsection. A subtle horizontal rule or a slightly darker top border may mark its beginning. Inside, a small heading such as “Practical Information” appears left-aligned at the top of the panel, followed by a series of concise info rows: opening times, location within the museum, accessibility notes, language availability, etc. On larger screens, these items may be arranged in two balanced sub-columns within the panel, but each line remains left-aligned and text-heavy, retaining the editorial feel. On smaller screens, the panel collapses into a single vertical list. Vertical spacing between lines is tight but readable (around 8–16px), making this section feel information-dense yet orderly, occupying roughly 15–20% of the card’s lower half.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the main vertical stack, with no sticky or floating behavior. Responsively reflows between one and two internal columns while staying within the primary content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-route panel is positioned immediately beneath the practical info panel, separated by about 24px of vertical space. It spans the full internal width of the card but reads as a compact horizontal band. Within this band, explanatory text appears on the left—such as a short sentence explaining that the visitor can add this exhibit to their self-guided route—while the primary action button sits to the right, aligned either with the text’s baseline or vertically centered within the band. The button uses the brand red fill color with white text and has modest padding (approximately 8px vertically and 16px horizontally), giving it prominence without overwhelming the editorial feel. The panel may be enclosed with a thin border or a slightly tinted background to distinguish the action area from the surrounding informational content. Overall, this band is relatively low in height, likely taking up only 10–15% of the card’s visible area at the moment it comes into view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow, appearing as a fixed-positioned band relative to surrounding sections (it does not float or stick). Its layout remains a left text block and right-aligned button on larger screens, collapsing to stacked text-above-button on narrow viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The route summary inline panel appears directly under the add-to-route panel, separated by a modest 16–24px gap. It occupies the full internal width of the card but is visually quieter to avoid competing with the main call to action. The panel is rendered as a thin, bordered or lightly shaded box with 16–24px of internal padding and sits flush left within the main content column. Inside, a compact heading (e.g., “Your Route”) aligns to the left at the top, with one or two lines summarizing the current route (number of exhibits, estimated duration) below it, and a subtle text link such as “View My Route” right-aligned on the same row or at the bottom. The typography is slightly smaller than the main body text to signal secondary importance. The panel’s height is relatively small, occupying only a narrow band near the lower third of the card, and it visually ties this exhibit back to the broader route planning experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the main vertical document flow. On smaller screens, interior elements stack vertically while the panel maintains full-width alignment within the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related exhibits list is placed near the bottom of the main content card, forming the final major section in the vertical sequence. A larger vertical gap of about 32px separates it from the route summary inline panel to emphasize it as a new section. It spans the full internal width of the card. At the top of this section, a left-aligned heading such as “Related Exhibits” appears in a slightly larger serif font. Below the heading, a compact grid of related exhibit cards is displayed, visually echoing the image gallery above but typically with smaller thumbnails and more emphasis on exhibit titles. On larger screens, three to four related exhibit cards appear in a row, each taking up roughly one quarter or one third of the card width, with 8–12px gutters between them. Each card features a small image on top and the exhibit title and perhaps a short label beneath, all enclosed in a thin bordered rectangle or a clean, card-like block. On medium and small screens, this grid collapses to two and then one column respectively. This section provides a visually cohesive ending to the page, using a denser grid layout that still respects the conservative, content-first attitude of the design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, forming the terminating section of the main card’s scrollable content. The grid layout is responsive, adjusting the number of columns based on viewport width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state replaces the main content card when the exhibit cannot be loaded. It appears in the same position as the card, directly below the navigation bar, and occupies a centered white panel of similar width (around 60–70% of the viewport) with 24–32px padding. Inside, an error icon or subtle graphic appears near the top center, followed by a concise error title and message, both center-aligned or gently left-aligned depending on overall site patterns. A link or secondary button to return to the exhibits list or retry is placed beneath the message with around 16–24px vertical separation. The layout remains calm and text-focused, with generous whitespace above and below the message to emphasize clarity and reduce frustration. Everything outside this panel—the red header and the light grey background—remains unchanged, but no other exhibit-specific sections (description, images, route actions) are visible when this panel is active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the normal main content card in the document flow. Static, centered below the header, and scrolls with the page if content height exceeds the viewport.

name: Self-Guided Tour / My Route
title: Self-Guided Tour
description: Manage your personalized self-guided tour by viewing selected exhibits, configuring a start time and duration preferences, and adjusting the order or contents of your route.

core_features: Load and display the current self-guided route with all ordered stops, including exhibit details and any calculated schedule using getRouteOverview.
Show aggregated route metrics such as total estimated visit time, walking time, and overall duration using data from getRouteOverview and updateRouteSettingsAndSchedule.
Allow users to set or adjust a planned route start time and optional maximum total duration, and request an optimized schedule using updateRouteSettingsAndSchedule.
Allow users to reorder stops in the route (e.g., via drag-and-drop or move controls) and persist the new order using reorderRouteStops.
Allow users to remove individual exhibits from the route and refresh the route overview using removeRouteStop and getRouteOverview.
Provide direct navigation from each route stop to the corresponding exhibit detail page (exhibit_detail.html?id=EXHIBIT_ID).
Provide a clear call-to-action to add more exhibits to the route, navigating to exhibitions.html with an appropriate region parameter.
user_workflows:
View my current self-guided route and schedule:
1. User navigates to my_route.html (via header link or a direct link).
2. On page load, the route-overview-loader component calls getRouteOverview with no parameters.
3. The response is bound to the route-summary-panel (for totals and route settings) and route-stops-list (for the ordered list of stops and any scheduled start/end times).
4. The user reviews the list of exhibits, their countries, estimated visit times, and the calculated schedule if a start time exists.
5. If needed, the user clicks any exhibit title to navigate to exhibit_detail.html?id=EXHIBIT_ID.

Configure start time and optimize my route duration:
1. The user opens my_route.html, which loads the current route via getRouteOverview.
2. In the route-settings-form, the user enters or edits a start_time value (HH:MM), optionally checks a control to enable optimize_for_duration, and enters their desired max_total_duration_minutes.
3. The user submits the form; the form handler calls updateRouteSettingsAndSchedule(start_time, optimize_for_duration, max_total_duration_minutes).
4. The response updates the SelfGuidedRoute state and replaces the currently displayed stops and schedule in route-stops-list and route-summary-panel.
5. The URL query parameters (start_time, optimize, max_duration) are updated to reflect the new settings so that reloading or sharing the URL preserves these preferences.

Reorder or remove exhibits to fine-tune the route:
1. The user opens my_route.html and the current route loads via getRouteOverview.
2. To change the order, the user drags a stop (or uses move-up/down buttons) in the route-stops-list; the route-reorder-controller determines a new ordered list of RouteStop IDs.
3. The controller calls reorderRouteStops(ordered_route_stop_ids) to persist the new order.
4. After a successful response, the page calls getRouteOverview again to obtain a refreshed schedule and updated total durations, which are bound to route-summary-panel and route-stops-list.
5. To remove a stop, the user clicks a remove action on a specific stop; the route-remove-stop-handler calls removeRouteStop(route_stop_id).
6. After a successful removal, the page calls getRouteOverview to refresh the list, totals, and any schedule.
7. If the user wants to add more exhibits, they click the Add more exhibits call-to-action, which navigates to exhibitions.html?region=europe.
interactions: Page load: triggers getRouteOverview to populate the route summary and the list of stops.
Changing start time input in route-settings-form: updates local form state; schedule is recalculated only after form submission via updateRouteSettingsAndSchedule.
Toggling optimize_for_duration (e.g., checkbox or switch): updates local form state; after submitting, updateRouteSettingsAndSchedule is called with the new flag.
Editing max_total_duration_minutes (e.g., numeric input): updates local form state; after submitting, updateRouteSettingsAndSchedule is called with the new value.
Submitting route-settings-form: calls updateRouteSettingsAndSchedule with current form values, updates UI with returned route, stops, and schedule, and synchronizes URL query parameters.

Dragging a stop to a new position or clicking a move control: triggers route-reorder-controller to construct ordered_route_stop_ids and call reorderRouteStops; then triggers getRouteOverview to refresh schedule.
Clicking Remove on a route stop: triggers a confirmation (handled in UI), and if confirmed, calls removeRouteStop(route_stop_id); then triggers getRouteOverview to refresh the list and totals.
Clicking an exhibit title in the route-stops-list: navigates to exhibit_detail.html?id=EXHIBIT_ID derived from the RouteStop.exhibit_id.
Clicking Add more exhibits: navigates to exhibitions.html?region=europe.

Reloading or sharing a URL with query parameters for start_time, optimize, and max_duration: on page load, route-overview-loader reads these parameters and uses them to prefill route-settings-form and to determine whether to immediately call updateRouteSettingsAndSchedule after the initial getRouteOverview, ensuring the displayed schedule matches the URL state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is managed through a combination of API responses and URL query parameters, without using localStorage. The base URL is my_route.html. Optional query parameters include: route_id (identifier for a specific SelfGuidedRoute when supported), start_time (HH:MM string), optimize (boolean-like flag, such as '1' or '0' / 'true' or 'false'), and max_duration (numeric value representing max_total_duration_minutes). On initial load, route-overview-loader reads these query parameters. It calls getRouteOverview to load the current SelfGuidedRoute and RouteStop list. If the query includes start_time, optimize, or max_duration and they differ from the route settings returned by getRouteOverview, the page may immediately call updateRouteSettingsAndSchedule using the values from the URL to align the backend route configuration with the URL state, then update the UI with the returned schedule. After any user-initiated change to route settings (start time, optimization flag, or max duration) that results in a successful updateRouteSettingsAndSchedule response, the page rewrites or replaces the current URL to include updated query parameters (start_time, optimize, max_duration) so that browser refresh or sharing the link reconstructs the same configuration. After any reorder or removal of stops, the page calls getRouteOverview to obtain the new state; the route_id in the URL remains the same, while other query parameters remain unchanged unless the route settings from the server differ, in which case the page updates the URL to match the server's canonical values.

components.functionality: Initializes the Self-Guided Route page by reading URL parameters, calling getRouteOverview, and distributing the returned route and stops data to dependent components. Optionally triggers an immediate updateRouteSettingsAndSchedule if URL parameters for start_time, optimize, or max_duration differ from the route settings returned by getRouteOverview.

Displays high-level information about the current self-guided route, including route name (if present), number of stops, total estimated visit time, walking time, overall duration, and whether optimization for duration is enabled. May also show non-marketing informational messages, such as when the total estimated duration exceeds the configured maximum duration.

Allows the user to configure and submit route-level settings: planned start time, whether to optimize for duration, and the maximum total duration in minutes. On submit, calls updateRouteSettingsAndSchedule and updates the page based on the response while also updating URL parameters.

Renders the ordered list of route stops with their associated exhibits and any scheduled times. Each list item shows exhibit details (such as title, country, estimated visit time), the route order, and if available, scheduled_start_time and scheduled_end_time. It also exposes controls for reordering and removing stops and navigation to exhibit detail pages.

Encapsulates per-stop interactive controls within each route stop: navigation to exhibit detail via the exhibit title click, trigger for removal of the stop, and trigger for reordering (drag handle or move up/down controls). Delegates API calls to the route-reorder-controller and route-remove-stop-handler.

Manages reordering of route stops based on drag-and-drop or move-up/down actions. Builds a new ordered_route_stop_ids array and calls reorderRouteStops. After a successful response, it requests the updated overview via getRouteOverview to refresh schedule and totals.
data_binding: RouteStop[] (current order and IDs from getRouteOverview)

components.functionality: Handles removal of a specific stop from the route. When triggered, optionally confirms with the user, calls removeRouteStop(route_stop_id), and upon success calls getRouteOverview to refresh route data and schedule.

Shows functional warnings when the total_estimated_duration_minutes exceeds the configured max_total_duration_minutes while optimize_for_duration is enabled, or when no start_time is set but the user has expressed a desire to see a schedule (determined by route-settings-form state). It does not perform API calls but responds to route and settings changes.
Provides a clear action to add more exhibits to the route by linking to the exhibitions listing page, pre-filtered to the European region via URL parameters.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width grid similar to a 12-column system with a main content column and an internal 4-column image grid. Each image/caption acts like one column of four within the content width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom editorial flow inside a single centered white content card. Above the card, the global museum header bar remains unchanged (logo on the left, utilities on the right). Inside the content card, the structure becomes: (1) page title and a short explanatory intro paragraph at the very top, (2) route summary panel just below the intro, (3) route settings form directly under the summary, (4) a duration warning banner sitting immediately beneath the settings when relevant, (5) a visually denser section for the route stops list, using a grid-like arrangement reminiscent of the 4-column image band from the mockup, and (6) an add-more-exhibits call-to-action anchored at the bottom of the card beneath the list.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom editorial flow inside a single centered white content card. Above the card, the global museum header bar remains unchanged (logo on the left, utilities on the right). Inside the content card, the structure becomes: (1) page title and a short explanatory intro paragraph at the very top, (2) route summary panel just below the intro, (3) route settings form directly under the summary, (4) a duration warning banner sitting immediately beneath the settings when relevant, (5) a visually denser section for the route stops list, using a grid-like arrangement reminiscent of the 4-column image band from the mockup, and (6) an add-more-exhibits call-to-action anchored at the bottom of the card beneath the list.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header occupies the top 15–20% of the viewport, with the main content card centered below using roughly 70–80% of the viewport width. Within the card, the top 40% of the card height is dedicated to textual and control information (title, intro, summary, settings, warning). The lower ~60% is dominated by the route stops list area; this section appears more content-dense and visually similar to the mockup’s image row. Horizontal space within the stops area behaves like a 4-column grid for larger screens (each stop occupying approximately one quarter of the content width) and collapses responsively into fewer columns on narrower screens, while all other components remain full-width rows.
layout_strategies.content_density: Text sections (title, intro, summary, settings) use a comfortable, moderate density: readable line lengths, generous line-height, and vertical spacing of about 24–32px between sections. Interactive controls (form fields, buttons, item actions) use slightly tighter vertical spacing but never feel cramped. The stops list adopts the densest layout, with minimal 8–12px gutters between stop cards and concise captions, echoing the compact photo strip from the mockup while still preserving clear separation via subtle borders and padding.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual attention starts at the red brand bar and logo in the global header, then moves rightward across language/search utilities. It drops down to the large red page title and follows into a short explanatory paragraph. From there, the eye moves naturally to the route summary panel as a key status snapshot, then into the route settings form. Any duration warning banner sitting just below the settings becomes the next focal point due to its tinted background. Finally, the eye settles on the denser block of route stops laid out as a horizontal grid band, and ends at the add-more-exhibits call-to-action link at the bottom. Any loading overlay temporarily takes over the center of the card but respects the overall alignment and framing of the layout.

layout.overall_description: The page sits within a conservative, centered layout: a light grey background frames a fixed-width white content card aligned to the vertical center line of the viewport. At the top of the viewport, the familiar red museum header bar with the logo on the left and language/search utilities on the right occupies roughly one-fifth of the screen height. Below it, with a 32px gap, a white card stretches across about three-quarters of the page width. Inside this card, a bold red serif page title and a brief, calm explanatory paragraph establish an editorial tone. Just beneath, a compact yet prominent route summary panel shows key facts about the self-guided tour. The route settings form follows in a clean, stacked layout of labeled fields and subtle buttons. If the planned duration is problematic, a tinted warning band spans the full width under the form. The lower half of the card is visually denser: a grid of route stops, each in a bordered tile, flows from left to right in up to four columns, mimicking a strip of historical photo cards. Reorder and action controls sit neatly within each stop’s tile but never overpower the content. At the very bottom, a simple red-accented text link invites visitors to add more exhibits to their route. Throughout, the spacing remains consistent and based on an 8px scale, giving the page an organized, museum-catalog feel. A scroll-to-top affordance (outside this spec) can remain as a subtle, peripheral element on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a semi-opaque overlay confined to the main white content card, not the full viewport. It covers the area from just below the page title and intro paragraph down to the bottom of the card, dimming the summary, settings, and stops list beneath it. At the center of the card (both horizontally and vertically within the card’s interior), a small loading indicator and short status text are displayed, aligned to the same central axis as the content column. The overlay respects the card’s rounded or squared edges, leaving the outer light grey page background and the global header visible, reinforcing the document-like feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as an absolute overlay within the content card; centered loading indicator; appears and disappears dynamically based on data state, blocking interaction with underlying components while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the main white content card, the route summary panel sits directly below the intro paragraph with about 24–32px vertical separation. It spans the full content width, aligned left with the page title’s text baseline, leaving equal left and right margins inside the card. Vertically, it has 16–24px of internal padding and is framed either by a subtle border or a slightly tinted background to distinguish it as a compact, informative block. It occupies roughly the upper-middle portion of the card, taking up about 15–20% of the card height before the settings form begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level component within the linear flow; it expands vertically with content but always remains full-width and directly pushes the settings form downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The route settings form is placed immediately beneath the route summary panel with about 24px of vertical spacing, maintaining the same left and right alignment as the summary. It also spans the full content width, but its internal content is arranged in a single-column stack of form fields and labels, avoiding multi-column complexity to preserve the editorial, text-first feel. Each form row (label + input) is separated by 16–24px, and form actions (like save/apply buttons) sit aligned to the left beneath the last field with a 24px gap. Overall, the form occupies roughly another 15–20% of the card height, acting as the final piece of the ‘textual control’ band before the more visual list of stops.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width block component; resizes vertically depending on the number of fields, always maintaining consistent vertical rhythm with the summary above and banner or list below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The route stops list occupies the lower half of the content card and visually parallels the mockup’s horizontal image grid. It sits beneath the route settings form or duration warning banner (if present) with a 32px gap. Across wide viewports, it uses a 4-column grid within the content width: each stop appears as a vertical tile occupying approximately one quarter of the card’s width minus 8–12px gutters between tiles. Each tile has a light grey border, 12–16px internal padding, and a short title/description stacked from top to bottom, with action controls at the bottom or right edge of the tile. On narrower screens, the grid collapses gracefully to 2 columns and then a single column while preserving similar gutters and margins. Vertically, multiple rows of stop tiles stack with 16–24px between rows, creating a compact but readable band of content mimicking a strip of curated exhibits.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid-like block component within the card; responsive behavior adjusts the number of columns based on available width while maintaining consistent left/right card margins and vertical spacing to adjacent sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within each stop tile of the route stops list, the item actions are grouped into a compact control cluster placed near the bottom edge of the tile. They align to the right side of the tile on larger screens, forming a horizontal row of subtle text buttons or icon-labeled actions (e.g., “Remove”, “Details”) with 8–12px spacing between each. On smaller screens, they may wrap into two lines but still remain visually grouped as a single action strip. There is about 8–12px of vertical space separating the main stop content (title/description) from this action cluster, ensuring a clear hierarchy while preserving the denser, card-like layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows within each stop card as an inline block aligned to the card’s right or bottom edge; no independent scrolling or fixed behavior; reflows responsively with the card’s width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reorder controller appears in two coordinated positions: (1) As a subtle global hint or control above the stops grid—aligned left, just inside the top edge of the route-stops-list area, with a short label like “Reorder stops” and possibly a small icon; this sits about 8–12px above the first row of stop tiles and is visually understated so it doesn’t compete with headings. (2) As a drag handle or up/down arrow control within each stop tile, typically aligned along the left edge or just to the left of the stop title. The in-tile handle is compact, occupying a narrow vertical strip (~10–15% of the tile width) or a small icon-sized area, separated by 8px from the main stop text, maintaining the grid’s rectangular structure while making reordering affordances discoverable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Global reorder label/control is a static inline element anchored at the top-left of the stops section; in-tile handles are part of each tile’s internal layout and support drag-and-drop or click-based reordering without changing the overall grid alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove stop handler has no independent visible footprint; its presence is expressed through ‘Remove’ actions within each stop’s action cluster. Visually, this maps to a small, text-based or icon-button label within the route-stop-item-actions group, typically placed at the far right of that cluster. When invoked, any confirmation UI (if present) appears as a lightweight overlay within the specific stop tile or as a small confirmation dialog centered within the content card, keeping the focus tightly scoped to the related content and maintaining the editorial integrity of the overall layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Logically bound to each stop card; triggers local state changes and potential micro-overlays within the card. It does not alter the card’s structural layout except when temporarily displaying a small confirmation micro-panel in place or on top of the stop’s content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The duration warning banner is a full-width horizontal strip inside the content card, positioned immediately below the route settings form with 16–24px of vertical separation. It spans the same width as the summary and form, with a tinted background (e.g., pale yellow or light red) and a thin border to differentiate it from the white card while staying within the conservative visual palette. The text is left-aligned and wraps naturally, with 12–16px internal vertical padding. Any small icon (e.g., warning symbol) appears on the left, followed by the message text. The banner is visually noticeable but not overpowering, occupying about 10–15% of the card height when present and pushing the route-stops-list downward.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level element; appears and disappears conditionally between the settings form and the stops list, without overlaying other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-more-exhibits call-to-action is placed at the very bottom of the content card, directly beneath the last row of the route stops list. A 24–32px vertical gap separates it from the stops grid, giving it clear breathing room as the concluding element. It appears as a simple text link or understated button with red accent color, aligned to the left baseline with the rest of the content rather than centered, maintaining the editorial flow. The surrounding whitespace below (another 24–32px before the end of the card) helps it stand out without heavy visual decoration, echoing the mockup’s simple navigational links.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline/inline-block component anchored at the bottom of the linear content flow; it stays attached to the end of the stops list and moves downward as the list grows.

name: Tours & Workshops
title: Tours & Workshops
description: Browse and filter guided tours and workshops, switch between views, and open individual program detail pages.

core_features: Segmented view toggle between guided tours and workshops, controlled by the `view` URL parameter and tab clicks.
Dynamic loading of filter options (languages, audience types, months, price ranges, tour sort options) using `getProgramFilterOptions`.
Guided tours listing using `listGuidedTours`, filterable by date, language, maximum price, and sortable by duration (and other sort options returned by the API).
Workshops listing using `listWorkshops`, filterable by month, weekend-only flag, audience type, and maximum price.
Program cards for both tours and workshops showing key information from `Program` and associated `ProgramSession` objects, with click navigation to `program_detail.html`.
Pagination for both guided tours and workshops lists, driven by the `page` and `page_size` arguments of `listGuidedTours` and `listWorkshops`.
Cart access via a cart icon that navigates to `cart.html` without modifying cart data on this page.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Landing on page with presets from homepage:
1) User clicks a link from `index.html` that includes some of: `view`, `date`, `language`, `month`, `weekend_only`, `audience_type`, `max_price`.
2) On page load, the controller reads URL parameters and determines the active view (`guided` or `workshops`), defaulting to guided tours if `view` is absent or invalid.
3) The controller calls `getProgramFilterOptions` and populates filter controls (languages, audience types, months, price ranges, tour sort options).
4) If the active view is `guided`, the controller initializes the guided tours filters from `date`, `language`, and `max_price` (if present) and calls `listGuidedTours` with those filters and a default sort option.
5) If the active view is `workshops`, the controller initializes workshop filters from `month`, `weekend_only`, `audience_type`, and `max_price` (if present) and calls `listWorkshops` with those filters.
6) The corresponding list is rendered as cards; the user can scroll and click any card to open `program_detail.html?id={program.id}`.

Workflow 2 – Refining guided tours by filters and sorting:
1) With the guided tours tab active, the user changes one or more filters: date (date picker), language (dropdown from `getProgramFilterOptions.languages`), maximum price (input or range selector), or sort order (dropdown from `getProgramFilterOptions.sort_options_tours`).
2) The guided filter form emits an `onFilterChange` event with the updated filter state and selected sort option.
3) The controller updates the URL query parameters to reflect guided filters (`date`, `language`, `max_price` for the new state; it does not alter workshop-specific parameters) and keeps `view=guided`.
4) The controller calls `listGuidedTours` with the updated filters, chosen sort option, and resets the page argument to the first page.
5) When the response returns, the guided tours list component displays the updated cards and updates any pagination controls based on `total_count`, `page`, and `page_size`.

Workflow 3 – Exploring workshops with month, weekend-only, and audience filters:
1) User clicks the Workshops tab, switching the active view to workshops and setting `view=workshops` in the URL (guided-specific URL parameters like `date` and `language` remain but are ignored by workshop filtering).
2) The workshops filter form initializes its controls from URL parameters (`month`, `weekend_only`, `audience_type`, `max_price`) and from `getProgramFilterOptions` (months, audience types, price ranges).
3) The controller calls `listWorkshops` with filters derived from the current workshop controls.
4) The user changes filters (e.g., selects a different month, toggles weekend-only, selects an audience type, adjusts maximum price).
5) On each filter change, the workshops filter form emits `onFilterChange`; the controller updates the corresponding URL parameters (`month`, `weekend_only`, `audience_type`, `max_price`) while keeping `view=workshops`, then recalls `listWorkshops` with the updated filters and resets to the first page.
6) The workshops list component renders updated workshop cards using `Program` and `sample_weekend_morning_session` data and updates pagination.

Workflow 4 – Navigating to program details and cart:
1) From either the guided tours or workshops list, the user clicks a program card.
2) The card click handler constructs a navigation URL `program_detail.html?id={program.id}` using the `Program.id` field from the list response and performs a client-side navigation to that URL.
3) At any time, the user can click the cart icon in the header section of this page.
4) The cart icon click navigates to `cart.html` without passing additional parameters.
5) The user can then return (via browser back or header navigation) and the page will re-read URL parameters and re-fetch data to restore the state as encoded in the URL.
interactions: Tab click on 'Guided Tours' or 'Workshops' updates the active view, sets `view` in the URL, and triggers either `listGuidedTours` or `listWorkshops` with filters derived from URL and current control state.
Changing the guided tours date filter updates in-page state, synchronizes the `date` URL parameter, and triggers a new `listGuidedTours` call with the updated date filter.

Changing the guided tours language filter (dropdown fed by `getProgramFilterOptions.languages`) updates in-page state, synchronizes the `language` URL parameter, and triggers `listGuidedTours` with the selected language.
Changing the guided tours maximum price filter updates in-page state, synchronizes the `max_price` URL parameter, and triggers `listGuidedTours` with the numeric maximum price constraint applied.

Changing the guided tours sort option (dropdown from `getProgramFilterOptions.sort_options_tours`) updates local sort state and triggers `listGuidedTours` with the chosen `sort_by` argument (sort state is not encoded in URL).

Clicking guided tour pagination controls (next/previous or specific page number) calls `listGuidedTours` with the selected `page` and existing filter and sort arguments; page index is tracked in component state.

Changing the workshops month filter (dropdown from `getProgramFilterOptions.months`) updates in-page state, synchronizes the `month` URL parameter, and triggers `listWorkshops` with the selected month.

Toggling the workshops weekend-only filter updates in-page state, synchronizes `weekend_only` in the URL (e.g., set or remove the flag), and triggers `listWorkshops` with a boolean weekend-only filter derived from the parameter.

Changing the workshops audience type filter (dropdown from `getProgramFilterOptions.audience_types`) updates in-page state, synchronizes the `audience_type` URL parameter, and triggers `listWorkshops` with that audience filter.
Changing the workshops maximum price filter updates in-page state, synchronizes the `max_price` URL parameter, and triggers `listWorkshops` with a maximum price constraint.
Clicking workshops pagination controls calls `listWorkshops` with the selected `page` and current filter arguments.
Clicking any guided tour card triggers navigation to `program_detail.html?id={program.id}` using the `Program.id` from the tour item.
Clicking any workshop card triggers navigation to `program_detail.html?id={program.id}` using the `Program.id` from the workshop item.
Clicking the cart icon in the header navigates to `cart.html`.

Using the browser back/forward buttons causes the page to re-read URL parameters (`view`, filters, max price, etc.), reapply those to filter components, and re-call `listGuidedTours` or `listWorkshops` depending on the restored `view` value.
page_functionality.state_logic:
On initial load and on any URL change, the page controller parses the query string parameters and maps them to internal state as follows:
- Active view: `view` parameter is checked; if it matches the allowed values for this page (such as a value used for guided tours or workshops), it determines which tab is active. If absent or not recognized, the guided tours view is used as the default. The active view controls which list interface (`listGuidedTours` or `listWorkshops`) is called.
- Guided tours filters: When the active view is guided tours, `date` and `language` parameters are mapped directly into the guided tours filter state, if present. The `max_price` parameter, if present, is interpreted as a numeric maximum price filter for guided tours and is passed to `listGuidedTours` through the `filters` argument. Workshop-specific parameters (`month`, `weekend_only`, `audience_type`) are ignored when composing guided tour filters, although they remain in the URL for potential later use when switching views.
- Workshops filters: When the active view is workshops, `month`, `weekend_only`, and `audience_type` parameters are mapped directly into workshop filter state, if present. The `max_price` parameter, if present, is interpreted as a numeric maximum price filter for workshops and is passed through the `filters` argument to `listWorkshops`. Guided-specific parameters (`date`, `language`) are ignored for workshop queries but remain in the URL.
- Filter options: Regardless of view, `getProgramFilterOptions` is called once on page initialization to retrieve lists of languages, audience types, months, price ranges, and tour sort options. These values are stored in shared state and bound to filter controls. When URL parameters reference a value, the matching option is selected if it exists.
- Sorting state: Sorting for guided tours uses the `sort_options_tours` from `getProgramFilterOptions`. The currently selected `sort_by` is maintained in internal component state only and is not encoded as a URL parameter. If no sort option is selected yet, a default is chosen from the available sort options.
- Pagination state: The current page index for each view (guided tours and workshops) is tracked separately in component state. When the user changes filters or switches views, the corresponding page index is reset to the first page before calling `listGuidedTours` or `listWorkshops`. Pagination is not stored in URL parameters.
- URL synchronization: When the user interacts with filter controls or view tabs, the controller updates the URL query string to reflect the new state of `view` and relevant filter parameters. Parameters not related to the interaction are preserved. After URL updates, the controller triggers the appropriate list interface call using the synchronized state.
- Reload and deep-link behavior: When the page is reloaded or accessed via a deep link containing any combination of supported parameters, the controller reconstructs the view and filters from the URL alone (no local storage), ensures filter controls reflect those values, calls `getProgramFilterOptions`, and then calls the appropriate listing interface for the active view.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, active view selection, filter state initialization, and calls to `getProgramFilterOptions`, `listGuidedTours`, and `listWorkshops`. It also synchronizes state back to the URL when the user changes filters or tabs.

Provides two tabs (guided tours and workshops). Clicking a tab changes the active view, updates the `view` URL parameter, and notifies the page controller so that the correct list (`listGuidedTours` or `listWorkshops`) is loaded.

Displays and manages guided tours filters: date selector, language dropdown, maximum price input or selector, and sort dropdown. Initializes from URL parameters (`date`, `language`, `max_price`) and `getProgramFilterOptions` results, and emits filter changes to the controller.

Displays and manages workshop filters: month dropdown, weekend-only toggle, audience type dropdown, and maximum price input or selector. Initializes from URL parameters (`month`, `weekend_only`, `audience_type`, `max_price`) and `getProgramFilterOptions` results, and emits filter changes to the controller.

Renders a paginated list of guided tour cards based on the result of `listGuidedTours`. Uses the `program` data for basic information and `earliest_matching_session` and `matching_session_count` to show next available date/time and availability summary.

Renders a paginated list of workshop cards based on the result of `listWorkshops`. Uses the `program` data for core workshop information and `sample_weekend_morning_session` to highlight a representative session when available.

Displays pagination controls for guided tours (e.g., current page, next/previous). Reads `total_count`, `page`, and `page_size` from the `listGuidedTours` response and emits page change events to the controller.
Displays pagination controls for workshops. Reads `total_count`, `page`, and `page_size` from the `listWorkshops` response and emits page change events to the controller.
Header-level cart icon that, when clicked, navigates directly to `cart.html` without modifying cart data on this page.

Reusable card component used in both guided tours and workshops lists. Receives a `Program` object and related `ProgramSession` data and exposes a click area that navigates to `program_detail.html?id={program.id}`.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width grid similar to a 12-column system with a main content column and an internal 4-column image grid. Each image/caption acts like one column of four within the content width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom, single-column flow inside a centered white content card. At the very top of the content card sits the page controller (title + description). Directly beneath that is a horizontal view-toggle tab bar that controls which program type is shown. Below the tabs, the corresponding filter form for the active view appears, followed by that view’s results list (a vertical stack of program cards), and finally pagination controls at the bottom. Only one stack (Guided Tours or Workshops) is visible at a time, sharing the same vertical slot.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom, single-column flow inside a centered white content card. At the very top of the content card sits the page controller (title + description). Directly beneath that is a horizontal view-toggle tab bar that controls which program type is shown. Below the tabs, the corresponding filter form for the active view appears, followed by that view’s results list (a vertical stack of program cards), and finally pagination controls at the bottom. Only one stack (Guided Tours or Workshops) is visible at a time, sharing the same vertical slot.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A conservative header bar at the top of the page occupies about 15–20% of the viewport height and spans full width. The main white content card is centered and spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, with light grey background margins to the sides. Within the card, the page controller (title + description) uses approximately the top 25–30% of the card’s height, the view-toggle tabs and active filter form together use around 10–15%, and the remaining 55–65% is dedicated to the results list and pagination. Horizontally, all inner components align to the same content width, with filters and lists respecting the same left/right padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate and consistent with an editorial, museum-information feel. Line lengths stay within a comfortable reading width; text blocks have 16–24px vertical spacing between headings and paragraphs. Interactive elements (tabs, filters, pagination) use compact but not cramped padding (roughly 4–8px vertical, 8–12px horizontal). Program cards are stacked with clear separation via 16–24px vertical gaps or thin dividers, allowing a substantial number of items per page without feeling crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow starts at the strong red brand/header area, moves to the main page title in red at the top-left of the content card, then down through the descriptive introduction. The eye is then drawn to the horizontal view-toggle tabs, which read from left to right, establishing the current context (Guided Tours vs Workshops). From there, attention moves downward into the active filters, then continues linearly through the vertical list of program cards, and finally to the pagination controls at the bottom center. The cart icon in the global header and any scroll-to-top affordance remain peripheral and secondary to this main vertical narrative.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a centered, white content card on a light grey background, framed by a traditional museum-style header bar at the top. The header carries the logo on the left and a small set of utilities on the right, including language options, search, and a subtle cart icon. Below this, the Tours & Workshops content lives entirely within a fixed-width, editorial-style card. At the top of the card, a bold red page title and a short explanatory paragraph establish context in a calm, serif layout. Immediately beneath, a horizontal tab strip lets visitors switch between Guided Tours and Workshops; the active tab is highlighted with a red accent. Depending on the tab, a compact filter band appears next, stretching across the content width with dropdowns and inputs laid out in a left-aligned row that wraps on smaller screens. Under the filters, a single-column stack of program cards fills most of the vertical space. Each card runs nearly full width within the card’s inner padding, using a balanced mix of text and a small thumbnail or date block aligned to the internal four-column grid. Thin grey separators and 16–24px vertical gaps maintain clarity between entries.
