- Behavior: The browser navigates directly to the reading list page using the defined URL; no interfaces from this page are invoked.

Interaction: Refreshing the page or sharing a filtered link
- Trigger: User reloads the page or another user opens a shared URL containing filter parameters.
- Behavior: The page re-executes initialization logic, parsing URL parameters to recreate the same filter state; the article listing interface is called with those filters; the resulting article list matches the previously shared configuration.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Learn & Articles page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for filter and pagination state, ensuring that state is shareable and reproducible.

URL parameters handled by this page:
- topic: Represents the selected topic value for the article filter. When present on load, it initializes the topic filter control and is passed into the filters object used with the article listing interface.
- start_year: Represents the lower bound of the time period filter. When present, it initializes the time period filter's start year state and is included in the filters object.
- end_year: Represents the upper bound of the time period filter. When present, it initializes the time period filter's end year state and is included in the filters object.
- reading_time: Represents the selected reading time option key. When present, it initializes the reading time filter control and is mapped to the corresponding option from the article filter options interface.
- page: Represents the current page index for paginated article results. When absent, the page defaults to the first page in internal logic; when present, it is used directly when calling the article listing interface.
- sort_by (optional): Represents the current sort order for articles, if the UI exposes sorting. When present, it is passed to the article listing interface.

State behavior:
- On initial load, the controller parses all known URL parameters and constructs a unified filter state object, including topic, start_year, end_year, reading_time, and page, plus sort_by if used.
- The article filter options interface is called without parameters to obtain the current valid options. The existing URL parameters are reconciled with these options (for example, if a topic from the URL no longer exists in the options list, the page can treat it as no selection).
- When any filter control changes (topic, time period, or reading time), the page controller updates the in-memory filter state, resets the page index to the first page, and writes the new state to the URL query string using navigation APIs without reloading the page.
- After every state change that affects filters or pagination, the article listing interface is called with an object that includes the active filters, sort_by value, and page/page_size values.
- The page never uses localStorage or other client-side persistent storage to manage filter state; all persistent state relevant to this page is encoded in the URL so that reloading or sharing the link reconstitutes the same configuration.
- The learn page content interface and article filter options interface do not depend on URL parameters and are called on every page load (or refresh) to ensure content and options remain in sync with backend data.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, filter state management, and calls to interfaces to populate the Learn page and article list.
Displays introductory Learn page text, featured topics, and highlighted articles returned by the learn page content interface.
Shows a list of featured topics from the learn page content interface and allows users to click one to apply it as the current article topic filter.
Displays highlighted articles from the learn page content interface and provides navigation to their detail pages when clicked.
Acts as the entry point to the articles area, including a control that scrolls or focuses the page to the articles list when activated.
Hosts and synchronizes the topic, time period, and reading time filters for articles, initializing from URL parameters and emitting unified filter change events.
Displays selectable article topics retrieved from the article filter options interface and allows the user to select one to filter the article list.
Displays time period presets and optionally allows custom start and end years for filtering articles by time period.
Displays reading time filter options from the article filter options interface and lets users restrict the article list by estimated reading duration.
Allows users to change the sort order of articles (for example, by date or reading time) and updates the article listing request accordingly.
Renders the list of articles returned from the article listing interface according to the current filters, sort order, and page.
Represents a single article entry in the articles list, displaying its key metadata and providing a clickable element to navigate to the article detail page.
Displays pagination controls based on the total count and page information from the article listing interface and allows navigation between pages.
Provides a direct navigational link from the Learn & Articles page to the 'My Reading List' page.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width grid similar to a 12-column system with a main content column and an internal 4-column image grid. Each image/caption acts like one column of four within the content width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow inside a single centered content card, mirroring the mockup’s pattern: a global brand header at the top (outside this spec), then one main white card. Within that card the sequence is: (1) Learn & Articles intro section with page title and explanatory text, (2) a horizontal featured topics selector, (3) a full-width strip of highlighted article teasers in a 4-column image grid (echoing the mockup’s photo row), (4) an 'All articles' section header with access to the reading list, (5) a horizontal filters bar containing topic, time period, reading time, and sort controls, and (6) a vertical list of article items with pagination at the bottom. No sidebars; all components stack vertically and stay within the same centered column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow inside a single centered content card, mirroring the mockup’s pattern: a global brand header at the top (outside this spec), then one main white card. Within that card the sequence is: (1) Learn & Articles intro section with page title and explanatory text, (2) a horizontal featured topics selector, (3) a full-width strip of highlighted article teasers in a 4-column image grid (echoing the mockup’s photo row), (4) an 'All articles' section header with access to the reading list, (5) a horizontal filters bar containing topic, time period, reading time, and sort controls, and (6) a vertical list of article items with pagination at the bottom. No sidebars; all components stack vertically and stay within the same centered column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A conservative fixed-width content column (about 70–80% of the viewport, with a max width in the ~1040–1200px range) sits centered on a light grey page background. The global header occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport; the main content card fills the remaining 80–85%. Inside the card: the intro section (title + text) uses about the top 25–30% of the card’s height; the featured topics and highlighted articles strip together use another ~25–30%, creating a strong mid-page band of visual options; the lower 40–50% is dedicated to the filter bar and the scrollable articles list with pagination. Horizontal space is treated as a 12-column grid; multi-card elements (featured topics and highlighted articles) use an internal 4-column layout, each card roughly one quarter of the content width with 8–12px gutters.
layout_strategies.content_density: Typography and spacing follow a moderate, editorial density: text lines are comfortably long but not wide-screen, with clear paragraph spacing (roughly 24px between blocks) and card padding around 24–32px inside the main content card. The intro feels breathable but not minimalist. The highlighted articles strip and featured topics list are denser: cards sit relatively close with thin gutters and subtle borders to maximize items in one row without feeling cramped. The filters bar is compact but legible, with controls arranged in a single row on large screens and allowed to wrap on smaller widths while preserving comfortable touch targets.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the museum’s red brand bar and logo in the global header, then moves across any top utilities. From there, it drops into the main content card: first catching the red-styled page title at the top of the Learn & Articles intro, then scanning the explanatory paragraph below with occasional colored links. The gaze is then pulled to the horizontal featured topics row, which acts as a bridge into the content. Below that, the highlighted articles strip – a strong horizontal band of four visual cards – becomes a secondary focal point similar to the mockup’s historical photo row. As users scroll, attention flows down to the 'All articles' header, the filters bar aligned beneath it, and finally into the vertically stacked article list. A subtle, fixed scroll-to-top control at the lower right edge of the viewport remains peripheral and secondary throughout.

layout.overall_description: The Learn & Articles page sits within a conservative museum-style layout: a red brand header at the top, and below it a single large white content card centered on a light grey background. Inside this card, a red-accented page title and descriptive text establish the editorial tone at the top, followed by a horizontal band of featured topics that resemble selectable chips or compact cards. Just beneath, a full-width strip of four highlighted article teasers, each with an image and caption, echoes the mockup’s historical photo grid and creates a strong horizontal visual anchor in the middle of the page. Lower down, an 'All articles' header and a compact filters bar span the content width, organizing topic, time period, reading time, and sort options into a single row. Under that, a vertical stack of article list items fills the remainder of the card, each item reading like a small editorial card with a thumbnail and text. Pagination is centered at the bottom of the card, while a discreet fixed scroll-to-top arrow hovers near the bottom-right of the viewport, keeping the experience practical and content-first without distracting from the main reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the whole Learn & Articles page canvas below the global museum header. It places a single, large white content card in the vertical center of the viewport, anchored to the top just beneath the header. This card is centered horizontally, occupying roughly 70–80% of the viewport width with a fixed max width (around 1040–1200px) and equal left/right grey margins. The controller ensures consistent outer padding (about 32px) around the card content and enforces the linear top-to-bottom stacking of all subsequent components: intro section at the top, followed by featured topics, highlighted articles strip, the articles section header, filters bar, articles list, and pagination at the bottom. The page background remains a calm, light grey, contrasting the white card. A small, circular scroll-to-top button is positioned by the controller at the lower right of the viewport, just inside the content width, appearing after the user scrolls past the fold, echoing the mockup’s peripheral scroll arrow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines a centered, fixed-width main column; the content card scrolls with the page, while the scroll-to-top control is fixed near the bottom-right of the viewport within the page’s content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The intro section sits at the very top inside the white content card, directly beneath its top padding. It spans the full content width, aligning with the internal grid. At the top-left of this section is the page title 'Learn & Articles' in large, red serif type, stretching across most of the width but left-aligned and not center-justified. Below the title, with about 16–24px vertical separation, one or two paragraphs of descriptive body text occupy roughly 60–70% of the width, leaving comfortable margins on both sides within the card. Inline colored links and small inline icons punctuate the text but do not break the overall linear flow. The section’s vertical height takes roughly the top quarter of the card, with about 32px top padding from the card edge and 24–32px bottom margin before the next component, creating a clear but not oversized separation from the featured topics below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section pinned to the top of the main content card, expanding in height based on text length.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured topics list sits immediately below the intro section, separated by roughly 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the full internal width of the content card and is visually positioned as a horizontal selection band. Within this band, topics are laid out using an internal 4-column grid: on large screens, up to four topic items (pills or compact cards) sit in a single row, each occupying about one quarter of the content width with 8–12px gutters between them. Each topic chip/card is left-aligned with its column, with generous horizontal padding (8–12px) and modest vertical padding (4–8px), bordered or underlined subtly to suit the conservative style. If more topics are present, they wrap to a second row but maintain alignment to the 4-column structure. The top of this component aligns with the same left margin as the intro text, and the bottom sits about 24px above the highlighted articles strip, forming a tight but clear cluster of options between the page intro and the visual teaser row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that adapts to the number of topics by adding rows; items wrap but stay within the same centered content width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The highlighted articles strip is positioned directly below the featured topics list, with an approximate 24–32px vertical gap, and acts as the page’s primary mid-level visual band. It occupies the full width of the content card and is rendered as a single horizontal row of four article teaser cards on large screens. Each teaser card uses the internal 4-column grid and is about one quarter of the card width, separated by 8–12px horizontal gutters. Within each card, a thumbnail image sits on top, edge-to-edge inside the card with a thin grey border or subtle outline, and the article title and a short caption or label sit directly beneath the image, left-aligned, with 8–16px of internal padding. All cards share the same fixed height to form a clean, continuous strip reminiscent of the mockup’s historical photos row. The overall band sits roughly in the middle third of the content card, visually bridging the intro and featured topics above with the more detailed article list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal strip; cards remain in a single row on large screens and can responsively stack or reduce in columns on smaller viewports while respecting internal gutters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The articles section header appears below the highlighted articles strip, separated by a slightly larger vertical gap of about 32–40px to signal the transition from featured content to the main catalogue. It spans the full content width but is visually concentrated in a slim horizontal band. On the left, aligned with the card’s text column, is a medium-sized serif heading such as 'All articles' in red or dark text, matching the editorial tone. On the right, on the same baseline, the 'My reading list' link is placed (implemented by the my-reading-list-link component), creating a left-right balance across the header’s width. The total height of this header band is modest, around 48–64px, just enough to clearly separate it from the content above and the filters bar below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal band that anchors the articles section and provides an anchor point for in-page navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The articles filters bar sits directly beneath the articles section header with a small vertical gap of about 16–24px, visually attached to that heading as a unified control area. It stretches across the entire internal width of the content card and appears as a low-profile horizontal bar with a very light grey background or subtle top/bottom rule lines. Inside, the topic filter control is anchored to the left, followed by the time period filter in the center segment, the reading-time filter to its right, and the sort control aligned to the far right. The bar has about 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal internal padding, creating a compact but readable row of inputs. On larger screens, all controls align on a single baseline; on smaller widths, the controls may wrap to a second line while maintaining a left-to-right order. The filters bar sits about 24px above the first article list item, clearly separating the controls from the content they affect.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a sticky element within the main content area: once the user scrolls the page so that the filters bar would leave the viewport top, it pins to the top edge of the viewport within the content width, remaining visible as the articles list scrolls beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the articles filters bar, the topic filter control is positioned at the far left and occupies roughly 20–25% of the bar’s width. It appears as a labeled dropdown: a small serif or sans-serif label sits above or to the left of a conservative dropdown field. On wide screens, the label is left-aligned directly above the dropdown text, with about 4–8px of vertical separation, keeping the total height compact. The dropdown itself is about the same height as other controls in the bar, with 4–8px internal vertical padding and 8–12px horizontal padding, and aligns vertically with the other controls so the row feels orderly. There is a small 8–12px gutter to the right before the time period control begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the filters bar, aligning to the left and resizing proportionally with the bar; wraps to the next line under the same left alignment if horizontal space is constrained.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time period filter control occupies the central portion of the filters bar, immediately to the right of the topic filter with a modest horizontal gap. It spans roughly 30–35% of the bar’s width, making it one of the wider controls to accommodate a range slider and preset buttons. Within this block, a small label sits at the top-left, with a horizontal slider or range input directly beneath it, and optional preset buttons (such as 'Ancient', 'Medieval', 'Modern') sitting in a single row under or alongside the slider. Vertical spacing within this block is tight but legible, with 4–8px gaps between label, slider, and presets, maintaining the overall compact feel of the bar. The control aligns vertically with the other filters, so its visual height defines much of the filter bar’s height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the filters bar, centered between the topic and reading-time controls; shrinks or stacks internal elements (e.g., presets wrap to two lines) as the bar narrows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-time filter control is placed to the right of the time period control, separated by an 8–12px gutter, and uses about 15–20% of the filters bar’s width. It presents as a compact labeled group of options, likely a segmented control (e.g., 'Short', 'Medium', 'Long') or a small dropdown. The label sits just above the options, left-aligned, with 4–8px spacing. The segmented options fit in a single line where possible, reinforcing the horizontal rhythm of the bar; each option has minimal horizontal padding but remains finger-friendly. The control’s overall height aligns with the neighboring controls so the bar reads as a single, cohesive strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the filters bar, right of the time-period control; if space is constrained, the segmented options may wrap under the label but remain left-aligned within their column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control sits at the far right end of the filters bar, visually anchoring the row. It occupies roughly 12–15% of the bar’s width and is right-aligned, with its label (e.g., 'Sort by') aligned to the right above a dropdown field. The dropdown width is just enough to show the sort criterion (e.g., 'Newest', 'Oldest', 'A–Z'), with 8–12px inner horizontal padding, and its right edge aligns with the right padding of the content card. This right alignment mirrors the positioning of utilities in the mockup’s header, providing a subtle echo of that visual pattern. The vertical spacing between label and dropdown is minimal (4–8px) to keep the bar compact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element pinned to the right of the filters bar; maintains right alignment even when the bar becomes sticky at the top of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The articles list is placed directly beneath the filters bar with about 24px of vertical spacing, occupying the full width of the content card and extending downward as the main scrollable body of the page. It is composed of vertically stacked article list items, each separated by approximately 24–32px of whitespace to keep the list readable and structured. The list adheres to the same centered grid as the card, so each item is full-width within the content column, with left and right margins lining up exactly under the intro text, topics, and highlighted strip above. The list visually dominates the lower half of the card, continuing down for multiple viewport heights while the filters bar can remain sticky at the top, giving the impression of a long editorial index of articles.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls vertically as a standard block; its top is aligned just beneath the (potentially sticky) filters bar, so the list itself moves while the filters remain fixed at the top once activated.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each article list item appears as a subtle, full-width content row within the articles list, styled like a compact editorial card. The item spans the entire width of the internal content column with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides, sitting on the white background of the main card with either a thin bottom border or a shadowless card edge to separate items. Inside each row, a thumbnail image sits on the left, occupying roughly 25–30% of the row width and aligned with the text baseline. To the right of the thumbnail, using the remaining 70–75% width, the article title (in medium-weight serif, slightly larger than body text) appears at the top, followed by metadata (date, topic, reading time) in a smaller line, and then a two- to three-line excerpt below, all left-aligned. Vertical spacing between these text elements is modest (4–8px), keeping the item dense but readable. Each entire row is clickable, with a hover state that might slightly darken the border or subtly tint the background, in line with the conservative aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the articles list; items stack vertically with consistent spacing and adjust in height based on text length while maintaining the same thumbnail-to-text proportion.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination control sits at the very bottom of the main content card, positioned directly beneath the last article list item with around 32–40px of vertical space to mark the end of the list. It is centered horizontally within the content width, forming a short, single-line row of page numbers, previous/next buttons, or both. The pagination controls are compact, with 4–8px of internal vertical padding and 8–12px horizontal padding per button, spaced 4–8px apart, following the site’s button sizing conventions. A little extra vertical padding above and below (about 16px) visually grounds the control at the end of the content. The bottom of this component aligns with the bottom padding of the card, leaving a clean margin to the card edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the content flow; appears after users scroll through the articles list and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'My reading list' link is visually integrated into the articles section header as a subtle, right-aligned utility. It appears on the same horizontal line as the 'All articles' heading, anchored to the far right of the content card, with about 8–16px of horizontal breathing room from the card’s right edge. The link is styled as underlined or subtly accented text (possibly with a small bookmark icon to its left), slightly smaller than the section heading but still clearly legible. There is no heavy button styling; instead, the link adopts a conservative, inline text appearance that feels like a secondary action. Its proximity to the articles heading communicates that it’s a personal subset of the main articles catalogue without competing visually with the primary navigation elements above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline link within the articles section header; scrolls with the page and maintains its right-aligned position relative to the content card’s width.

name: Article Detail
title: Article Detail
description: Displays the full details of a single article about cheese history and lets users save or remove the article from their personal reading list, with navigation back to article listings and the reading list.

core_features: Load and display a single article using the article ID from the URL via getArticleDetail, including title, topic label, time period display, estimated reading time, publication date (if provided), and full content.
Show whether the article is already in the user's reading list using the is_in_reading_list flag returned by getArticleDetail.
Provide a single toggle control (Add to Reading List / Remove from Reading List) that calls addArticleToReadingList or removeArticleFromReadingList based on current state.
Update the UI state immediately after add/remove actions to reflect the article's current reading-list status without reloading the page.
Provide navigation back to the articles list (Learn page) and to the My Reading List page using URL-based navigation.
Handle loading and error states when fetching article details or updating the reading list, and prevent duplicate submissions while an operation is in progress.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Open an article from the Learn page
1. User is on learn.html with their own filters/search parameters encoded in that page’s URL.
2. User clicks an article link, which navigates to article_detail.html with at least id in the query string (optionally including an additional parameter such as return_to carrying the originating list URL if that is set up on learn.html).
3. Article Detail page initializes, reads the id parameter from the URL, and calls getArticleDetail with this article_id.
4. While data is loading, the page shows a loading state instead of article content.
5. On success, the article’s metadata and content are rendered, and the Save/Remove toggle reflects the is_in_reading_list flag from the response.
6. User can read the article and, if desired, click the Save/Remove toggle to update their reading list.
7. User clicks "Back to Articles"; the page navigates to learn.html. If a return URL parameter is available, that exact URL (including filters) is used; otherwise, navigation goes to a default learn.html URL.

Workflow: Open an article directly (e.g., from index.html)
1. User clicks an article promotion or link from index.html, which navigates to article_detail.html with the article id in the query string.
2. Article Detail page reads id and calls getArticleDetail(article_id).
3. The article is displayed once loaded; the reading list toggle reflects the is_in_reading_list value.
4. User can save or unsave the article via the toggle.
5. User can navigate to My Reading List via the dedicated link, which takes them to reading_list.html.

Workflow: Open an article from My Reading List
1. User is on reading_list.html viewing their saved articles.
2. User clicks an article in the list, which navigates to article_detail.html with the saved article’s id.
3. Article Detail page loads the article via getArticleDetail and shows the article as already saved (is_in_reading_list = true) where applicable.
4. User can read the article and optionally click the toggle to remove it from the reading list.
5. If the user navigates to My Reading List using the header link after removal, reading_list.html will reflect the updated list when it loads (based on its own data loading logic).

Workflow: Add an article to the reading list from the detail page
1. User arrives on article_detail.html with id in the URL and waits for the article to load via getArticleDetail.
2. The reading list toggle indicates that the article is not currently in the reading list.
3. User clicks the toggle control.
4. The toggle component disables further clicks while the request is in progress and calls addArticleToReadingList with the current article_id.
5. On success, the component updates its internal state to indicate that the article is now in the reading list and changes the label/appearance of the control to the "Remove" state.
6. If the user navigates to reading_list.html, the saved article appears according to that page's logic.

Workflow: Remove an article from the reading list from the detail page
1. User arrives on article_detail.html where getArticleDetail indicates is_in_reading_list = true.
2. The toggle shows the "Remove from Reading List" state.
3. User clicks the toggle control.
4. The component disables further clicks and calls removeArticleFromReadingList with the article_id.
5. On success, the toggle updates its state to indicate the article is no longer saved and changes back to the "Add" state.
6. When the user visits reading_list.html, this article no longer appears in their list.

Workflow: Handle failure when updating the reading list
1. User clicks the Save/Remove toggle.
2. The component calls addArticleToReadingList or removeArticleFromReadingList accordingly.
3. If the call fails (for example, due to network or server issues), the component restores the previous toggle state and re-enables the control.
4. An error state is displayed in a non-blocking way (for example, inline near the toggle) so that the user understands the action did not complete and can retry.
interactions: Page load (onInit): On initial page load, the component responsible for initialization reads the id (and any optional navigation-related parameters) from the URL query string and triggers getArticleDetail(article_id).

Loading state: While getArticleDetail is in progress, article content and actions are disabled or replaced with a loading indicator; navigation links that do not depend on article data may still be available.

Click Back to Articles link: Navigates to learn.html. If an originating list URL or filter parameters have been encoded in an optional URL parameter (e.g., return_to), that value is used; otherwise, the user is taken to a baseline learn.html.
Click My Reading List link in header: Navigates directly to reading_list.html without additional API calls from this page.

Click Save/Remove toggle: If is_in_reading_list is false, the click dispatches addArticleToReadingList(article_id); if true, it dispatches removeArticleFromReadingList(article_id). The control is temporarily disabled until the call resolves.

API response handling for getArticleDetail: On success, binds article, topic_label, time_period_display, and is_in_reading_list to the respective UI components. On failure, shows an error state and may provide a retry action that re-calls getArticleDetail with the same article_id.

API response handling for addArticleToReadingList: On success, sets is_in_reading_list to true and may optionally update any in-page indicators related to the reading list. On failure, restores the toggle to the unsaved state and surfaces an error indication.
API response handling for removeArticleFromReadingList: On success, sets is_in_reading_list to false. On failure, restores the toggle to the saved state and surfaces an error indication.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is derived from URL parameters and API responses, not from localStorage or other persistent client-side storage. The required URL parameter is id, representing the article ID. The page is accessed as article_detail.html with id in the query string. On initialization, the page reads the id parameter and calls getArticleDetail with this value as article_id. The response provides article (the Article entity), topic_label (a human-readable label for the topic enum), time_period_display (a formatted description based on the time period fields), and is_in_reading_list (a boolean). These values populate the page state and are passed down to child components. The is_in_reading_list flag is the single source of truth for whether the article is saved while the user remains on this page; it is updated in-memory when addArticleToReadingList or removeArticleFromReadingList complete successfully. The URL does not change when the user toggles the reading list state. Navigation back to the Learn page and to My Reading List uses pure URL navigation. For returning to a filtered article list, the implementation may optionally accept an additional URL parameter (such as a return_to URL) that encodes the originating list state; if present, this parameter is used to construct the Back to Articles navigation target. If such a parameter is not present, Back to Articles navigates to a default learn.html URL. All other transient UI states (loading, error, disabled buttons) are managed in component state and are not encoded in the URL.

components.functionality: Initializes the Article Detail page by reading the article ID (and any optional navigation-related parameters) from the URL query string, calling getArticleDetail with that ID, and distributing the loaded data (article, topic_label, time_period_display, is_in_reading_list) and loading/error states to child components.

Displays high-level article information and primary navigation controls. This includes the article title, topic label, formatted time period display, estimated reading time, publication date if available, and any additional metadata provided by the Article object. It also renders a Back to Articles navigation link and a My Reading List link.
Renders the main body of the article using the content field from the Article model. It displays the full educational text and any structured content provided, without altering the article state.

Provides a single control that allows the user to add or remove the current article from their reading list. It inspects the current is_in_reading_list flag to decide whether to call addArticleToReadingList or removeArticleFromReadingList on click, manages a transient loading state during the API call, updates the local is_in_reading_list state on success, and reverts the UI on failure.

Displays non-blocking status and error feedback related to article loading and reading-list actions. It listens to events from the data loader and the reading list toggle to show messages for loading errors and add/remove failures, and clears messages when actions are retried or succeed.

Provides duplicate navigation controls at the end of the article content so users can easily go back to the article list or open My Reading List when they finish reading. It uses the same navigation logic as the header component.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width grid similar to a 12-column system with a main content column and an internal 4-column image grid. Each image/caption acts like one column of four within the content width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a strictly top-to-bottom, single-column editorial flow inside a centered white content card. Above the card sits the global museum header (logo, red brand bar, utilities). Inside the card, the article header (title, metadata) occupies the top, with the reading-list toggle visually attached to this header near the top-right. Directly beneath, any transient article-status messages appear as full-width notice bars. Below that, the article-content-section forms the main body of the page, running as a readable single column that may internally use the 4-column image grid pattern for inline galleries. At the bottom edge of the card, the article-footer-nav provides back/next-style navigation and links to the reading list.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a strictly top-to-bottom, single-column editorial flow inside a centered white content card. Above the card sits the global museum header (logo, red brand bar, utilities). Inside the card, the article header (title, metadata) occupies the top, with the reading-list toggle visually attached to this header near the top-right. Directly beneath, any transient article-status messages appear as full-width notice bars. Below that, the article-content-section forms the main body of the page, running as a readable single column that may internally use the 4-column image grid pattern for inline galleries. At the bottom edge of the card, the article-footer-nav provides back/next-style navigation and links to the reading list.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header occupies roughly the upper 15–20% of the viewport. The main content card sits centered below it, spanning about 70–80% of the viewport width, constrained by a fixed max width (around 960–1040px) with light gray page background visible on both sides. Within the card, the article-header plus reading-list-toggle and any status messages together use roughly the top 25–30% of the card height. The article-content-section dominates the central 55–65%, expanding with article length. The article-footer-nav occupies a shallow horizontal band (about 10–15% of the card height) at the very bottom. Horizontal padding inside the card is about 24–32px on left and right; vertical spacing between major blocks (header, messages, content, footer) is in the 24–32px range, following the 8px base scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate and editorial. Text in the article-content-section runs in a single column with a comfortable line length (roughly 60–75 characters per line), and paragraph spacing of about 16–24px. There is enough breathing room between the article header, status messages, and body text to avoid crowding, but not so much that the page feels sparse. The internal image grid within the content uses a denser 4-column layout across the content width, with narrow 8–12px gutters to fit four thumbnails comfortably in a single row, echoing the mockup's compact photo band.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye first hits the strong red branding in the global header at the top, then narrows to the centered white content card. Within the card, the red article title in the article-header becomes the primary anchor, with any metadata directly beneath it. The reading-list-toggle, positioned near the title on the right, is noticed next as a clear but secondary control. If present, article-status-messages appear just below the header as a horizontal band, briefly capturing attention before the eye drops into the main article-content-section, scanning paragraphs and any inline green links or small images. As the user scrolls down and reaches the end of the content, the eye naturally rests on the article-footer-nav strip, offering clear paths back to listings or to the reading list. The data-loader, when active, temporarily interrupts this flow by occupying the main card area with a centered loading state.

layout.overall_description: The Article Detail page sits beneath the museum’s fixed global header, which presents a red brand bar with logo and utilities across the top. Below this, centered on a light gray background, a white rectangular content card spans roughly three-quarters of the viewport width, giving the page a calm, editorial feel. Inside the card, the top zone is dominated by the article header: a large red serif title aligned to the left, with smaller muted metadata just below. On the same horizontal band, toward the top-right corner of the card, a compact reading-list toggle button mirrors the language pill style from the mockup, visually tied to the title area without overpowering it. Just under this header band, any feedback about saving or removing the article appears as a full-width message strip. The main body of the article then flows straight down in a single column: paragraphs of text with moderate line length and regular vertical rhythm, occasionally punctuated by inline color-accented links or small icons. When the article includes a set of historical images, they appear as a full-width row of four evenly sized image cards within the text, each occupying about a quarter of the content width with thin gutters and small captions, forming a denser horizontal band. At the bottom of the card, a low-profile navigation bar runs edge to edge: on the left, a link or button leads back to the article list, while on the right another control leads to the user’s reading list or related navigation. The entire composition feels like a curated museum article—orderly, content-first, and slightly traditional—with a clear vertical reading path from brand, to title, to content, and finally to navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-loader sits in the same horizontal space as the main content card, centered within the page’s fixed-width content column. When active, it either replaces the card or overlays it with a semi-opaque white panel that matches the card’s width (around 70–80% of the viewport, max ~960–1040px) and inherits its corner radius and subtle shadow (if used in the system). Within this panel, a spinner or loading illustration is vertically and horizontally centered, with a brief loading message beneath it, both aligned to the card’s central axis. The gray page background remains visible around the card, preserving the centered editorial feel even during loading. There is generous vertical padding (32–48px) above and below the loader content inside this panel so it doesn’t feel cramped. When loading completes, this overlay or placeholder collapses away to reveal the article-header at the top of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: When active, it is effectively fixed within the viewport’s central column: it does not scroll independently but occupies the same scroll position as the main card, often appearing vertically centered in the visible area. It blocks interaction with the underlying article content until data is loaded, then disappears entirely, yielding its space to the other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-header occupies the topmost portion of the main content card, spanning the full internal width from left padding to right padding. It begins approximately 32–48px below the global header, with the card’s top margin providing this separation. Inside the card, the header’s content is aligned to the left: an optional breadcrumb or section label sits at the very top in a small serif or all-caps style, followed by a large red serif article title on the next line. The title spans most of the card width but respects sensible line breaks to avoid overly long lines. Below the title, a single line or two of metadata (such as date, category, reading time) appears in a smaller, muted color, left-aligned and separated from the title by about 8–12px. Vertically, the header band uses roughly 96–160px in height depending on title length. On the same vertical zone, the reading-list-toggle is aligned to the right edge of the card, either approximately aligned with the bottom of the title or with the metadata line, creating a balanced top-right focal point. There is a clear bottom margin of about 24–32px between the bottom of the header (metadata line) and the first article-status-messages or, if no messages exist, the start of the article-content-section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The header scrolls naturally with the page as part of the main content card; it is not sticky. Its vertical position is fixed relative to the top of the card, serving as the first element within the card layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-content-section forms the central body of the card and sits immediately below the article header (and below any article-status-messages if they are present). It spans the full internal width of the card between the left and right padding (roughly 24–32px in from the card edges) and stretches vertically for as long as the article requires. The first paragraph begins about 24–32px below the element above it, creating a clear yet moderate separation. Text is typeset in a traditional serif, with a comfortable line length of about 60–75 characters and line spacing that sits between tight and airy. Each paragraph is separated by 16–24px of vertical space, maintaining the moderate content density. Inline links use the established accent color (e.g., green) and may be slightly bold to echo the mockup’s emphasized links. Small inline icons can sit immediately before or after link text, aligned to the baseline, with a small 4–8px gap. When the article includes a band of historical photos or related images, they appear in this section as a dedicated horizontal block: a 4-column grid aligned to the same left and right content edges. Each image card occupies roughly one-quarter of the content width with thin 8–12px gutters between them and a light gray border or subtle outline, with concise captions directly beneath each image. This image band occupies about 40–60% of the visible card height when in view and is separated from surrounding paragraphs by 24–32px above and below. The rest of the body may continue beneath the image band, maintaining the same single-column rhythm until it meets the article-footer-nav at the bottom of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This section scrolls as the main content of the page, expanding vertically as needed. It does not use any fixed or sticky behavior; internal elements (like an image grid) are laid out in-flow, respecting the card’s fixed-width, centered layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-toggle is a compact, pill-like button positioned inside the main content card’s top area, visually associated with the article-header. It is anchored to the top-right corner of the card’s content area, sitting on the same horizontal band as the title or metadata. Horizontally, it is flush with the card’s right padding (about 24–32px from the card’s outer edge). Vertically, its vertical center roughly aligns with either the lower third of the title block or the metadata line, depending on header composition, so it feels part of the header cluster rather than floating separately. The button itself has a low-to-moderate profile: around 28–36px tall, with 8px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding, rounded corners, and a flat fill color that matches the site’s secondary actions (often a subtle tone, with a red outline or text accent when active). When the label text is longer (e.g., “Remove from reading list”), the button expands horizontally but remains constrained to its top-right zone, never overlapping the title text. There is a small 8–12px gap between the button and the header content below it. On narrow viewports, the toggle can drop below the title and align left, but still maintain about 16–24px top margin before the first body text or messages.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The toggle is an in-flow element within the header block but visually docked to the right edge. It scrolls with the rest of the content and does not stick to the viewport. Its position remains consistent relative to the article-header, adapting responsively by stacking below the title on very small screens while preserving its top-of-card prominence.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-status-messages area sits immediately beneath the article-header cluster (title, metadata, and reading-list-toggle), acting as a transitional band before the main article body. It spans the full internal width of the card, from left padding to right padding, and is separated from the header above by a vertical gap of about 16–24px. Each message appears as a horizontally oriented bar with a subtle background tint (e.g., pale green for success, pale red for error), a thin left border or accent stripe in a stronger tone, and text left-aligned within the bar. The bar height is modest—around 40–56px depending on text length—with 8–12px vertical padding and 12–16px horizontal padding inside the message itself. Multiple messages, if allowed, would stack vertically with 8–12px gaps between them, but the total block is still visually compact. There is a clear 24–32px gap between the bottom of the last message and the start of the article-content-section, so the messages feel temporally distinct yet spatially connected to the action (reading-list-toggle) that likely triggered them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Messages are inline, appearing and disappearing within the normal document flow. They push the article content down when present and collapse completely when absent, without leaving dead space. They do not use fixed or sticky behavior and scroll away with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-footer-nav is anchored at the bottom of the main content card, directly beneath the final paragraph or image block of the article-content-section. It spans the full internal width of the card. A subtle top border or faint gray rule separates it from the article body, with a 24–32px gap above the border for breathing room. Within this footer band, navigation elements are arranged along a single horizontal row with a conservative layout: on the left side, aligned to the card’s left padding, a link or button labeled something like “Back to articles” appears; on the right side, aligned to the card’s right padding, a counterpart control might link to “View reading list” or the next/previous article. The band’s height is modest—around 56–72px total—with vertical padding of 12–16px so the controls are comfortably clickable but not oversized. The controls themselves echo the flat button styles from the mockup: simple outlined or text buttons with red accent for primary navigation and neutral tones for secondary. On very narrow viewports, these elements can stack vertically, with the back link above and the reading-list link below, centered horizontally, and separated by about 8–12px. Beneath this footer nav, the bottom margin of the card (24–32px) creates a cushion before the page background continues.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The footer navigation is in-flow and sits at the natural end of the article content. It scrolls into view as the user reaches the bottom and scrolls out of view when the user moves away; it is not sticky. It consistently stays attached to the bottom of the card, regardless of article length.

name: My Reading List
title: My Reading List
description: Displays the user’s saved educational articles, allows removal of items, basic organization via sorting/filtering, and navigation to article details or the Learn section.

core_features: Load the current user’s reading list and associated article metadata using the getReadingList interface.
Display all saved articles with key information (title, topic, historical time period, estimated reading time, date added, and any item notes).
Provide a clickable article title that navigates to the article detail page (article_detail.html?id={article_id}).
Allow users to remove an article from their reading list via removeArticleFromReadingList, with success/error feedback and automatic list refresh.
Offer simple organization tools (client-side sorting and filtering by article topic, historical period, and estimated reading time) controlled by URL parameters.
Provide client-side pagination of the reading list controlled by URL parameters.
Provide a printing action that invokes the browser’s print dialog for the current list view.
Provide a link to browse additional articles in the Learn section (learn.html).
user_workflows:
View and browse the reading list:
1. User navigates to reading_list.html (via header navigation or a direct link).
2. On page initialization, the reading-list-data-loader component calls getReadingList.
3. The page reads URL parameters (e.g., sort, topic, min_read_time, max_read_time, period_start, period_end, page, page_size, view).
4. Client-side logic applies the selected filters and sorting to the items returned by getReadingList.
5. The reading-list-items component displays the appropriate subset of articles for the current page.
6. The reading-list-summary and reading-list-toolbar components update counts, total reading time, and control states based on the current filtered list.

Open an article from the reading list:
1. In the reading-list-items component, the user clicks an article title.
2. The click handler reads the article.id (which equals reading_list_item.article_id).
3. The browser navigates to article_detail.html?id={article_id} using standard link navigation.
4. The article detail page handles its own data loading; this page is not responsible for that.

Remove an article from the reading list:
1. In the reading-list-items component, the user clicks the remove control associated with a specific item.
2. The onClickRemoveItem handler captures the associated article_id from the item.
3. The handler calls removeArticleFromReadingList with the article_id.
4. While waiting, the corresponding row can show a loading or disabled state.
5. On success, the reading-list-notifications component displays a success message.
6. The reading-list-data-loader component re-calls getReadingList to obtain the updated list.
7. The page re-applies the current URL-based filters/sorting/pagination to the refreshed data and re-renders.
8. On error, the notification component displays an error message; the item remains in the list.

Organize the reading list with sorting and filtering:
1. The user changes a control in the reading-list-toolbar (e.g., selects a topic filter, changes sort order, adjusts reading-time range, or selects a time-period range).
2. The corresponding event handler updates URL search parameters (e.g., topic, sort, min_read_time, max_read_time, period_start, period_end) via history.pushState or similar, without reloading the page.
3. The reading-list-data-loader (or a shared state manager) listens for URL change events or is notified directly and re-applies client-side filtering and sorting to the already-loaded items from getReadingList.
4. The reading-list-items and reading-list-summary components update to reflect the new criteria.

Paginate through a long reading list:
1. The user interacts with pagination controls (next/previous/page number) in reading-list-pagination.
2. The onPageChange handler updates the page and page_size URL parameters.
3. No additional API call is needed; client-side logic calculates the correct slice of the filtered, sorted list to display.
4. reading-list-items renders only the items for that page while the summary and toolbar remain in sync.

Print or export the reading list view:
1. The user clicks a print button in the reading-list-toolbar.
2. The onClickPrint handler invokes the browser’s print dialog (e.g., window.print) for the current view.
3. The printed content reflects the current filters/sorting/pagination applied on the page.

Browse more articles from the Learn section:
1. The user clicks the "Browse more articles" link in the reading-list-toolbar or reading-list-empty-state component.
2. The browser navigates to learn.html via a standard link.
3. The Learn page manages its own data loading and behavior.
interactions: On page load, the reading-list-data-loader component triggers getReadingList and handles loading and error states.
The user can click article titles to navigate to article_detail.html?id={article_id}.
The user can click a remove button for any item, which calls removeArticleFromReadingList(article_id), then refreshes the list with getReadingList.
Sorting selection (e.g., by date added, title, or reading time) updates the sort URL parameter and re-orders the client-side item list.
Topic filtering selection updates the topic URL parameter and filters the list by Article.topic.
Time period filter inputs update period_start and period_end URL parameters and filter by article time_period_start_year/time_period_end_year.
Reading time filter controls update min_read_time and max_read_time URL parameters and filter by Article.estimated_reading_time_minutes.
Pagination controls update page and page_size URL parameters and change which portion of the filtered list is visible.
A view mode control (e.g., list vs compact) updates the view URL parameter and adjusts how much metadata is shown per item without changing the underlying data.
A print button triggers the browser’s print dialog for the page as currently filtered/sorted.
A "Browse more articles" link navigates to learn.html.
Notification banners appear after remove operations (success or failure), then can be dismissed by the user.
page_functionality.state_logic: This page is identified by reading_list.html and does not require any incoming URL parameters to function; it always loads the current user’s single ReadingList using getReadingList. URL query parameters are used strictly for client-side state and organization of the already-loaded data. Supported URL parameters include: sort (e.g., added_desc, added_asc, time_desc, time_asc, title_asc) controlling the order of items; topic (matching Article.topic enumeration values) limiting visible items by topic; min_read_time and max_read_time (numeric) constraining visible items by Article.estimated_reading_time_minutes; period_start and period_end (numeric) constraining items by Article.time_period_start_year/time_period_end_year; page and page_size (numeric) determining which slice of the filtered, sorted items is visible; and view (e.g., list, compact) controlling how much metadata is displayed per item. All of these parameters are optional; when absent, the page uses default client-side behaviors (such as sorting by added_at descending) without altering server data. After any change in filters, sorting, or pagination, the page updates the URL parameters to keep state shareable and restorable on reload. Mutating operations (removing an article) never rely on URL parameters for identification; they strictly use the article_id from the selected ReadingListItem when calling removeArticleFromReadingList, then call getReadingList again to update the page. Navigation to article_detail.html passes id={article_id} as a URL parameter, and navigation to learn.html includes no additional parameters by default from this page.

components.functionality: Initializes and maintains the core data for the page by calling getReadingList, exposes the ReadingList and associated items to child components, and re-fetches data after mutations such as deletions.
Provides controls for sorting, filtering, view mode switching, printing, and navigation to the Learn section. Reflects and mutates URL parameters to keep client-side state in sync.

Displays a concise summary of the current list state, such as total number of articles in the reading list, number of articles visible under current filters, and aggregated estimated reading time for the currently visible items.

Renders the list of reading list items after client-side sorting and filtering, showing article title (linked to article_detail.html?id={article_id}), topic, historical time period, estimated reading time, added_at, and any notes. Includes a remove control for each item and supports alternative view modes (e.g., list vs compact) based on URL state.
Controls which subset of the filtered items is shown by adjusting page and page_size URL parameters and computing item index ranges for the reading-list-items component.
Displays when getReadingList returns a ReadingList with no items or when filters result in zero visible items. Provides context-aware messaging and a prominent link to browse articles on learn.html.

Shows transient status messages such as success or error after removeArticleFromReadingList operations, and optionally load errors from getReadingList. Can also reflect state from URL parameters (e.g., a lastAction indicator) if used.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width grid similar to a 12-column system with a main content column and an internal 4-column image grid. Each image/caption acts like one column of four within the content width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a strict top-to-bottom, centered editorial layout. Above everything is the global red museum header (logo, utilities), which is not part of this component set. Below that, a single white main content card is centered within the viewport. Inside the card, the flow is: page title and short description at the top, followed by any notification banners, then a compact summary panel, then the reading list toolbar (sorting/filtering controls), followed by the main list of saved articles or the empty state, and finally the pagination controls at the bottom. All components are arranged in a single primary column; multi-column structure exists only inside components (e.g., within list items), mirroring the mockup’s content-first layout.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a strict top-to-bottom, centered editorial layout. Above everything is the global red museum header (logo, utilities), which is not part of this component set. Below that, a single white main content card is centered within the viewport. Inside the card, the flow is: page title and short description at the top, followed by any notification banners, then a compact summary panel, then the reading list toolbar (sorting/filtering controls), followed by the main list of saved articles or the empty state, and finally the pagination controls at the bottom. All components are arranged in a single primary column; multi-column structure exists only inside components (e.g., within list items), mirroring the mockup’s content-first layout.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height. The main content card sits below it and spans approximately 70–80% of the viewport width (max-width around a traditional content column, e.g., ~1040px), centered with light grey background margins to the left and right. Inside the card, top padding is around 24–32px and side padding also ~24–32px. The title, description, notifications, summary, and toolbar together take about 35–40% of the card’s height above the fold; the reading list items (or empty state) occupy the remaining ~50–55%, with pagination using the lower ~5–10%. Vertical gaps between sections are consistent with the 8px base scale, typically 24–32px between major blocks and 16px inside tighter clusters.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density follows a moderate editorial standard: text lines are comfortably readable (roughly 60–80 characters), with 1.4–1.6 line-height. Vertical spacing is present but not overly generous, ensuring a sense of information richness. The upper text region (title, description, summary) is fairly open to maintain readability, while the reading list items area is denser—stacked cards with modest gaps to fit several items within the viewport. Controls in the toolbar and pagination are compact with 4–8px vertical and 8–12px horizontal padding, and gutters between list items or internal elements adhere to the 8–12px spacing suggested by the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the red museum header down to the large red serif page title, then through the short descriptive text. Any notification banner directly beneath this draws quick attention without overpowering the title. From there, the eye naturally continues downward to the summary panel (showing counts and key context), then to the toolbar with sorting/filtering controls, and finally to the reading list items themselves, which form the main visual mass in the lower portion of the card. Pagination at the bottom provides a clear end to the content column. The flow is strictly vertical and calm, with no sidebars; secondary elements such as notifications and the data loader overlay are designed to be noticeable yet not visually aggressive, fitting the museum’s conservative, trustworthy tone.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a calm, editorial reading list within a museum website. At the top of the viewport, the familiar red museum header bar anchors the experience. Beneath it, centered on a light grey background, sits a single white content card with soft, 24–32px padding on all sides and a fixed-width column that feels like a printed page. At the top of this card, a large red serif title, “My Reading List,” spans the full content width, followed by a short explanatory paragraph in dark grey text that explains how saved articles work. Directly underneath, a slim notification banner appears whenever there are system messages. Below this, the summary panel gently highlights how many articles are saved and their basic breakdown, still within the same column. A narrow toolbar row then introduces sorting and filtering controls, aligning to the left and right edges of the content area. The bulk of the lower card is filled with vertically stacked article cards—each a simple, bordered block with a small thumbnail and text arranged according to a subtle internal grid. These items fill the lower half of the card, giving a sense of a curated reading archive. At the very bottom, centered pagination controls sit with a comfortable top margin, framing the end of the list and providing navigation to more items. When there are no saved articles, a quiet empty state occupies the area where the list would be, centered with more breathing room. A semi-transparent data loader overlay can temporarily cover the card content, and any notification banners subtly sit near the top of the card, ensuring that the overall experience feels like a structured, content-first museum page, consistent with the original mockup’s hierarchy and pacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears as a semi-transparent overlay within the bounds of the main content card, not across the entire viewport. When active, it covers the region from just inside the card’s top padding to just above its bottom padding, spanning the full internal width (100% of the card’s content area). The overlay slightly dims the underlying title, summary, toolbar, list, and pagination, indicating a loading state while preserving context. At the vertical and horizontal center of this overlay, a compact spinner or loading indicator is displayed, roughly 48–64px in diameter, with a short ‘Loading your reading list…’ label below it. There is at least 24px of empty space around this center cluster so it doesn’t feel cramped. The overlay respects the card’s rounded corners (if present) and aligns visually with the conservative, understated style—no heavy blur, just a tasteful, light veil over the content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a positioned overlay anchored to the main content card, covering all interactive elements (toolbar, list, pagination) while active. It does not push or shift any content; instead, it layers above the existing layout. The overlay scrolls together with the page—if the user scrolls, the loader remains fixed relative to the card contents until loading completes. Once dismissed, it disappears without any layout reflow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the main content card, the reading-list-toolbar sits as a full-width horizontal band, directly below the summary panel (or below the description text if the summary is absent), with approximately 24px of vertical space separating it from the element above. It respects the card’s internal padding, so its left and right edges align with the text column edges. The toolbar height is compact—around 48px total—with a subtle bottom border or tonal separation to echo the understated flat elements of the mockup. On larger screens, the left half of the toolbar hosts sorting controls (e.g., a dropdown and label) aligned to the left, while the right half hosts filtering or view options aligned to the right, creating a balanced horizontal tension without clutter. Controls are laid out on a loose internal grid: small gaps of 8–12px between buttons and inputs, and 4–8px vertical padding within each control. Below the toolbar, a 24px vertical gap separates it from the top edge of the reading list items (or the empty state).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the vertical flow of content, the toolbar is not sticky; it scrolls out of view as the user scrolls down the list. Its width always matches the card’s content width, and it reflows gracefully on smaller screens by stacking controls into two lines if necessary, while keeping its top and bottom separation consistent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-summary occupies a full-width block near the top of the content card, directly below the page description text and above the toolbar, separated by about 24px from both. It spans the same content width as the title and body, aligning flush with the card’s left and right padding. Visually, it appears as a subtle panel—either a very light grey background or a thin bordered box—that sets it apart from the plain white of the card, but with minimal visual weight to maintain the conservative, information-first feel. The panel’s height is modest, roughly 15–20% of the card’s height above the fold, with 16–24px of vertical padding inside. Within it, key metrics (e.g., ‘You have 12 saved articles’) might be set in a slightly larger serif or bold text on the left, with optional secondary stats arranged to the right or in a second line, following an internal 2–4 column grid. The overall effect is a quiet, structured summary that bridges the descriptive copy and the interactive toolbar below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The summary panel is a standard block element within the vertical flow. It expands vertically to accommodate content but does not scroll independently—any overflow is handled by wrapping text within the same panel. It remains static relative to the page; as the user scrolls, it moves off-screen with the rest of the upper content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-items component forms the main content mass in the lower portion of the card. It starts directly below the toolbar, with a 24px vertical gap separating the toolbar’s bottom edge from the top of the first item. The component spans the full available content width within the card (100% of the inner column), running from left to right padding. Within this region, individual article entries are presented as vertically stacked cards, each occupying the full width of the column, with a 16–24px vertical gap between them. Each card is about 96–144px tall, depending on text length, and uses a subtle internal grid reminiscent of the mockup’s 4-column system: for example, a narrow left column (about 1/4 of the card width) for a small thumbnail or icon, and a wider right area (about 3/4) for title, metadata, and short description. Thin grey borders or very soft drop-shadows emphasize separation while keeping a flat, traditional look. The list extends downward to fill roughly the lower 50–60% of the card’s height above the fold, with enough density to see 3–6 items at a time on a typical laptop screen. Below the last item, a 24–32px gap leads into the pagination controls, providing a clear visual break between the list and navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The list is fully integrated into the page scroll—there is no internal scrolling container. As the user scrolls the page, more items come into view in a continuous vertical flow. If pagination is used, changing pages replaces the items within this same vertical region without altering the position of elements above or below. The width of individual item cards remains tied to the card’s column; on smaller screens, internal grids collapse gracefully (thumbnail stacking above text) while preserving the vertical order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-pagination sits at the very bottom of the main content card’s content stack, directly below the reading list items—or, when present, below the empty state component. It is centered horizontally within the card, with its controls (page numbers, next/previous arrows) grouped into a compact row that occupies about 25–40% of the card’s width on larger screens. A vertical gap of 24–32px separates it from the content above, ensuring a clear end to the list. The height of the pagination area is relatively small, around 48–56px, with controls vertically centered within this space. Individual page buttons follow the design’s compact padding (4–8px vertical, 8–12px horizontal) and align to a subtle baseline grid. The overall look is understated—thin borders, flat buttons, and perhaps a hint of the brand red used only to highlight the current page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The pagination controls are part of the normal vertical document flow and scroll with the page. They remain anchored to the bottom of the card’s content and do not stick to the viewport. On narrower viewports, buttons may wrap to two lines, but the group stays centered with the same top margin from the list or empty state above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-empty-state occupies exactly the same spatial region that the reading list items would use, ensuring consistency in layout whether the user has saved articles or not. It appears directly beneath the toolbar (and summary and notifications above), with a 24px gap from the toolbar’s bottom edge. The empty state content is centered horizontally within the card and vertically padded to feel calm and intentional: there is 48–64px of vertical padding above and below the main message within the card’s content area, making it feel like a purposeful panel rather than a gap. The layout typically includes a simple illustrative icon or subtle line-art graphic at the top center (roughly 64–96px tall), followed by a short headline in serif type, then a concise explanatory sentence and a primary action button (e.g., “Browse Learn articles”). The text lines are limited in width (no more than 60% of the content column), and the entire stack is center-aligned, mirroring the museum’s informational tone while inviting exploration. Pagination controls are either hidden when this state is active or pushed down with a generous gap (32px) if they must remain visible, ensuring there’s no confusion about the lack of results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The empty state replaces the list items block; only one of these (list or empty) is visible at a time. It sits in the normal vertical flow and scrolls with the page, although in typical cases it fits comfortably within a single viewport along with the title and upper elements. It does not introduce any independent scrolling areas, and its centered content adapts responsively, narrowing on smaller screens while keeping the vertical rhythm intact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-notifications component appears as one or more horizontal banners within the main content card, positioned near the top of the card’s content area so messages feel timely but not dominant. It sits directly below the page title and introductory paragraph, with about 16–24px of vertical space separating it from the text above. The banner spans the full content width inside the card’s padding and has a modest height—typically 48–64px for a single-line message, expanding as needed for longer text. Background color is muted but distinct (e.g., soft yellow or pale red for warnings/errors), consistent with the museum’s conservative palette. Left-aligned text communicates the message, while any dismiss or action buttons are right-aligned, adhering to the same compact padding (4–8px vertical, 8–12px horizontal). Multiple notifications, if present, stack vertically with 8–12px gaps, forming a tidy column before the summary panel. A 24px gap below the notification block separates it from the next element (usually the summary or toolbar), maintaining the established vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Notifications are inline elements that occupy space in the vertical flow; when they appear, they push the summary, toolbar, and list downward. They scroll away with the rest of the content and are not fixed to the viewport. If a notification is dismissible, it collapses smoothly, closing the gap without disturbing the alignment of elements above or below beyond the necessary vertical shift.

name: Timeline
title: Cheese History Timeline
description: Interactive chronological timeline of cheese-related events with an optional personal view showing pinned events.

core_features: Load global timeline configuration (minimum/maximum years and era presets) using getTimelineConfig to initialize the era range controls.
Display an interactive, scrollable timeline of cheese-related events for a selected year range using listTimelineEvents, with optional region and category filters.
Allow users to adjust the visible era via an era range slider and optional era presets, updating the displayed events and the URL parameters (start_year, end_year, preset_id).
Provide zoom controls that change the visual scale of the timeline (zooming in/out) without changing the underlying data set unless the visible range is explicitly adjusted.
Show event detail popovers when a timeline event is selected, using data from the already-loaded TimelineEvent objects.
Allow users to pin an event from its popover to their personal timeline by calling pinTimelineEvent and reflecting success/failure in the UI.
Offer a mode toggle between the full timeline (mode=all) and the personal pinned timeline (mode=my), synchronized with the URL parameter mode.
Display a "My Timeline" view that shows only pinned events returned by getPinnedTimelineEvents in chronological order, optionally constrained by the current era range and filters on the page.
