user_workflows: View default timeline from navigation: 1) User clicks the "Timeline" link in the site header or a direct link without extra parameters. 2) Page initializes and timeline-config-loader calls getTimelineConfig. 3) URL-state-synchronizer reads URL parameters (if any); if none are present, era-range-controls are initialized to a default based on getTimelineConfig (for example the full min_year to max_year). 4) all-events-timeline-view calls listTimelineEvents with the resolved start_year, end_year, and any filter parameters (region, category) from the URL. 5) Events are rendered on the interactive timeline, with zoom and scrolling enabled.

Open timeline with a preset era from another page: 1) User clicks a link such as "Cheese Through Time" on index.html which navigates to timeline.html with preset_id in the URL (and no explicit start_year/end_year). 2) On load, timeline-config-loader calls getTimelineConfig and finds the era preset whose id matches preset_id. 3) The selected preset's start_year and end_year become the active range; URL-state-synchronizer may add/confirm start_year and end_year parameters based on that preset. 4) all-events-timeline-view calls listTimelineEvents using this range (plus optional region/category if present). 5) The slider and preset selector reflect the chosen preset, and events in this era are shown.

View timeline with explicit year range from another page: 1) User follows a link from about.html that includes start_year and end_year. 2) On load, URL-state-synchronizer reads these parameters; preset_id is ignored if not provided. 3) timeline-config-loader still calls getTimelineConfig to know global bounds, but uses the URL start_year/end_year as the active range. 4) all-events-timeline-view calls listTimelineEvents with that range and renders events. 5) The era-range-controls slider reflects the explicit years and no preset is marked as selected if none matches exactly.

Adjust visible era via slider or preset: 1) User drags the era-range-controls slider handles to new start and end years. 2) The component updates internal state, then notifies url-state-synchronizer to update the URL (start_year and end_year) and clear preset_id if the range does not match a known preset. 3) all-events-timeline-view re-calls listTimelineEvents with the updated range and current filters. 4) The timeline re-renders with events for the new era. 5) Alternatively, if user selects an era preset (by label) from a dropdown or buttons, era-range-controls sets the range to the preset’s start_year/end_year and sets preset_id in the URL; listTimelineEvents is called with that range, and the slider updates accordingly.

Filter by region and category: 1) User changes region and/or category in timeline-filter-controls. 2) The component updates region and category URL parameters via url-state-synchronizer. 3) all-events-timeline-view calls listTimelineEvents again with the same start_year/end_year but updated region and category filter values. 4) The timeline refreshes with only matching events. 5) In mode=my, pinned-timeline-view still uses getPinnedTimelineEvents (which has no filter parameters) but applies the same region and category filters client-side when deciding which pinned events to display.

Zoom and scroll within the timeline: 1) User clicks zoom-in or zoom-out buttons in timeline-zoom-controls. 2) The component adjusts a zoom factor in all-events-timeline-view or my-timeline-view, changing spacing and density of event markers without altering the listTimelineEvents query parameters. 3) User scrolls horizontally along the timeline; this changes the visible viewport over the already-loaded events but does not change the current start_year/end_year unless the user explicitly changes the era range via the dedicated controls.

View event details and pin to personal timeline: 1) User clicks on an event marker in all-events-timeline-view. 2) The component sets the selected TimelineEvent in state and opens timeline-event-popover populated with that event’s data. 3) Inside the popover, user clicks the "Pin to My Timeline" action. 4) timeline-event-popover calls pinTimelineEvent with the event_id. 5) On success=true, the UI shows confirmation, optionally triggers my-timeline-view to refresh its data on next activation, and can offer a direct link or button to switch to mode=my (which updates the URL to mode=my). 6) On error or success=false, an appropriate error or retry option is shown.

Switch to personal pinned timeline (My Timeline): 1) From any state in mode=all, user clicks the "My Timeline" tab in timeline-mode-toggle. 2) url-state-synchronizer updates the URL mode parameter to my, preserving existing range and filter parameters where appropriate. 3) my-timeline-view becomes the active view and calls getPinnedTimelineEvents. 4) The component sorts the returned events chronologically if needed (or uses the order provided) and displays them along a timeline layout. 5) era-range-controls and timeline-filter-controls still show current settings; my-timeline-view applies these as client-side filters on the pinned events. 6) If the user switches back to "All Events", mode is set to all in the URL, and the last-used range and filters remain, with all-events-timeline-view active again.

Return directly to My Timeline via URL: 1) User or system navigates to timeline.html with mode=my (and optional range/filter parameters). 2) On load, URL-state-synchronizer sets mode to my. 3) timeline-config-loader initializes era bounds as usual. 4) my-timeline-view calls getPinnedTimelineEvents and displays the pinned events, with client-side filtering by the URL’s start_year/end_year/region/category if present.

Update filters or range while in My Timeline: 1) In mode=my, user adjusts era-range-controls or timeline-filter-controls. 2) URL parameters for start_year, end_year, preset_id, region, and category are updated but mode remains my. 3) my-timeline-view does not re-call getPinnedTimelineEvents (unless a refresh is requested) but instead re-applies the updated range and filters to the already-loaded pinned events and re-renders the view.
interactions: Click "Timeline" in the global header or a contextual link on other pages to navigate to timeline.html with optional start_year, end_year, preset_id, and mode parameters set in the URL.
On page load, read URL parameters (start_year, end_year, preset_id, mode, region, category) and initialize internal state and controls to match them.

Call getTimelineConfig immediately after load to obtain min_year, max_year, and available era_presets; use these to configure the era-range-controls and, when preset_id is present, determine the active range.

When user adjusts the era slider start or end handles, update internal start_year/end_year values, propagate these to the URL, clear preset_id if no longer matching a known preset, and call listTimelineEvents with the new range if mode=all.

When user selects an era preset, set preset_id in the URL and update start_year/end_year to match that preset, then reload the events for that range via listTimelineEvents in mode=all or re-filter pinned events in mode=my.

When user changes region or category in timeline-filter-controls, update corresponding URL parameters and trigger either a new listTimelineEvents call (mode=all) or a client-side re-filter of pinned events (mode=my).
When user clicks zoom controls, adjust a zoom factor used by the active timeline view (all-events-timeline-view or my-timeline-view) to change visual scaling without additional API calls.
When user clicks on a timeline event marker, set that event as the selected TimelineEvent and open the timeline-event-popover component.

Within timeline-event-popover, clicking the "Pin to My Timeline" action calls pinTimelineEvent with the event_id; the response is used to show confirmation or error and to mark the event as pinned in the UI.

Clicking the "My Timeline" tab triggers url-state-synchronizer to change mode to my in the URL and switch the active view to my-timeline-view, which calls getPinnedTimelineEvents to load pinned events.

Clicking the "All Events" tab changes mode back to all in the URL and re-activates all-events-timeline-view, which uses existing URL parameters to call listTimelineEvents and display all matching events.

Using browser back/forward navigation triggers url-state-synchronizer to re-read URL parameters and update mode, range, filters, and the active timeline view accordingly, ensuring the UI matches the URL at all times.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully derived from URL parameters and API responses, avoiding localStorage or other client-side persistence. The primary URL parameters are: (1) mode: determines which view is active. Accepted values are 'all' (default) and 'my'. If mode is missing or invalid, the page assumes mode=all. When the user clicks 'All Events' or 'My Timeline' tabs, the mode parameter is updated via navigation or history API. (2) preset_id: identifies a predefined era from getTimelineConfig. On load, if preset_id is present, the page waits for getTimelineConfig, then finds the matching preset and uses its start_year and end_year as the active range. When preset_id is present, it takes precedence over any start_year and end_year passed in the URL. Adjusting the slider to a non-preset range clears preset_id in the URL. Selecting a preset sets preset_id and updates the range. (3) start_year and end_year: define the currently visible time window. These are strings in the URL but are converted to numbers for API calls. If preset_id is not present, start_year and end_year parameters are used to initialize the range. If they are missing, the page defaults to a range derived from getTimelineConfig (for example min_year to max_year) but this default is not hardcoded: it always uses the API response. Any change to the slider or preset updates start_year and end_year in the URL. listTimelineEvents always uses these values in mode=all. In mode=my, they are used only for client-side filtering of pinned events. (4) region and category: used as optional filters mapped directly to the listTimelineEvents parameters. When present in the URL, they determine the initial filter state. Changing filters updates these parameters and triggers either a fresh listTimelineEvents call (mode=all) or a re-filtering of the pinned events (mode=my). (5) Interactions and API calls: In mode=all, all-events-timeline-view calls listTimelineEvents with start_year, end_year, region, and category resolved from the URL and from getTimelineConfig (when needed). In mode=my, my-timeline-view calls getPinnedTimelineEvents without parameters; all filtering by start_year/end_year/region/category is done client-side based on URL parameters. pinTimelineEvent does not introduce new URL parameters; instead, changes are reflected by merging the new pinned event into the client-side pinned list or ensuring that getPinnedTimelineEvents will reflect it the next time it is called. The URL-state-synchronizer component is responsible for reading parameters on initialization, updating the URL when user actions change mode/range/filters, and reacting to browser navigation events so that the visual state always matches the encoded URL state.

components.functionality: Central controller that reads and writes URL query parameters (mode, preset_id, start_year, end_year, region, category) and exposes them to other components as the single source of truth for page state. It ensures that changes to mode, era range, or filters update the URL, and that browser back/forward navigation restores the corresponding state.

On page initialization, calls getTimelineConfig to retrieve min_year, max_year, and era_presets. It resolves the active era range by combining this configuration with URL parameters: if preset_id is present, it selects the corresponding preset's range; otherwise, it uses start_year and end_year from the URL if available, or falls back to a range derived from min_year and max_year. It then passes the resolved configuration and active range to era-range-controls and the active timeline view.

Provides two tabs or buttons ('All Events' and 'My Timeline') that switch between the full timeline and the user's pinned timeline. It reflects the current mode based on the URL parameter and, when a tab is clicked, updates the mode in the URL while preserving other parameters such as start_year, end_year, region, and category.

Displays controls for selecting the visible time range, including a year-range slider and an era preset selector. It uses min_year, max_year, and era_presets from getTimelineConfig and the current start_year, end_year, and preset_id from the URL. User interactions update the active range and preset selection and propagate corresponding changes to the URL; in mode=all these also trigger listTimelineEvents reloads, while in mode=my they cause my-timeline-view to re-filter pinned events.

Manages selection of region and category filters for events. It initializes its state from URL parameters region and category, and when the user changes these filters, it updates the URL and triggers either a new listTimelineEvents call (for mode=all) or a client-side re-filtering of pinned events (for mode=my).

Provides zoom-in and zoom-out controls (and optionally a zoom level indicator) that adjust the visual scale of the currently active timeline view. It does not change URL parameters or trigger new API calls; instead, it updates a zoom factor used by the active timeline view to render events with more or less spacing.

Active when mode=all. It calls listTimelineEvents with start_year, end_year, region, and category obtained from URL-state-synchronizer and era-range-controls, and then renders the returned TimelineEvent objects on an interactive, scrollable timeline. It listens to zoom controls for visual scaling and to user clicks on event markers to open the timeline-event-popover.

Active when mode=my. It calls getPinnedTimelineEvents to retrieve the user's pinned events, each combining PinnedTimelineEvent and TimelineEvent data. It sorts them chronologically if needed and displays them along a timeline layout. It applies client-side filtering based on the current URL parameters (start_year, end_year, region, category) and responds to zoom controls. It updates its display when a new event is pinned if requested by other components.

Displays detailed information for a selected timeline event when a user clicks an event marker in either timeline view. It receives a TimelineEvent object from the caller and shows its properties. It provides a 'Pin to My Timeline' action, which calls pinTimelineEvent with the event_id; based on the response, it updates its UI to reflect success or error and can notify my-timeline-view to refresh pinned events or instruct url-state-synchronizer to switch mode to my.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width grid similar to a 12-column system with a main content column and an internal 4-column image grid. Each image/caption acts like one column of four within the content width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow. Above everything sits the existing global museum header (logo, language switcher, search) in a red bar. Below that, a single centered white content card contains the entire Timeline experience. Inside this card, the structure is: (1) a full-width red page title (“Timeline”) at the top left, (2) a short introductory paragraph explaining the timeline, (3) a full-width band of timeline controls arranged horizontally (mode toggle, era range, filters, zoom), behaving like the mockup’s multi-column image row, and (4) the main interactive timeline view filling the remaining vertical space. Only one timeline view (all events or personal) is visible at a time, with an event-detail popover overlaying it on interaction.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow. Above everything sits the existing global museum header (logo, language switcher, search) in a red bar. Below that, a single centered white content card contains the entire Timeline experience. Inside this card, the structure is: (1) a full-width red page title (“Timeline”) at the top left, (2) a short introductory paragraph explaining the timeline, (3) a full-width band of timeline controls arranged horizontally (mode toggle, era range, filters, zoom), behaving like the mockup’s multi-column image row, and (4) the main interactive timeline view filling the remaining vertical space. Only one timeline view (all events or personal) is visible at a time, with an event-detail popover overlaying it on interaction.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header keeps about 15–20% of the initial viewport height. The main content card is centered and spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, with light grey margins on both sides. Inside the card, the top 25–35% of its height is dedicated to the page title, intro text, and the timeline control band. The remaining 65–75% is given to the timeline view itself, which runs as a full-width band across the card. Horizontally, the control band uses an internal 4-column grid: era range, filters, and zoom controls share space across those four virtual columns, while the mode toggle is aligned toward the top-right in the header area to visually echo a utility control.
layout_strategies.content_density: Typography follows moderate, readable density: the title and intro paragraph have comfortable line length and 24–32px vertical separation. The controls band is denser, similar to the mockup’s image row: controls sit fairly close together with 8–12px gutters, using thin boundaries and compact padding to conserve vertical space. The timeline view itself can be denser horizontally (events packed along the axis) but maintains sufficient vertical breathing room above and below the axis line so the card doesn’t feel cramped. Overall the page feels informational and content-heavy but not overloaded, with clear grouping of text, controls, and data visualization.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first catches the red global brand bar at the very top, then moves to the left-aligned red page title within the white card. From there, attention is drawn to the small intro paragraph directly beneath. Next, the eye naturally travels down into the horizontal controls band, scanning from left (era range controls) through the middle (filter controls) towards the right (zoom controls), with the mode toggle noticeable in the upper-right of the card’s header area. Once controls are understood, the gaze drops into the main timeline band, where the events and axis dominate. When an event is selected, a popover appears just above or over the timeline at that point, briefly pulling focus without breaking the vertical reading flow. A global scroll-to-top arrow remains a peripheral, tertiary element at the lower right edge of the viewport, consistent with the mockup.

layout.overall_description: The Timeline page sits beneath the museum’s familiar red header bar with logo, language options, and search stretching across the top 15–20% of the viewport. Centered below this is a large, white, card-like panel against a light grey background, occupying about three quarters of the viewport width. Inside the card, a bold red “Timeline” heading anchors the top left, followed by a brief explanatory paragraph set in a traditional serif face, with comfortable spacing that suggests an editorial article rather than an app dashboard. Aligned to the right of this header zone is a pill-style mode toggle that lets visitors switch between the full historical view and their personal pinned events. Beneath the text, a full-width control band spans the card, divided visually into a four-column rhythm: era range selection on the left, then event-type filters, and finally zoom controls toward the right, all separated by modest 8–12px gaps and subtle grey delineation. Below this band, the main timeline extends across the full width of the card, taking up most of the remaining height and inviting horizontal exploration while still staying within the calm editorial frame. Only one timeline—either all events or “My Timeline”—is visible at a time, occupying the same visual slot. When a visitor clicks an event, a small white popover with a thin grey border appears just above the chosen point, aligned to it with a small pointer, overlaying the timeline but not the header or global navigation. The whole composition feels like a well-structured page in a museum catalog: content-first, linear, and calm, with interactivity nested inside a traditional, trustworthy layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This routing-controller component has no direct visual representation. It resides at the page or app shell level, managing URL parameters and synchronization for the Timeline card. It does not occupy any visible space and does not alter the fixed-width centered layout of the content card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, structural component; effectively positioned off-canvas with no rendered footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader component is also non-visual. It sits logically behind the main content card, loading configuration and data before the Timeline card’s visible elements (title, controls, timeline view) appear. While it affects when the contents render (e.g., possibly showing a generic loading state in the card), it does not occupy any dedicated visible region itself.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, structural component; does not claim layout space but governs readiness of child components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mode toggle appears inside the white content card, in the upper right of the card’s header area. Horizontally, it is aligned flush with the right edge of the card’s content column (about 70–80% of viewport width), and vertically it sits either on the same baseline as the page title or just below it with a 8–16px gap, depending on typographic constraints. The control itself is a compact pill or segmented switch approximately 120–200px wide, with 4–8px vertical padding and 8–12px horizontal padding per option, following the compact pill sizing from the mockup. It does not span the full width; instead, it occupies roughly one of the internal 4 columns in the top row, balancing the left-aligned title. There is a 24–32px vertical gap between the mode toggle/title header area and the control band below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the rest of the card content (not sticky). Remains anchored to the top-right corner of the card’s content area and maintains fixed distance from top and right card padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The era range controls sit at the far left of the horizontal control band directly below the introductory paragraph. The top edge of this band is about 24–32px under the paragraph’s baseline, reinforcing the clear section separation from the text. The era controls align with the card’s left content padding and occupy roughly the leftmost 25–35% of the card’s width—equivalent to the first of four internal grid columns. If implemented as a slider, the track runs horizontally within this area; if dropdowns, two dropdowns align side by side. Vertically, the controls band is about 48–64px tall, with 8–12px internal vertical padding and 8–12px horizontal gutters between individual inputs. The bottom of this band transitions directly into the top of the timeline view, with a 16–24px gap separating the controls from the timeline axis, giving the band a compact, functional feeling similar to the image row in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page as part of the control band. The component is left-anchored within the band, maintaining consistent distance from the card’s left padding and sharing horizontal space with the filter and zoom controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timeline filter controls appear in the central portion of the same horizontal control band, immediately to the right of the era range controls. They occupy approximately the middle 30–40% of the card’s width, corresponding to the second and third internal grid columns. If multiple filter pills or checkboxes are used, they line up horizontally with small 8–12px gaps between them, wrapping to a second line only if necessary but remaining visually grouped as a central cluster. The vertical height aligns with the era range area so the band has a unified top and bottom edge. There is a subtle 8–12px horizontal gap between the rightmost era control and the leftmost filter control, visually suggesting separate but related groups. The entire filter area is left-aligned within its central region, not centered on the card, preserving the editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the scrollable control band, non-sticky. It flexes horizontally within the middle columns of the card’s grid; on narrower viewports, it can stack within the band but should preserve its alignment underneath the era controls and before the zoom controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The zoom controls are placed at the right end of the control band, mirroring the mockup’s tendency to place utilities toward the right side. They align flush with the card’s right content padding and occupy roughly the rightmost 20–25% of the card’s width—approximately the fourth internal grid column. The controls themselves are compact buttons or a small segmented control (e.g., plus/minus icons or discrete zoom levels), each with 4–8px vertical and 8–12px horizontal padding, arranged horizontally. There is a small 8–12px horizontal gap between the leftmost zoom control and the rightmost filter control. The vertical size of the zoom controls matches the height of the other controls in the band, maintaining a consistent band height. This cluster visually terminates the control band on the right, helping the user’s eye move from left to right across all controls before dropping down to the timeline.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the card. Right-anchored within the control band, maintaining fixed spacing to the card’s right padding even as the central area flexes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The all-events timeline view occupies the entire width of the content card beneath the control band, forming a large horizontal band that dominates the lower 60–70% of the card’s height within the initial viewport. The top edge of the timeline’s interactive area sits about 16–24px below the bottom of the controls band, creating a clear but modest separation. The main timeline axis runs horizontally across nearly the full width of the card, with 24–32px left/right padding matching the card’s content padding. Events are plotted along this axis, and any event markers or cards sit above or below the axis while staying inside this band. Vertical space is generous enough that labels and markers don’t feel cramped but still more compact than the textual header, reflecting an information-dense data area. As the user scrolls, this timeline band moves with the page rather than remaining fixed. When the mode toggle is set to “All events,” this view is fully visible and the “My timeline” view is hidden; both views share the same spatial container and never appear simultaneously.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page as a large, block-level section. It is not sticky or floating; it simply fills the entire width of the card below the controls, extending vertically as needed for the timeline content and any internal scrolling or zooming behaviors.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The personal “My Timeline” view occupies the exact same spatial slot as the all-events view: a full-width band beneath the control band, separated by a 16–24px gap from the controls, and filling the lower 60–70% of the card’s height on initial load. The horizontal axis and card padding mirror the all-events view, maintaining visual consistency. The difference lies in content density: because only pinned events are shown, markers may appear more spaced out, but they still conform to the same axis position, margins, and vertical breathing room. When the mode toggle is switched to this mode, the all-events view is completely hidden and replaced with this personal view, so the user experiences a simple content swap within an unchanged layout. This preserves the editorial linear flow—title, intro text, controls, then a single timeline band—regardless of the mode.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the all-events view, sharing its block-level area and scrolling behavior. It is not sticky; it scrolls as part of the card’s main content. Visibility is controlled by the mode toggle state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event popover is a small, card-like overlay that appears within the boundaries of the main content card, anchored to the specific event marker the user selects on either timeline view. It floats above the timeline band, typically positioned just above the selected marker so that its pointer or small triangular notch can visually connect to the event. Horizontally, its left edge aligns such that the popover is centered over the event marker when possible; if near the card’s edges, it shifts inward to avoid overflowing beyond the card’s 70–80% width. The popover itself is roughly 240–360px wide with content-aware height, uses the same white background as the card, and features a thin grey border and 16–24px internal padding—echoing the aesthetic of the main card but at a smaller scale. It never overlaps the global header; if the event is high on the screen, the popover appears slightly lower to stay within the visible card region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Floating overlay within the card’s stacking context. It is positioned absolutely relative to the timeline container, following the event marker as the user scrolls or pans within the timeline. It does not affect the layout of other components and disappears when dismissed or when another event is selected.

name: Shop
title: Shop
description: Shop page for viewing museum merchandise categories and browsing, filtering, and adding cheese-related books to the cart.

core_features: Load and display available shop categories using getShopCategories, highlighting the active 'books' category based on the URL parameter.
When the active category is 'books', load available book filter options (format, topic, rating thresholds, price ranges) using getBookFilterOptions and render them as interactive filters.
Read URL parameters (category, topic, format, rating_min, max_price, page, sort_by) to initialize current filter and pagination state for the book list.
List book products for the active filters, sort and pagination using listBooks, and display key details such as name, price, and rating.
Allow users to change filters (format, topic, rating_min, max_price) and sorting, updating the URL parameters and reloading the book list accordingly via listBooks.
Allow users to add one or more copies of a book directly from the list by calling addProductToCart from each product card.
Provide a persistent cart link/icon that navigates to cart.html and updates basic cart indicators (such as item count or total) using the response from addProductToCart calls.
user_workflows: Browse books from a category link: 1) User clicks a 'Shop' link or a deep link such as shop.html?category=books&topic=... from index.html or learn.html; 2) Shop page reads the URL parameters, normalizes category to 'books' if applicable, and calls getShopCategories; 3) For category='books', page calls getBookFilterOptions, derives initial filter state from URL parameters, then calls listBooks with the derived filters, sort_by, page, and page_size; 4) User scrolls through the results, potentially navigating between pages using pagination controls.

Refine book results using filters: 1) User adjusts one or more filters in the book filter panel (format, topic, rating minimum, or maximum price); 2) Filter component updates the corresponding URL parameters (removing parameters for cleared filters and resetting page to the first page), then triggers a re-fetch by calling listBooks with the new filters; 3) Product grid updates to reflect the new filtered list; 4) Applied filters bar updates its chips to match the currently active filters as defined by the URL parameters.

Sort and paginate book list: 1) User selects a sort option in the sort bar (for example, by price or rating); 2) Sort component updates the sort_by URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and triggers a listBooks call with the updated sort_by and current filters; 3) For pagination, user clicks next/previous or a specific page number; 4) Pagination component updates the page URL parameter and calls listBooks with the updated page and current filters/sort_by; 5) Product grid and results summary update using the listBooks response (total_count, page, page_size, products).

Add books to cart from the list: 1) From a product card, user optionally adjusts quantity and clicks an 'Add to cart' action; 2) The card or associated add-to-cart component calls addProductToCart with product_id and quantity; 3) On success, the component receives the updated CartItem and Cart in the response and triggers a UI update event; 4) Cart link/icon component listens for this event and updates its cart indicator (for example, showing the number of items based on cart.items length or the subtotal based on cart.subtotal) without using any additional interfaces; 5) User may repeat this for multiple books; 6) User can click the cart icon/link at any time to navigate to cart.html.

Open the cart to review purchases: 1) After adding items, user clicks the cart link/icon; 2) Browser navigates to cart.html (without passing additional parameters from this page by default, unless globally configured); 3) cart.html is responsible for full cart display and checkout using its own interfaces (outside this page’s scope).
interactions: Category selection: Clicking a category in the category navigation updates the category URL parameter and reloads the Shop page state; when 'books' is selected, the book filter panel and book list are activated and listBooks is called with the current filters.

Filter changes: Changing any filter control (format, topic, rating threshold, or maximum price) updates the corresponding URL parameters, resets pagination to the first page, and triggers a new listBooks call with the updated filters.

Clearing filters: Clicking a clear/remove action on an individual filter chip removes the corresponding URL parameter and re-fetches the book list with the reduced filter set; clicking a global 'Clear filters' action (if implemented) removes all book-related filter URL parameters and re-fetches with only the category parameter.
Sorting: Changing the sort option updates the sort_by URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and triggers listBooks with the current filters and new sort_by value.

Pagination: Clicking next/previous or a specific page button updates the page URL parameter and re-calls listBooks with the current filters and sort_by; the pagination UI is driven by listBooks.total_count, listBooks.page, and listBooks.page_size.

Add to cart: On each product card, clicking the add-to-cart button triggers addProductToCart with the selected product_id and quantity; success responses update local cart-related UI (such as the cart indicator and per-product feedback), and failure responses show the returned message to the user.
Cart navigation: Clicking the cart link or icon triggers a normal navigation to cart.html, allowing the user to review the full cart and proceed to checkout.

Deep-linked filter initialization: When the page is loaded with topic, format, rating_min, or max_price in the URL (typically from index.html or learn.html), the filter panel initializes its controls to match those parameters after validating them against getBookFilterOptions, and listBooks is called with those filters already applied.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Shop page is controlled entirely by URL query parameters and the responses from the assigned interfaces. Recognized query parameters are: category, topic, format, rating_min, max_price, page, and sort_by. On initial load, the page reads these parameters and constructs an internal state object representing the current category selection and book filters. If the category parameter is absent or does not match an available shop category, the page normalizes it to a default category (intended to be 'books') and may update the URL (for example, using history.replaceState) so that category is always explicit. When the active category is 'books', the page calls getBookFilterOptions and listBooks. Filter-related URL parameters (topic, format, rating_min, max_price) are validated against the options returned by getBookFilterOptions; invalid or unsupported values are ignored and not passed into listBooks. For rating_min and max_price, string parameter values are converted to numbers before being used in filters. The filters object passed to listBooks includes category and any validated filter parameters. The sort_by and page parameters, if present, are passed through directly to listBooks; if absent, implementation-defined defaults are used but not hardcoded in this specification. Whenever the user changes category, filters, sort, or pagination via UI interactions, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters (adding, modifying, or removing them as needed), optionally resets the page parameter to the first page when filters or sort_by change, and then re-calls listBooks with the updated state. The URL is the single source of truth for current category and filter state, and all components read from this unified state rather than maintaining independent copies. Cart state is not stored in the URL; instead, after each addProductToCart call, any cart-related UI derives its state directly from the Cart and CartItem data returned by that call.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL state parsing, and re-fetching of book listings and filter options. On page load, parses category, topic, format, rating_min, max_price, page, and sort_by from the URL. Calls getShopCategories to retrieve category metadata and determines the active category. If the active category is 'books', calls getBookFilterOptions to retrieve filter options, validates current URL filter values against those options, builds a filters object, and then calls listBooks with filters, sort_by, page, and page_size. Listens for events from child components (category selection, filter changes, sort changes, pagination, add-to-cart success) to update URL parameters and trigger the appropriate API calls. Distributes the resulting Product list and pagination data, as well as the current effective filter state, to the relevant child components.

Displays the list of shop categories (e.g., books, apparel) obtained from getShopCategories and highlights the currently active category based on the URL state. For each category code returned, renders an interactive item that, when selected, updates the category URL parameter and notifies the page controller. When the user selects the 'books' category, triggers the book-specific workflow (filter loading and book listing). Categories other than 'books' can either navigate to other relevant pages or be handled as non-book categories depending on the broader site architecture, but this component itself only updates the category parameter and emits a category change event.

Visible only when the active category is 'books'. Uses getBookFilterOptions to render available filter controls: formats, topics, rating thresholds, and price ranges. Initializes its selected values from the current URL parameters (topic, format, rating_min, max_price) after validating that each parameter matches an available option. When the user changes a filter (selects a different format, toggles a topic, picks a rating threshold, or chooses a price range / manually edits a maximum price), the component computes the new filter state, updates or removes the corresponding URL parameters, resets pagination to the first page, and emits a filters change event so the page controller can call listBooks with the new filters. It does not maintain independent filter state beyond what is reflected in the URL.

Summarizes the currently active book filters as removable chips or tags generated directly from the URL-driven filter state (topic, format, rating_min, max_price) as interpreted through getBookFilterOptions. For each active filter, shows a chip with a remove action. Removing a chip updates the related URL parameter (removing it) and emits a filters change event so that the page controller can call listBooks with the updated filters. May also provide a global clear-all action that removes all book filter URL parameters and triggers a fresh listBooks call.

Provides sorting controls for the book list, such as a dropdown of sort options. Initializes its selected sort value from the sort_by URL parameter, if present, or from an implementation-defined default. When the user selects a different sort option, the component updates the sort_by URL parameter, resets the page parameter to the first page, and emits a sort change event so that the page controller can re-call listBooks with the existing filters and new sort_by.

Displays summary information about the current book results, such as the total number of books found and the current page range, based on the latest listBooks response. It binds to total_count, page, and page_size from listBooks to derive human-readable information (for example, displaying the range of items currently being viewed) but does not modify any state itself.

Renders the list of book products returned by listBooks as a grid or list of product cards. For each Product entity, shows key information such as name, price, rating, and availability where exposed by the Product model. Each card embeds an add-to-cart component that allows users to add that book to the cart. When listBooks returns an empty products array, the grid can show a no-results state based on the applied filters.

Encapsulates the add-to-cart interaction for a single book within the product grid. Accepts a Product entity and an initial quantity (typically 1, but configurable). When the user submits the add-to-cart action (e.g., clicking a button), the component calls addProductToCart with the associated product_id and selected quantity. On success, it receives the updated CartItem and Cart from the interface and emits an add-to-cart-success event containing this data. The component may also provide basic per-item feedback (e.g., a success status or error message) using the message field from the addProductToCart response, without persisting any data beyond the current view state.

Controls navigation between pages of book results. Binds to listBooks.total_count, listBooks.page, and listBooks.page_size to compute available pages and enabled/disabled state for previous/next controls. When the user selects a page or clicks previous/next, it updates the page URL parameter and emits a page change event so the page controller can call listBooks with the new page and the existing filters and sort_by.

Provides a cart icon or link that navigates to cart.html. Initially, it can render based on a neutral state, since this page has no interface to load the existing cart; after any successful addProductToCart call, it updates its visual indicators (such as item count or subtotal) using the Cart and CartItem data from the latest add-to-cart response. It does not directly modify the cart; it only reflects the most recent cart state known to this page and provides navigation to the dedicated cart page.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width grid similar to a 12-column system with a main content column and an internal 4-column image grid. Each image/caption acts like one column of four within the content width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strictly top‑to‑bottom, single‑column editorial flow. At the very top is the existing global header bar (logo, navigation, language, search, and the cart link). Beneath that, centered on the page, sits a large white content card that holds the entire Shop experience. Inside this card, the order is: (1) the shop page controller area with the page title and a brief descriptive paragraph; (2) a horizontal shop category navigation row; (3) a full‑width filter panel laid out as an in‑card band rather than a left sidebar; (4) an applied filters bar directly below as a slim status strip; (5) a compact row combining results summary on the left and sorting controls on the right; (6) a multi‑column product grid of book cards (echoing the 4‑column image row from the mockup); and (7) centered pagination controls at the bottom. All interactive controls remain inside this vertical content card; there is no separate visual sidebar.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strictly top‑to‑bottom, single‑column editorial flow. At the very top is the existing global header bar (logo, navigation, language, search, and the cart link). Beneath that, centered on the page, sits a large white content card that holds the entire Shop experience. Inside this card, the order is: (1) the shop page controller area with the page title and a brief descriptive paragraph; (2) a horizontal shop category navigation row; (3) a full‑width filter panel laid out as an in‑card band rather than a left sidebar; (4) an applied filters bar directly below as a slim status strip; (5) a compact row combining results summary on the left and sorting controls on the right; (6) a multi‑column product grid of book cards (echoing the 4‑column image row from the mockup); and (7) centered pagination controls at the bottom. All interactive controls remain inside this vertical content card; there is no separate visual sidebar.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header with the cart link occupies about 15–20% of the initial viewport height. The main content card, centered and fixed‑width (roughly 70–80% of viewport width with a max width around a 12‑column grid), occupies the remaining vertical space and scrolls. Within the card, the top 20–25% is dedicated to the shop page title and description; the category navigation and filter strip together take another ~15–20%. The applied filters bar and sort/results row are compact, together using less than 10% of the card height, leaving the lower ~45–55% for the product grid and pagination. Horizontally, all text sections span the full internal content width; the product grid uses this width to form four equally sized columns with modest gutters between cards, mirroring the original 4‑image row.
layout_strategies.content_density: Text sections (page title, description, summaries) use moderate density: readable line lengths, 1.4–1.6 line height, and vertical spacing of roughly 24–32px between blocks. Controls like filters, sort, and applied filter chips are slightly denser to keep the interaction area compact but not cramped. The product grid is the densest area, with cards placed close together using ~8–16px gutters and thin, subtle borders, maximizing the number of books visible at once while maintaining clarity. Overall, the layout favors information and scannability over large expanses of white space, consistent with a museum editorial feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts with the red brand bar and logo at the top left, then moves along the header to the right where language options, search, and the cart link live. From there, the eye drops to the large red serif page title inside the white card, then into the introductory copy below. The horizontal category navigation acts as the first interactive focal strip, followed by the filter band and the applied filters bar that reinforce the notion of narrowing results. The eye then moves to the results summary and sort controls row, and finally settles into the main product grid where the book covers and titles form a repeating visual rhythm similar to the historical photo thumbnails in the mockup. Pagination at the bottom terminates the scroll experience. All of this is framed by quiet grey page margins that keep attention anchored on the centered white card.

layout.overall_description: The Shop page appears as a calm, centered content experience within the museum site. A traditional header with the red brand bar sits at the top, stretching full width, with the logo on the left and utilities (language selector, search, and the cart link) on the right. Below, a single, large white content card is centered on a light grey background, occupying about three‑quarters of the viewport width. Inside this card, a bold red serif heading "Shop" sits at the top left, followed by a short descriptive paragraph that explains the museum’s cheese‑related merchandise. Beneath this narrative, a horizontal row of understated category pills lets visitors switch between Books and other merchandise, echoing navigation tabs rather than flashy buttons. Next, a full‑width filter strip runs across the card: reserved, grey‑tinted, and subdivided into several filter fields aligned into a subtle internal grid instead of a left sidebar. A narrow applied‑filters bar just below shows active filter chips and a clear‑all link. Then a compact row shows the total number of books on the left and sort controls on the right. The lower half of the card is dominated by a tight but readable 4‑column grid of book cards; each card shows a cover image, title, and details above a simple add‑to‑cart area, all framed by thin borders reminiscent of the mockup’s image thumbnails. At the bottom, centered pagination links provide a clear end to the page’s content. The entire layout feels like a structured, editorial catalog page—traditional, content‑first, and restrained—rather than a flashy e‑commerce layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shop page controller occupies the top section inside the main white content card, immediately below the global header. It spans the full internal width of the card (roughly 70–80% of the viewport, centered) with generous padding of about 24–32px on all sides, matching the card’s spacing system. The primary page title "Shop" is left‑aligned near the top of this block, rendered in the signature red serif, with about 16–24px of space above and below it. Just below the title, a short descriptive paragraph of one to three lines runs full width within the content column, maintaining comfortable line lengths and 24px line spacing. This entire controller section uses roughly the top 20–25% of the card height before the user scrolls, establishing the editorial introduction before any controls appear. There are no side columns; text flows as a single column centered within the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section inside the main content card; it scrolls naturally with the page and anchors the content hierarchy at the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the descriptive paragraph in the shop page controller, the shop category navigation appears as a horizontal strip of text‑based category pills (e.g., Books, Gifts, Apparel) aligned along the left edge of the content column. The navigation row spans the full internal width of the card but its items cluster toward the left, leaving open space to the right for breathing room. Each pill is a flat, slightly rounded rectangle with subtle 4–8px vertical and 8–12px horizontal padding, arranged with 8–12px horizontal spacing between them. The active category (Books) is highlighted in the brand red with white or off‑white text, while inactive categories appear as outlined or lightly filled neutrals. There is about 24px of vertical space above this row (from the paragraph) and 24–32px below it before the filter panel begins. The navigation visually reads as a single, thin belt of options within the otherwise text‑centric card, keeping the layout linear and avoiding a sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal row; remains in document flow and scrolls with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The book filter panel sits directly below the category navigation, spanning the full width of the content card’s internal area. It reads as a distinct band within the card, set on a very light grey or slightly tinted background to differentiate it from the white body, while still feeling subtle and museum‑like. Vertical padding of about 16–24px on top and bottom frames the controls. Inside this band, filters are organized into a multi‑column internal grid (e.g., four equal sections: Category, Author, Publication Date, Price) aligned left to right, mirroring the 4‑column image layout from the mockup. Each filter group has a small serif or simple sans label above its control (dropdowns, checkboxes, etc.), with 8px spacing between label and control. Horizontal spacing between filter groups is modest (about 16px) to avoid crowding while still keeping the band compact. On narrower screens, these groups wrap to a second row but remain part of the same band. There is about 8–16px of space between this panel and the applied filters bar below, maintaining a continuous, related block of filtering UI.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the content card but can optionally become sticky relative to the viewport top once scrolled past the page title, so that the filter band pins to the top edge of the card area while the user scrolls the product grid beneath it. When sticky, it maintains its full width within the card and a consistent 0–8px gap from the global header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied filters bar is positioned immediately below the filter panel, forming a slim horizontal strip that spans the full internal width of the card. It has a low‑profile appearance: a white or very light background with a subtle top border to visually anchor it under the filter band. Vertical padding is minimal (about 8–12px) to keep it compact. On the left, small pill‑shaped chips list each active filter (e.g., “Author: Smith”, “Price: Under $20”), arranged in a single row that wraps to multiple lines if necessary with 8px spacing between chips. On the right, a light‑weight “Clear all filters” text link aligns vertically with the chips and remains right‑justified within the same row. There is roughly 16–24px of vertical space between this bar and the results/sort row below it. Visually, it acts as a thin caption line for the filter panel above and a preface to the product results below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static strip that scrolls with the page. If the filter panel is sticky, this bar scrolls away normally; it does not stick, keeping the sticky area from feeling too tall.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The book sort bar shares a horizontal row with the results summary, together forming a functional band immediately above the product grid. Occupying the right half (or right side) of this row on larger screens, the sort controls align to the right edge of the content column. A concise “Sort by” label sits to the left of a dropdown selector, with 8px spacing between them. The dropdown itself is modest in size, mirroring the editorial style with flat styling and a thin border, and uses around 8–12px horizontal padding. On wider viewports, the entire sort group is right‑aligned, leaving clear visual separation from the results summary on the left. On smaller viewports, the row stacks vertically: results summary on top, sort controls immediately beneath, both left‑aligned. There is about 8–16px of top padding for the row relative to the applied filters bar, and 16–24px below before the product grid begins, keeping the band visually light but clearly distinct.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline controls within a flex‑like horizontal row; they reflow responsively (right‑aligned on wide screens, stacked under results summary on narrow screens).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The book results summary appears in the same horizontal row as the sort bar but anchored to the left side of the content column. It is a short, plain text line (e.g., “24 books in ‘Books’”) rendered in the same serif body style as other text, perhaps with the quantity highlighted in a slightly stronger weight. It sits flush with the left margin, aligning visually under the page title and category navigation above, reinforcing the strong left edge. On wide screens, this summary occupies roughly the left half of the row, with the sort controls occupying the right half. On narrow screens, it becomes the first line in a stacked pair (summary above, sort below), left‑aligning with the content column. Vertical spacing is restrained—about 8–12px of padding above and below—to keep the band compact yet readable. This summary acts as a final textual cue before the visual grid of book cards begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text block that shares a row with the sort controls on larger viewports and stacks above them on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The book product grid is the dominant visual area in the lower portion of the content card, positioned directly beneath the sort/results row. It spans the full internal width of the card and is laid out as a 4‑column grid on standard desktop widths, explicitly echoing the mockup’s 4‑image row pattern. Each column is of equal width with horizontal gutters of approximately 16px and vertical spacing between rows of about 24px. The grid begins about 16–24px below the sort/results band and extends downward, potentially occupying most of the scrolling experience. Each cell contains a book product card with a thin grey border and subtle 8–16px interior padding, visually similar to the historical photo thumbnails but adapted for commerce. On slightly narrower screens, the grid gracefully collapses to three columns, then two on tablet, and a single column on very small screens, while maintaining consistent gutter spacing. The overall effect is a dense but ordered matrix of book covers and captions, matching the museum catalog feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fluid grid within the fixed content width; the number of columns adjusts responsively based on available width while preserving consistent gutters and card proportions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within each cell of the product grid, the add‑to‑cart form occupies the lower portion of the book product card, directly beneath the book’s informational text (title, author, price). The card itself has a vertical structure: at the top a book cover image centered within the card width, below that the title and metadata in stacked text lines, and at the bottom the add‑to‑cart area. This area spans the full width inside the card’s padding, with about 8–12px of vertical spacing separating it from the text above. The add‑to‑cart control consists of a quantity selector (if present) aligned to the left and a primary button aligned either to the right or full‑width depending on design choice; the button uses the brand red fill with white text, with 4–8px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding, but scaled to fit the card width without overwhelming it. The margin between the bottom of the card content and the card border is about 8–12px, preserving the tidy appearance. This section is visually subordinated to the cover image and title but remains immediately discoverable at the bottom of each card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained within the product card; its position is fixed to the bottom area of each card and does not float or overlap neighboring elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls sit directly beneath the product grid, forming the last major element inside the content card. There is a clear vertical gap of about 24–32px between the bottom row of product cards and the pagination row, allowing the grid to visually conclude before pagination appears. The pagination row is horizontally centered within the content column and fairly compact: simple numbered links (1, 2, 3…) in a serif or neutral typeface, with the current page indicated by a solid or outlined shape in the brand red, and optional “Previous”/“Next” links flanking the numbers. Each pagination item has about 4–8px of horizontal padding and 8–12px of space between items, keeping the row legible but restrained. There is slightly more bottom padding (32–48px) between pagination and the bottom edge of the card to give the layout a dignified, finished feel before the page background resumes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered row at the bottom of the content card; it scrolls away with the page and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart nav link resides in the global header at the very top of the page, aligned with other utility items. It is positioned in the upper right area of the red brand bar or just below it, depending on the site’s header structure, typically to the right of the search field and language selector. The link is text‑forward (e.g., “Cart” or “Cart (2)”) potentially accompanied by a small, simple cart icon. It uses a clear, slightly heavier weight than surrounding navigation links to ensure discoverability without becoming a dominant focal point. Horizontal spacing of about 16–24px separates it from neighboring utilities, and it sits on the same baseline as search/language options to maintain a tidy header line. This link is always visible at the top of the viewport before scrolling, reinforcing the global shopping context for the Shop page while respecting the conservative header layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed within the global header component, which itself is pinned to the top of the page (if the overall site uses a fixed header). The cart link does not move independently; it remains in place as the user scrolls the main content card beneath it.

name: Cart & Visit Summary
title: Cart & Visit Summary
description: Unified cart page where visitors review museum entry tickets, tours/workshops, and shop products, adjust quantities, manage shipping for physical products, and see an up-to-date order total.

core_features: Load the unified cart with all current items (tickets, tours/workshops, shop products), available shipping options, and order summary totals using getCartSummary.

Display each cart item with its label, type-specific details (when available via AdmissionBooking or ProgramBooking), quantity, unit price, and line total based on the CartItem data from getCartSummary.
Allow users to update the quantity of individual cart items by calling updateCartItemQuantity, then refresh the cart data via getCartSummary.
Allow users to remove items from the cart by calling removeCartItem, then refresh the cart data via getCartSummary.

List available ShippingOption entries when the cart includes physical shop products, allow the user to select one via selectShippingOption, and ensure the order summary reflects the chosen shipping cost.
Provide an order summary section that displays subtotal, shipping total, and overall total using the order_summary object from getCartSummary.
Offer navigation actions to add more museum tickets, tours/workshops, or shop products using the specified outgoing links and URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Review current cart contents and totals:
1. User navigates to cart.html via the header link or after adding items on another page.
2. On page load, the controller component calls getCartSummary with no parameters.
3. While loading, a non-blocking loading indicator is shown.
4. When getCartSummary resolves, the page displays all items, shipping options (if any), and the order_summary totals.
5. If there are no items, the empty-cart state is shown with navigation options to visit.html, tours_workshops.html?view=guided, and shop.html?category=books.

Update the quantity of a cart item:
1. User locates an item in the cart and interacts with its quantity control (e.g., increments, decrements, or edits a numeric input).
2. The quantity control validates locally that the value is a positive integer; if the user enters 0, the UI may prompt to remove the item instead of updating.
3. On confirmation of a valid new quantity, the component calls updateCartItemQuantity with { cart_item_id, quantity }.
4. If the API call succeeds, the controller triggers getCartSummary again to reload items, shipping options, and order_summary.
5. If the API call fails (e.g., invalid quantity), an error message is displayed in the notification component and the UI reverts to the previous valid quantity.

Remove an item from the cart:
1. User clicks the remove action for a specific item.
2. An optional confirmation is displayed by the UI; upon confirmation, the component calls removeCartItem with { cart_item_id }.
3. On success, the controller calls getCartSummary again to refresh items and totals.
4. If the removed item was the last item of a certain type (e.g., last shop product), the refreshed data may remove the shipping options section accordingly.
5. If the cart becomes empty, the empty-cart state is rendered instead of the items list.

Select or change a shipping option for physical products:
1. When getCartSummary returns, if the items include at least one shop_product, the shipping options section is shown using the shipping_options array and selected_shipping_option_id value.
2. If a shipping option is already selected in the cart, the corresponding option is marked as selected; the URL parameter shippingOptionId is updated to match this value.
3. When the user selects a different shipping option, the component updates the URL parameter shippingOptionId and calls selectShippingOption with { shipping_option_id }.
4. On success, the controller calls getCartSummary again so that order_summary.shipping_total and order_summary.total match the newly selected option.
5. If selectShippingOption fails, the UI shows an error message and reverts the selection to the last known valid option from getCartSummary.

Add more tickets, tours/workshops, or books from the cart:
1. User scrolls to the navigation actions section of the cart page.
2. To add museum entry tickets, the user clicks the relevant action, which navigates to visit.html.
3. To add a guided tour or workshop, the user clicks the relevant action, which navigates to tours_workshops.html?view=guided.
4. To add more books or continue shopping from the cart, the user clicks the relevant action, which navigates to shop.html?category=books.
5. After the user adds additional items on those pages and uses their cart links or direct return navigation, they come back to cart.html, which again calls getCartSummary to show the updated cart.

Focus on a specific item after navigation or action:
1. Another page (e.g., a booking review step) may link to cart.html with a query parameter such as cart.html?highlightItemId={cart_item_id} to focus a specific item.
2. On load, the controller parses highlightItemId from the URL and, after getCartSummary resolves, scrolls to and visually emphasizes the corresponding item row if it exists in the returned items.
3. The user can then adjust this item’s quantity or remove it as needed, triggering updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem and subsequent getCartSummary refreshes.
interactions: Page load interaction: on initial navigation to cart.html (with or without query parameters), the page controller calls getCartSummary, manages the loading state, and distributes the returned cart, items, shipping_options, selected_shipping_option_id, and order_summary to child components.

Quantity change interaction: when the quantity input for a cart item loses focus or a plus/minus control is activated, and the new value is valid, the quantity component calls updateCartItemQuantity(cart_item_id, new_quantity); on success it triggers a cart refresh via getCartSummary.

Remove item interaction: when the remove control for a cart item is confirmed by the user, the item-row component calls removeCartItem(cart_item_id) and, upon success, signals the controller to call getCartSummary to obtain the updated cart state.

Shipping option selection interaction: when a user selects a shipping option radio/selector entry, the shipping selector component updates the URL query parameter shippingOptionId and calls selectShippingOption(shipping_option_id). On success, the controller refreshes the cart using getCartSummary so that order_summary reflects the new shipping cost.

Navigation interactions: clicking the 'Add more tickets' link navigates to visit.html, clicking 'Add a tour or workshop' navigates to tours_workshops.html?view=guided, and clicking 'Add more books' or 'Continue shopping' navigates to shop.html?category=books, leaving cart state managed server-side via the Cart entity.

Empty-cart interaction: when getCartSummary returns with no items, the empty-cart component is rendered; interacting with any of its navigation links leads the user to visit.html, tours_workshops.html?view=guided, or shop.html?category=books.

URL-driven section interaction: when a user toggles between different logical sections on the page (e.g., between items list and shipping section if such toggling exists in the UI), the controller updates a section query parameter in the URL (such as section=items or section=shipping) so that reloading or sharing the URL returns the user to the same section without using localStorage.

Error handling interaction: if any call to getCartSummary, updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem, or selectShippingOption fails, the controller passes an error message to the notification component, which displays it until dismissed by the user (e.g., clicking a close button), while the rest of the cart remains interactable using the last successfully loaded data.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The cart.html page does not rely on localStorage; all persistent state comes from the backend Cart and related entities via the assigned interfaces, and transient UI state is encoded in URL query parameters.

URL parameters used by this page (read and/or written by the controller):
- section: Optional. Controls which logical part of the page is emphasized or scrolled into view on load (e.g., section=items or section=shipping). When the user changes sections via UI controls, the controller updates this parameter using pushState/replaceState. On load, the controller reads section and, after getCartSummary resolves, adjusts focus or scrolls to the corresponding section.
- highlightItemId: Optional. If present (e.g., cart.html?highlightItemId={cart_item_id}), after getCartSummary returns, the controller searches the items array for a matching CartItem.id. If found, it instructs the items list component to focus and emphasize that item row (e.g., scroll into view). This parameter is not required by the backend and may be removed or updated by the page when no longer needed.
- shippingOptionId: Optional. If present, it represents the user’s intended shipping selection. On load, after getCartSummary resolves, the shipping selector component compares shippingOptionId with the returned selected_shipping_option_id. If they differ and the URL’s shippingOptionId corresponds to one of the returned shipping_options, the component calls selectShippingOption with that ID and, on success, the controller refreshes the cart via getCartSummary. When the user changes the shipping option through the UI, the shippingOptionId parameter is updated accordingly.

These URL parameters are used purely for client-side behavior and navigation continuity; they do not alter server-side cart content directly beyond triggering the allowed interfaces (getCartSummary, updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem, selectShippingOption). All textual labels, prices, shipping option names (including any options whose names might correspond to concepts such as standard domestic or multi-day delivery), and totals are obtained dynamically from the API responses rather than being hardcoded in the page implementation.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading, URL parameter handling, error handling, and propagation of cart state (cart, items, shipping options, and order summary) to child components.

Renders the list of cart items returned by getCartSummary, or hides itself when there are no items. Delegates item-specific interactions (quantity changes and removal) to child components and emits events to the controller.

Displays a single cart item with its label, type-specific booking details where available, quantity controls, unit price, and total price. Provides remove functionality and communicates user actions upward.
Provides numeric input or increment/decrement controls for adjusting CartItem.quantity, validates the input, and triggers quantity update API calls via events handled by the controller.
Allows the user to remove an item from the cart, optionally after a confirmation step, by triggering a removeCartItem call via the controller.

Displays available shipping options for the current cart using shipping_options from getCartSummary, highlights the selected option, and lets the user change the selection by calling selectShippingOption. Hidden or disabled when there are no applicable shop_product items in the cart.

Shows a concise summary of the order’s subtotal, shipping total, and overall total based on the order_summary object from getCartSummary, and updates whenever the cart is refreshed after any modification.
Provides direct navigation actions to continue adding items: general admission tickets, tours/workshops, or shop products. Uses the specified target pages and URL parameters.
Displays when getCartSummary returns a cart with no items. Provides guidance and navigation options to start adding items to the cart, using the same links as the visit-actions-panel.
Shows success or error messages resulting from API calls (getCartSummary, updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem, selectShippingOption) and allows the user to dismiss them.
Indicates that data is being loaded or updated while getCartSummary, updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem, or selectShippingOption are in progress.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width grid similar to a 12-column system with a main content column and an internal 4-column image grid. Each image/caption acts like one column of four within the content width.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom flow contained in a single centered white card beneath the existing red museum header. At the top of the card: notification banner (when present), then a single, strong red serif page heading ('Cart & Visit Summary'), followed by one concise explanatory paragraph. Below the narrative text, the functional content flows in this order: visit-actions panel (compact, link-like actions), the main cart-items list (stack of rows), a full-width shipping options section, and finally a full-width order summary panel that internally uses a 3–4 column breakdown similar to the mockup’s image row.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom flow contained in a single centered white card beneath the existing red museum header. At the top of the card: notification banner (when present), then a single, strong red serif page heading ('Cart & Visit Summary'), followed by one concise explanatory paragraph. Below the narrative text, the functional content flows in this order: visit-actions panel (compact, link-like actions), the main cart-items list (stack of rows), a full-width shipping options section, and finally a full-width order summary panel that internally uses a 3–4 column breakdown similar to the mockup’s image row.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header + any notification occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport. The centered content card spans about 70–80% of the viewport width with a comfortable max-width, and dominates the remaining vertical space. Inside the card, the textual intro (heading + paragraph + visit actions) takes about the top 30–40% of the card’s height, while the cart-items list, shipping options, and order summary panel collectively occupy the lower 60–70%. The order summary panel visually reads as a distinct band at the bottom of the card. Horizontal space inside the card follows a simple editorial column for text and list rows, and a 4-column-inspired internal grid inside the order summary panel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain moderate density: readable line lengths, generous but not airy spacing between sections (24–32px), and clear separation between rows. The cart-items list is slightly denser, with items stacked closely but still separated by thin dividers and 16–24px vertical padding per row. The order summary panel and visit-actions panel are compact and information-rich, analogous to the denser image grid in the mockup, while still respecting 8–12px gutters between grouped elements.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the red museum header to any prominent notification banner, then to the large red page title centered within the white card. From there it drops to the descriptive paragraph, then to the subtle but clearly grouped visit actions. It then flows down through the vertical stack of cart items (left-aligned product/visit info with right-aligned controls), then to the shipping options block, and finally settles on the darker-framed order summary band at the bottom, which provides closure with totals and next-step emphasis. Any loading indicator temporarily overlays this flow, and empty cart states reuse the same vertical path but replace the list and summary with a centered message and visit actions.

layout.overall_description: The page presents a classic, centered white content card floating on a light gray background beneath the museum’s red brand header. The card is about three-quarters of the viewport width, with 24–32px padding inside. At the very top of the card, a full-width notification banner appears when needed, followed by a large red serif heading announcing 'Cart & Visit Summary'. A short paragraph beneath it calmly explains that this page contains tickets, tours, and shop products, with occasional green links for emphasis. Just below the narrative, a compact strip of visit-related actions sits like a subtle navigation line. The main body of the card is a vertical stack of cart item rows, each running the full content width, with item details on the left and quantity/remove controls aligned to the right. Beneath this stack, a full-width shipping options block spans the card, framed with a light grey border and arranged in a simple columnar layout. At the very bottom, a visually distinct order summary band stretches across the card, using an internal multi-column grid to lay out subtotals, taxes, shipping, and the final total, echoing the mockup’s horizontal image strip. When the cart is empty, the middle of the card instead shows a centered, editorial-style empty state with a small illustration, heading, and inviting text, followed by the same visit actions.
