A loading indicator, when active, subtly dims the card and centers a spinner over the content without breaking the overall classic, content-first feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This invisible controller conceptually spans the entire main content area beneath the global red header, matching the width of the centered white content card (approximately 70–80% of the viewport width, with a max-width around a traditional editorial column). It is aligned to the horizontal center of the page and manages the vertical stack of child components inside the card: notification banner at the top, then heading and introductory paragraph (not separate components), followed by the visit-actions panel, the cart-items list, the shipping-options selector, and finally the order-summary panel at the bottom. It also governs switching between the standard cart layout and the empty-cart-state, and overlaying the loading-indicator on top of all content within the card’s bounds.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the parent layout context for all cart components, but has no visible styling itself. It controls stacking order for state components: when loading, it places the loading-indicator as an absolute/fixed overlay centered within the card; when the cart is empty, it hides the cart-items-list, shipping-options-selector, and order-summary-panel and centers the empty-cart-state instead.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-items-list occupies a full-width section in the middle of the content card, directly below the visit-actions panel and above the shipping-options selector. Horizontally, it spans the entire inner width of the card’s content column, leaving only the card’s standard left/right padding (24–32px). Vertically, it grows with the number of items, forming a stack of cart-item-rows with thin dividers between them. The top of the list begins roughly one third down the card, following the heading and introductory text, and can occupy anywhere from a quarter to over half of the card height depending on how many items exist. There is about 24px of vertical spacing separating the list from the visit-actions panel above and another 24–32px before the shipping-options-selector below, clearly segmenting the list as the central content block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a scrollable segment within the page, but not independently scrollable—its height simply extends the card. On taller lists, the page scrolls, keeping the header fixed at the top of the viewport while this list moves beneath. The list’s width and alignment remain locked to the centered card; on narrower screens it retracts proportionally but maintains full-width usage relative to the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each cart-item-row is a horizontal band within the cart-items-list, stretching from the left inner padding of the card to the right inner padding, minus a small 8–12px gutter from the list’s outer border. It has approximately 16–24px vertical padding above and below its content, separated from adjacent rows by a subtle light grey divider line running full width. The left two-thirds of the row are reserved for stacked textual information: item title in a slightly larger serif font, followed by one or two lines of smaller metadata (date/time for tickets, workshop details, or product attributes). The right one-third of the row is a compact control area aligned to the right edge: the quantity-selector sits slightly to the left of the far right border, and the remove-item-button is aligned either to the top-right or just to the right of the quantity control, with an 8–12px gap between them. If shipping-specific controls are needed per item (for physical goods), a subtle secondary line of text or icon appears just beneath the main title block on the left. Overall, the row retains a clean, table-like structure but in a comfortable editorial style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Each row is a fixed-height band that expands minimally with multi-line titles or messages. It is anchored within the flow of the cart-items-list, and reflows responsively: on smaller widths, the right-hand controls may stack vertically while still remaining right-aligned, but the row always consumes the full available width within the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity-selector appears within the right-hand control zone of each cart-item-row. It is positioned near the right edge, aligned vertically to the center of the row’s content area. Visually it occupies a compact horizontal footprint—about one quarter of the row’s width at most—leaving space on its right side (8–12px) for the remove-item-button or for a buffer to the card edge. The selector has a simple rectangular form with subtle borders, consistent with the flat, conservative aesthetic: plus and minus controls flanking a centered numeric value, or a small dropdown, all set in a legible serif or supporting sans-serif. It is clearly separated from the text on the left by at least 24–32px of horizontal whitespace, reinforcing the distinction between information and controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains anchored to the right section of the cart-item-row. On wide screens it stays inline horizontally to the left of the remove-item-button; on narrow screens it may move above the remove-item-button but remains right-aligned within the row’s content area. It does not scroll independently and its size is constant regardless of the row height, maintaining a predictable target area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove-item-button sits at the far right end of each cart-item-row’s control area, aligned either vertically center or slightly top-aligned with the item title, depending on the row’s content height. It is a compact, text-first button—likely a red text label or an outlined pill—with 4–8px vertical and 8–12px horizontal padding. There is a small 8–12px gap between it and the quantity-selector to its left, creating a clear but close association between the two controls. Horizontally, the button aligns with the right inner padding of the card so that all remove buttons in the list form a clean vertical line down the page, echoing the ordered feel of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Consistently anchors to the rightmost edge of each cart-item-row. On smaller screens, it may drop beneath the quantity-selector but remains right-aligned to preserve the columnar visual rhythm. It does not float or stick; it scrolls naturally with the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping-options-selector appears as a distinct, full-width block directly beneath the cart-items-list and above the order-summary-panel. It is framed with a thin light-grey border and slightly rounded or squared corners, matching the conservative card motif. Inside, it uses the same centered content width as the rest of the card, with about 24px padding on all sides. The title or label for shipping sits at the top-left of this block, followed by a set of vertically stacked or lightly columnar options (e.g., radio buttons for standard vs. express shipping) arranged with 8–12px vertical spacing between each option. If there is additional explanatory text (e.g., expected delivery dates), it appears below each option in smaller type. Vertically, this block is relatively compact—around 15–20% of the card height on a typical cart—but can expand gracefully if multiple options exist.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored as a static section in the vertical flow of the card. It always occupies the full width of the card’s content area and maintains a consistent margin above (24–32px from the last cart-item-row) and below (24–32px to the order-summary-panel). On narrower screens, any multi-column arrangement of options collapses into a single column, but the block remains centered within the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-summary-panel forms the bottom band of the main content card, visually echoing the mockup’s horizontal image row. It spans the full inner width of the card, with 24–32px padding on all sides and a slightly stronger border or subtle background tint to distinguish it from the list above. Inside, it uses an internal grid divided into roughly four vertical segments: the first one or two segments on the left list subtotals and line items (tickets, tours, shop products) as stacked text; the next segment captures taxes and shipping; and the final segment on the right highlights the grand total and the primary checkout action, all right-aligned. Each segment acts like one column in a 4-column grid, with 16–24px horizontal gutters between them. This layout makes the summary feel like a structured, information-dense footer to the card, occupying approximately 20–30% of the card height. The total amount and the main action within this panel are slightly emphasized with bolder text and the brand red, but still within the restrained editorial style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed at the bottom of the content card’s flow, not sticky to the viewport. As the user scrolls, the panel comes into view after the shipping options and remains anchored there. On smaller screens, the internal 4-column structure stacks into 2 or 1 columns while maintaining a clear top-to-bottom ordering: subtotals first, then taxes/shipping, then total and action last. It always remains full-width within the card and aligned with the card’s center.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visit-actions-panel is a compact horizontal strip positioned between the introductory paragraph at the top of the content card and the cart-items-list. It sits about 16–24px beneath the paragraph, spanning the full inner width of the card but using only a small vertical height—roughly the size of a single line of pills or text links. Within this strip, 2–4 actions (such as 'Add more tickets', 'Browse workshops', 'Continue shopping') are arranged left-to-right, each styled like a flat pill or simple underlined link with 4–8px vertical and 8–12px horizontal padding. The items have small gaps of 8–12px between them, forming a subtle, navigation-like row that visually recalls the line of thumbnail images from the mockup while remaining text-based and unobtrusive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored within the upper third of the card, it does not float or stick. On narrower screens, the actions may wrap to a second line while staying left-aligned under the paragraph. If the introductory text is minimal, this panel still maintains a consistent vertical gap from both the heading and the cart-items-list to preserve the editorial rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-cart-state occupies the central portion of the content card when no items are present, replacing the cart-items-list, shipping-options-selector, and order-summary-panel. It is vertically centered between the intro text and the bottom of the card, with generous vertical padding above and below (32–48px) to emphasize the emptiness. The layout uses a simple vertical stack: at the top, a modest-sized illustrative icon or historical image related to cheese or the museum, centered; below it, a short serif heading in red or dark text stating that the cart is empty; followed by one or two lines of explanatory copy in a standard paragraph width. Beneath this, the visit-actions-panel is reused in a more prominent role: its actions are centered horizontally under the message, with 8–12px spacing between them, resembling a small row of calls to action in the otherwise empty space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed conditionally in place of the usual middle and lower sections of the card. It remains centered within the card’s content width and adjusts vertically as needed so that, on shorter viewports, it still appears roughly in the middle of the visible area. It scrolls with the page, but since it is compact and centered, it often fits without requiring scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification-banner appears at the very top of the white content card, directly beneath the global red header and above the page heading. It spans the full width of the card’s inner content area, with 16–24px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding. The banner may use a lightly tinted background (e.g., pale red for errors, pale green for success) framed by a thin outline, maintaining the flat aesthetic. Its text is left-aligned and single-column, with any icon placed to the left of the message. A close button, if present, sits at the far right of the banner, aligned on the same horizontal line as the text. When visible, the banner pushes the heading downward but does not overlap any content, preserving the clean vertical stacking.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears within the normal document flow, not fixed to the viewport. When shown, it increases the card’s height and shifts all subsequent components downward by its own height plus the standard 16–24px gap before the heading. On narrower screens, the text may wrap to two or three lines, but the banner remains full-width within the centered card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator is a modal-like overlay that appears centered over the content card when data is being fetched or updated. It covers the full area of the card with a semi-transparent white or light grey scrim, allowing the underlying layout (heading, list, summary) to remain faintly visible to preserve context. At the center of the card, a small spinner or simple animated motif is displayed, with a brief one-line loading message set in the same serif typography as the rest of the site. The indicator’s central content sits roughly in the middle of the visible portion of the card, with ample empty space around it so it feels unobtrusive yet clearly blocks interaction.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned absolutely or fixed relative to the content card via the cart-page-controller, it overlays all other components within the card, intercepting interaction while active. It does not affect the underlying layout’s flow or spacing; when it disappears, the user finds the same scroll position and layout beneath. On very long pages, the overlay extends to cover the visible portion of the card, but the central spinner always remains vertically and horizontally centered in the viewport.

name: Newsletter Sign-Up
title: Newsletter Sign-Up
description: Page to subscribe to the museum’s newsletter, choose interests and frequency, optionally review preferences, and confirm the subscription.

core_features: Load available newsletter interests, frequency options, and language options dynamically using getNewsletterOptions on page initialization.
Prefill the email input field from the optional email URL parameter if present (e.g., newsletter.html?email=...).
Capture the user’s email address and optional preferred language for newsletter communication.
List all active content interests returned by getNewsletterOptions to allow multi-selection, including options whose codes match ancient_cheese_history and museum_events_workshops when available.
Provide a frequency selector bound to the frequency_options from getNewsletterOptions, ensuring the user can select a frequency whose value equals monthly_digest when that option is available.
Handle initial subscription creation via subscribeToNewsletter with email, preferred_language, frequency, and selected interest_codes, showing success or error states based on the API response.
Offer an optional review and adjustment step for preferences on the same page after initial subscription, using updateNewsletterPreferences to save any changes to frequency or interest_codes.

Show contextual status and confirmation messaging after subscription creation and/or preference updates, and provide a functional 'Back to Home' navigation to index.html after successful confirmation.
Use URL parameters (email and a step parameter) to control initial form state and which step of the flow (subscribe, review, success) is shown, without relying on localStorage.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – New subscription (single-step completion):
1. User visits newsletter.html, optionally with newsletter.html?email=<address>.
2. On page load, the page-state manager reads the email URL parameter and sets the initial step to 'subscribe' if no step is provided.
3. The options loader component calls getNewsletterOptions and exposes the returned interests, frequency_options, and language_options to the sign-up form.
4. The sign-up form pre-fills the email field from the URL parameter if available and displays dynamic language, interest, and frequency options.
5. The user enters or edits their email, optionally selects a preferred language, selects zero or more interests from the dynamically loaded list, and chooses a frequency from frequency_options. If a frequency option with value monthly_digest exists, it is preselected; otherwise, no default is enforced.
6. The user submits the form. The component validates minimally (e.g., non-empty email) and calls subscribeToNewsletter with email, preferred_language (or null/undefined if not selected), frequency, and the selected interest_codes.
7. While the API call is in progress, the form disables inputs and submit to prevent duplicate requests.
8. If subscribeToNewsletter returns success=true, the subscription object is stored in page state, the status message component shows the success message from the response, and the page-state manager updates the URL to newsletter.html?step=success (optionally preserving email) and displays the success/confirmation view.
9. If success=false, the error message from the response is surfaced in the status component and near the form; the step remains 'subscribe' and the user can correct inputs and resubmit.

Workflow 2 – New subscription with review step:
1. Steps 1–7 are identical to Workflow 1, but instead of going directly to 'success', the page-state manager uses newsletter.html?step=review after a successful subscribeToNewsletter call.
2. The review component receives the current in-memory subscription data (email, frequency, selected interest_codes) and the dynamic options from getNewsletterOptions.
3. The review component shows a read-only or lightly editable summary of the email and fully editable controls for interests and frequency (reusing the same dynamic lists). The user can adjust interests and frequency if desired.
4. When the user confirms, the review component calls updateNewsletterPreferences with the updated frequency and interest_codes. Email and language are not changed here because the interface does not accept those parameters.
5. While updateNewsletterPreferences is in progress, its controls are disabled and a loading state is shown.
6. On success, the updated subscription returned is stored in state, a confirmation message is displayed, and the page-state manager updates the URL to newsletter.html?step=success (optionally also keeping email).
7. On error, an error message is displayed and the user stays on the review step to adjust inputs and retry.

Workflow 3 – Direct access to review or success via URL (guarded by state):
1. User manually opens newsletter.html?step=review or newsletter.html?step=success.
2. On initialization, the page-state manager reads the step parameter. If a valid step is present but no current subscription exists in the current page state (e.g., first load without having just subscribed), the manager falls back to step='subscribe' because there is no subscription to review or confirm.
3. For a valid existing subscription in memory (e.g., navigation back from browser history within the same session), the appropriate component for 'review' or 'success' is shown. No additional API calls are made unless the user modifies preferences and submits, which triggers updateNewsletterPreferences again as needed.

Workflow 4 – Post-confirmation navigation:
1. After a successful subscription and any optional preference updates (step='success'), the success section of the page becomes visible along with a 'Back to Home' link.
2. When the user clicks 'Back to Home', the navigation component sends the browser to index.html without extra parameters as specified in the outgoing navigation configuration.
3. No further API calls are made on this click; the newsletter page is simply left.
interactions: Typing in the email field updates the in-memory form state bound to the email value that will be sent to subscribeToNewsletter.
Changing the preferred language selection updates in-memory state with the selected language value from language_options, which will be passed to subscribeToNewsletter as preferred_language.
Selecting or deselecting an interest toggles the presence of its interest_code (from the interests array returned by getNewsletterOptions) in the in-memory interest_codes array.

Selecting a frequency updates the in-memory frequency value, constrained to the list of values returned in frequency_options. If a value equals monthly_digest, the UI can emphasize it as a recommended once-per-month option but still binds strictly to the backend-provided value.

Clicking the primary submit button on the sign-up form triggers client-side validation and, if validation passes, calls subscribeToNewsletter with the current email, preferred_language, frequency, and interest_codes.

Receiving a successful response from subscribeToNewsletter triggers: storing the returned subscription object in page state; displaying a success message via the status component; and updating the URL's step parameter (e.g., to 'review' or 'success') via the state manager to reflect the new phase.
Receiving an error response from subscribeToNewsletter triggers display of the returned message in the status component and near the form, and re-enables the form for correction and resubmission.

On the review step, changing checkboxes or selectors for interests or frequency updates in-memory preference state. Submitting this review form calls updateNewsletterPreferences with the selected frequency and interest_codes.

Receiving a successful response from updateNewsletterPreferences updates the stored subscription object and triggers a transition to the 'success' step, reflected both in displayed components and in the URL step parameter.
Clicking 'Back to Home' on the success step sends the browser to index.html as a full navigation change.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page reads and manages state using URL parameters and in-memory variables, without relying on localStorage or other client storage.

URL parameters:
- email: Optional. On initial load, if email is present in the query string (newsletter.html?email=...), the page-state manager passes this to the sign-up form to prefill the email field. This parameter is not required and has no direct effect on server-side logic; it is purely for convenience.
- step: Optional. Recognized values include 'subscribe', 'review', and 'success'. On initial load:
  - If step is missing or not one of the recognized values, the state manager defaults to step='subscribe'.
  - If step='review' or step='success' but there is no current subscription in page state (e.g., fresh load or after a hard refresh), the state manager overrides this and sets step='subscribe', since the review and success views depend on a prior subscription.

Internal state:
- subscription: Holds the most recent NewsletterSubscription object returned from subscribeToNewsletter or updateNewsletterPreferences. It is used to populate the review and success displays. It is not persisted beyond the current page session.
- options: Holds interests (NewsletterInterestOption[]), frequency_options, and language_options returned by getNewsletterOptions. These power all selectable controls and are never hardcoded.
- formState.subscribe: Holds in-progress values for the subscription form (email, preferred_language, frequency, interest_codes) before calling subscribeToNewsletter.
- formState.review: Holds editable values (frequency and interest_codes) for the review step before calling updateNewsletterPreferences.
- status: Holds the latest status information (loading flags for options, subscribe, and update operations; and the latest error or success message strings) that drives the status-messages component.

URL updates:
- After a successful subscribeToNewsletter call, the page-state manager updates the step parameter using history.pushState or equivalent (e.g., to 'review' or 'success' depending on the chosen flow). The email parameter may be preserved or omitted as appropriate, but no additional state is encoded in the URL.
- After a successful updateNewsletterPreferences call, the step parameter is set to 'success'.

All conditional rendering (which component is visible) is based on the internal step state, which is derived from the URL on load and then kept synchronized with the URL whenever step changes.

components.functionality: Initializes and manages page-level state, including parsing URL parameters (email and step), holding the current step of the flow (subscribe, review, success), and synchronizing step changes back into the URL without reloading the page.

On page load, calls getNewsletterOptions to retrieve dynamic interests, frequency options, and language options, stores them in shared state, and exposes loading and error status used by the form and review components.

Provides the primary subscription form where the user enters their email, selects an optional preferred language, chooses one or more interests, and selects a frequency. On submit, it validates inputs and calls subscribeToNewsletter.

Displays a summary of the current subscription (email and other non-editable fields) and allows the user to review and adjust interests and frequency before final confirmation. On save, it calls updateNewsletterPreferences with the current selections.

Shows contextual loading, error, and success messages for options loading, initial subscription, and preference update operations, based on status flags in shared state and any message strings returned from the interfaces.

When the step is 'success', displays a concise summary of the active subscription using the latest NewsletterSubscription object and the selected interests and frequency, confirming that preferences are saved.
Provides a functional link from the success state back to the website home page (index.html) after a successful newsletter subscription and preference confirmation.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width grid similar to a 12-column system with a main content column and an internal 4-column image grid. Each image/caption acts like one column of four within the content width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow. A site-wide header with logo, language options, and search occupies the top band. Below it, a single centered white content card holds everything for the newsletter: first the page heading, then a compact area for status messages, followed by the main newsletter sign-up form. Beneath the form, an optional preferences review panel appears within the same card. After a successful subscription, the success summary replaces the form/review section in the same central area. A small back-to-home link sits at the bottom-left of the card. Invisible state and loading components sit conceptually inside the card, either as thin inline rows or as subtle overlays, without breaking the single-column editorial structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow. A site-wide header with logo, language options, and search occupies the top band. Below it, a single centered white content card holds everything for the newsletter: first the page heading, then a compact area for status messages, followed by the main newsletter sign-up form. Beneath the form, an optional preferences review panel appears within the same card. After a successful subscription, the success summary replaces the form/review section in the same central area. A small back-to-home link sits at the bottom-left of the card. Invisible state and loading components sit conceptually inside the card, either as thin inline rows or as subtle overlays, without breaking the single-column editorial structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header occupies roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height. The main content card is centered and spans about 70–80% of the viewport width, with light grey page background on both sides. Inside the card, the heading plus status messages area takes roughly the top 20–25% of the card’s height. The newsletter form is the dominant block, using about 40–50% of the card height in the default state. The preferences review sits beneath the form and occupies about 20–30% when present. On success, the success summary panel uses the combined vertical space allocated to the form and review. The back-to-home link uses a narrow strip along the bottom edge of the card. Horizontal content within the card respects an internal grid: primary text in a single main column with occasional 2-column rows, and interest options / summaries arranged in a denser 4-column grid reminiscent of the mockup’s image row.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate and text-first. Major blocks (heading, status area, form, review, success summary, back link) are separated by 24–32px vertical gaps. Within the form, individual fields are spaced at about 16–24px to feel readable but not airy. The interest-selection area and any summary lists use a denser grid with smaller gutters (~8–12px) between items to echo the mockup’s thumbnail row while keeping borders and labels clear. Line lengths are comfortable, with the main text column limited to roughly 60–80 characters. Overall the page feels efficient and informational rather than spacious or minimalist.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the red brand bar and logo in the global header, moves to the right to notice language options and search, then drops down to the large red page title centered within the main card. Directly beneath that, status messages—when present—draw quick attention. The gaze then follows down into the stacked form fields, with interest and frequency options forming denser blocks that mimic the mockup’s horizontal grid. After the form, attention moves to the preferences review, which neatly encapsulates the chosen options. In the success state, the strong confirmation heading and summary take over this central area. Finally, the flow terminates on the small, understated back-to-home link at the card’s bottom-left, while any scroll-to-top affordance (site-wide) remains a subtle peripheral element anchored towards the right edge of the viewport.

layout.overall_description: The page sits within a conservative, editorial-style museum layout. At the very top, a horizontal red-accented header bar holds the museum logo at the left and language/search utilities towards the right. Beneath it, in the center of a light grey background, rests a single, large white content card with generous 24–32px padding on all sides. At the top of this card, a bold, red page title—“Newsletter Sign-Up”—spans the width of the main text column. Immediately below, a slim horizontal band is reserved for status messages so that success or error notices appear close to the title without disrupting the flow. The main body of the card is a vertically stacked, single-column form: name, email, consent, then a more compact but visually denser grid of interest options arranged in four columns, followed by frequency and confirmation controls. This central form block dominates the middle of the card. When the user has filled in details, a preferences review panel appears directly underneath as a full-width, lightly bordered section, visually tied to the form above yet separated by a clear 24–32px gap. On successful subscription, the form and review are replaced by a success summary that uses a similar hierarchy: a strong confirmation heading, concise explanatory text, and a compact grid of key details. Anchored at the bottom-left inside the card, a small serif back-to-home link sits in its own breathing space, acting as a quiet navigational escape. Invisible state and loading layers either show as slim in-flow rows or subtle overlays centered over the card, always preserving the clean, linear, content-first feel of the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state-manager component does not occupy a visible, permanent space in the layout. Conceptually, it lives attached to the main content card, controlling which visible child components occupy the central column. In the default case, the card shows the heading, status messages, and newsletter form stacked from top to bottom, with the preferences review below. In transitional states (like full-page loading or blocking confirmation), this component can project a semi-transparent white overlay that covers the entire interior of the card from padding edge to padding edge, roughly 24–32px inset from the card’s border on all sides. At the center of this overlay, both vertically and horizontally, a small spinner and one-line status text appear, aligned to the card’s internal grid. The overlay does not shift or resize underlying content; instead, it sits above it, dimming but preserving the visual structure, and scrolls naturally with the page since it is anchored to the card rather than fixed to the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normally non-rendered and occupying no layout space; when active, displayed as an absolutely positioned layer that stretches to cover the entire main content card area, with a high z-index. It scrolls with the card content and does not affect the document flow of other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The options loader appears within the vertical stack of the main content card, specifically in the portion of the card allocated to the form. When newsletter interest options are being fetched, the loader occupies a full-width horizontal strip inside the form area, positioned where the interest-selection fields will later appear. It spans the same text column width as the form fields and sits approximately halfway down the form, beneath the basic personal information fields but before any frequency or confirmation controls. Vertically, this strip is about 48–64px tall, with its content (spinner and short descriptive label) centered both horizontally and vertically in that band. Once the options have loaded, this loader strip collapses completely, allowing the interest grid to take its place without leaving extra whitespace, maintaining the linear flow and the moderate density seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rendered as a block-level element within the normal document flow of the form. It appears and disappears dynamically, expanding and collapsing the space where the interest grid will reside, without affecting the positions of the heading, status messages, or back-to-home link.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter sign-up form is the primary content block within the main card, occupying most of the card’s central vertical area. It begins just below the status messages area, with a clear top margin of about 24–32px separating it from the title region. The form adheres to a single main text column aligned to the left, within the card’s inner padding. Core fields (name, email, consent) are stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing. Approximately midway down the form, the layout shifts into a denser grid for the interests: this section spans nearly the full content width and is subdivided into an internal 4-column grid, each column hosting one interest option as a compact card or checkbox row. Horizontal gutters between these interest items are narrow (around 8–12px), and vertical spacing between their rows mirrors the image grid feel from the mockup. Below this grid, frequency selection and any additional toggles return to a simple single- or two-column arrangement, still aligned to the same left edge. The primary submit button is placed towards the left, directly under the last form field, with a 24–32px top margin to distinguish it as a clear action row. Bottom padding of similar size (24–32px) separates the end of the form from the preferences review panel when it is present. Overall, the form occupies roughly 40–50% of the card’s height in the default, unsent state, and controls the central portion of the user’s visual journey down the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that participates in the normal vertical flow of the main content card. It expands naturally as fields or validation messages appear, pushing the preferences review and back-to-home link downward while maintaining consistent card padding and column alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The preferences review panel is a full-width section located directly below the newsletter form within the main content card. It sits in the same central column, aligned to the left with the form fields and heading, and begins after a pronounced 24–32px vertical gap so it reads as a distinct, subsequent section. The panel itself is framed with subtle styling—such as a light grey top border or a faint background tint—to separate it visually from the form above while preserving the overall conservative aesthetic. Inside, the chosen interests, frequency, and email are displayed in a compact, text-first layout: either as a single column of labeled rows or as two columns on wider screens that still respect the main grid. Headings for groups (e.g., “Your interests”) align with the left edge of the text column, and their associated values sit directly underneath with 8–12px internal spacing. The panel’s height is flexible, typically using around 20–30% of the card’s height when content is present. A small, inline edit or change link may appear aligned to the right of section headers but remains visually subordinate to the summary text. A bottom margin of about 24–32px separates the end of this panel from the back-to-home link or, in success states where the panel may be hidden, leaves space for the succeeding component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that appears and disappears within the main card’s vertical flow. When it is shown, it pushes the back-to-home link downward; when hidden, the space collapses so that the next component below moves up into its place.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Status messages occupy a dedicated horizontal band near the top of the main content card, directly beneath the bold page heading and above the form. By default, this area is either absent or reduced to a minimal spacer (around 8–12px) so the layout remains tidy. When messages are present—such as errors, warnings, or transient confirmations—they appear as a full-width strip aligned to the same left text column as the form, with 16–24px height for short messages and up to 48–64px for multi-line content. The message strip may use a subtle colored cue (such as a slender vertical bar in red or green on the far left) paired with standard serif or clean sans-serif text in a color that stands out but remains consistent with the museum’s conservative palette. This band visually anchors feedback close to the page title, ensuring users see it before interacting further with the form. Below this strip, a clear 16–24px gap leads into the first form field, preserving the editorial rhythm seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block within the card’s vertical flow that expands and collapses based on message presence. It does not overlay other elements; instead, it simply pushes the form downward when visible and allows the form to move up when hidden.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The success summary replaces the combination of form and preferences review in the same central region of the main content card once a subscription has been confirmed. It starts immediately below the page heading (and status area if a short success notice is also shown), occupying the same primary column. A large, confident confirmation heading in red appears first, followed by one or two paragraphs of explanatory text that stretch across the main text width with ample 16–24px line spacing for readability. Beneath this text, a more structured summary section echoes the mockup’s lower image grid: key confirmation details (such as subscribed email, selected interests, and frequency) are presented in a compact 2- to 4-column layout, each column acting like a small information tile with a label and brief description. These tiles are evenly spaced across the width with 8–12px gutters, creating a subtle band of denser information toward the lower half of the summary area. The overall height of this block typically matches or slightly exceeds that of the former form, giving the impression that the central content card remains visually full and balanced. A generous 32px or so of vertical space separates the bottom of the summary from the back-to-home link, clearly distinguishing the conclusion of the subscription journey from general navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that occupies the same position within the card as the form and review would. It appears in the normal document flow while those components are hidden or unmounted, keeping the heading and back-to-home link anchored in their respective positions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-home link is positioned at the bottom-left corner of the main content card, acting as a quiet, tertiary navigational element. It aligns precisely with the left edge of the main text column and sits after a clear vertical gap—around 32–48px—from whichever primary content block precedes it (form and review, or success summary). The link is styled to resemble a piece of inline editorial text rather than a large button: a modest-sized serif label, possibly accompanied by a small left-pointing arrow icon, in the site’s link color. The surrounding space is relatively open: a small buffer (16–24px) below the link before the bottom padding of the card, and ample grey background beneath the card itself. This placement makes the link easy to find at the end of a scroll without competing visually with the main content above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element at the end of the card’s vertical flow. It does not stick or float; it simply moves downward as the card grows taller with content and remains anchored to the card’s bottom left within the fixed-width central layout.

name: About the Museum
title: About the Museum
description: Provides structured information about the Cheese History Museum’s mission, background, and key collections, and routes visitors towards Exhibitions, Timeline, Learn, and Visit pages.

core_features: Load and display dynamic About-page content (mission, history, key collections) using the getAboutPageContent interface.
Segment content into logical sections (Mission, History, Key Collections) that can be deep-linked via URL parameters and in-page navigation.
Highlight key collections and map each collection’s related_section field to navigational calls-to-action leading to Exhibitions, Timeline, or Learn pages.
Provide a clear route to visit.html via a 'Plan your visit' link within the About content.
Allow deep-linking and state restoration using URL parameters (e.g., selected section, highlighted collection) without relying on localStorage.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Basic visit to About page
1. User navigates to about.html from the footer or a direct link.
2. The page controller reads URL parameters (e.g., section, highlight_collection) but does not yet scroll because content is not loaded.
3. The content loader component calls getAboutPageContent with no parameters.
4. When the API response arrives, the Mission, History, and Key Collections sections are rendered.
5. The controller uses the URL parameters to determine the initial section to focus on (e.g., mission, history, collections) and an optional collection to highlight.
6. The in-page navigation and key collections list now reflect the current state (current section, highlighted collection).

Workflow 2: Navigate to a specific section from another page
1. User clicks an 'About the museum' teaser on the homepage linking to about.html?section=history.
2. The About page loads and calls getAboutPageContent.
3. Once content is rendered, the controller scrolls to the History section and marks that section as active in the in-page navigation.
4. If the user clicks another section button (e.g., 'Key Collections'), the section changes and the URL is updated to about.html?section=collections without a full reload.

Workflow 3: Explore a key collection and go to related section
1. User is on about.html (any entry point) and the page has loaded content via getAboutPageContent.
2. In the Key Collections list, the user sees dynamically rendered collection items from key_collections.
3. For a given collection, the component reads related_section (e.g., 'exhibitions', 'timeline', or 'learn') and presents a corresponding action link (e.g., 'Explore Exhibitions', 'Explore cheese history timeline', or 'Learn more about cheese history').
4. The user clicks the action link:
   - If related_section is 'exhibitions', navigate to exhibitions.html with optional parameters derived from the collection (e.g., exhibitions.html?from=about&collectionIndex={index}).
   - If related_section is 'timeline', navigate to timeline.html with optional parameters (e.g., timeline.html?from=about&highlightFromAbout=1).
   - If related_section is 'learn', navigate to learn.html with optional parameters (e.g., learn.html?from=about&related=cheese_history).
5. The target page uses its own logic to interpret the URL parameters; the About page does not make additional API calls.

Workflow 4: Plan a visit from About page
1. User scrolls to or navigates to any section where a generic 'Plan your visit' link or CTA is shown (e.g., near mission or history text).
2. User clicks 'Plan your visit'.
3. Browser navigates to visit.html?from=about (or just visit.html) so the Visit page can optionally adapt behavior based on the source.
4. No additional interfaces are used by the About page for this action.

Workflow 5: Deep-link to a specific key collection
1. Another page or an external source links to about.html?section=collections&highlight_collection={index}.
2. About page loads, calls getAboutPageContent, and renders all sections.
3. After render, the controller interprets section=collections and scrolls to the Key Collections section.
4. The controller interprets highlight_collection and passes it as a prop or state to the key-collections-list so one collection item is marked as highlighted (e.g., expanded or visually emphasized by internal logic).
5. If the user clicks a different collection, the component updates highlight_collection in the URL (e.g., about.html?section=collections&highlight_collection={newIndex}) using the History API, enabling sharable deep links.
interactions: On page load: The page controller reads URL parameters (section, highlight_collection) and initializes internal state. It triggers getAboutPageContent once to retrieve mission, history, and key_collections. After successful data load, it applies scroll/focus logic based on the initial state.

In-page navigation clicks: When the user clicks a section control (e.g., 'Mission', 'History', 'Key Collections'), the controller updates the active section state, scrolls to the corresponding section anchor, and updates the URL parameter section without reloading the page.

Key collection selection: Clicking on a key collection header or 'View details' toggle sets that collection as active within the key-collections-list component and updates highlight_collection in the URL. This allows the currently active collection to be preserved across refreshes or shared via URL.

Key collection outbound links: Within each key collection item, the user can click a dynamically rendered action link determined by the related_section field. This performs a standard navigation to exhibitions.html, timeline.html, or learn.html, optionally including parameters like from=about and collectionIndex to identify context.

Plan your visit link: A static navigation link in the relevant About section sends the user to visit.html (e.g., visit.html?from=about), enabling the dedicated Visit page to handle ticketing and scheduling using its own logic.

URL-driven state changes (back/forward): When the user uses browser back/forward, the page listens to popstate events. The controller re-reads the section and highlight_collection parameters and updates the active section and highlighted collection accordingly, including re-scrolling if necessary.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page uses only URL parameters and in-memory component state; no localStorage or other persistent client storage is used. Supported URL parameters include: 1) section: Determines which content section is considered active on load and on URL changes. Accepted values are treated as opaque strings but expected to match internal section IDs such as 'mission', 'history', or 'collections'. If missing or invalid, a default section (e.g., 'mission') is used. When the user changes sections via in-page navigation, the controller updates the section parameter using history.pushState or history.replaceState (e.g., about.html?section=history). 2) highlight_collection: Represents a specific key collection to emphasize (e.g., by index or identifier). The key-collections-list component parses this parameter on load. If it matches a valid collection index within key_collections, that collection is marked as active/highlighted. When the user selects another collection, the component updates highlight_collection in the URL without reloading the page. 3) from: A generic tracking parameter that might be set by other pages (e.g., from=home). The About page does not change behavior based on this parameter but preserves it when generating outbound links if desired. State derivation flow: a) On initial load, the controller reads section and highlight_collection, stores them in its internal state, and calls getAboutPageContent. b) Once getAboutPageContent resolves, the controller renders the sections, then performs a scroll to the active section and communicates highlight_collection to the key-collections-list. c) On user interactions (section navigation or collection selection), the controller updates its internal state and the URL query string, ensuring that a reload or direct share of the current URL reproduces the same viewed section and highlighted collection. d) On popstate (back/forward), the controller re-reads URL parameters and updates internal state and scroll positions accordingly. No other persistent mechanisms are used.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading via getAboutPageContent, interprets URL parameters (section, highlight_collection), manages the active section and highlighted collection state, and triggers in-page navigation behaviors such as scrolling and focus.
event_handlers: onLoad: Parse URL parameters, initialize internal state, and invoke getAboutPageContent().
onGetAboutPageContentSuccess: Receive mission, history, and key_collections, pass them as props to child components, and perform initial scroll to the section derived from section parameter.

onSectionChange: Triggered by in-page navigation component; updates active section state, scrolls to the appropriate section anchor, and updates section in the URL via history.pushState or history.replaceState.

onCollectionHighlightChange: Triggered by key-collections-list when the user selects a collection; updates highlight_collection in internal state and URL, and optionally adjusts scroll to ensure the highlighted collection is visible.
onPopState: Respond to browser back/forward events by re-reading section and highlight_collection from URL and updating internal state and scroll accordingly.

components.functionality: Executes getAboutPageContent with no parameters, manages loading and error states, and exposes the resulting mission, history, and key_collections data to presentation components through a shared context or props.
event_handlers: onLoad: Call getAboutPageContent() once when the component is mounted or when the page is loaded.
onSuccess: Store mission, history, and key_collections in component state and notify about-page-controller that content is ready.
onError: Expose an error state so the page can show a generic error message or fallback indicating that About content cannot be loaded.

components.functionality: Provides controls for jumping between the Mission, History, and Key Collections sections, keeps the current section highlighted, and updates the active section in both the UI and URL parameters.
event_handlers: onClickMission: Request the controller to change section to 'mission'; controller updates section state, adjusts scroll, and updates the URL parameter section=mission.
onClickHistory: Request the controller to change section to 'history'; controller updates state, scroll, and URL parameter section=history.
onClickCollections: Request the controller to change section to 'collections'; controller updates state, scroll, and URL parameter section=collections.

components.functionality: Displays the mission text returned by getAboutPageContent and provides a stable anchor for in-page navigation and deep linking. Optionally includes contextual navigation links to Learn or Visit pages based on the museum’s mission focus.
event_handlers: onVisible: Optionally notify the controller when the Mission section becomes dominant in the viewport so activeSection can be updated when the user scrolls manually.
onClickPlanYourVisit: Navigate to visit.html or visit.html?from=about using a standard link; no additional interfaces are called.
onClickLearnMore: Navigate to learn.html (optionally with from=about) using a standard link.

components.functionality: Renders the historical background text about the museum and/or cheese history from getAboutPageContent.history, and acts as the anchor for history-related deep linking and navigation.
event_handlers: onVisible: Optionally notify the controller when the History section is most visible so activeSection can reflect manual scrolling.
onClickExploreTimeline: Navigate to timeline.html (e.g., timeline.html?from=about) using a standard link, encouraging users to explore the detailed interactive timeline.

components.functionality: Hosts the key-collections-list and anchors the Key Collections area of the page for deep linking, such as when section=collections is provided.
event_handlers: onVisible: Notify the controller when the Key Collections section becomes dominant in the viewport so it can update activeSection accordingly.

components.functionality: Iterates over the key_collections array returned by getAboutPageContent and renders a list of key collection items. It supports highlighting a specific collection based on URL state and exposes outbound navigation links determined by each item’s related_section value.
event_handlers: onItemSelect(collectionIndex): Triggered when a user focuses or expands a collection; updates about-page-controller.highlightedCollection and causes the controller to update highlight_collection in the URL.

onItemActionNavigate(collectionIndex): Triggered when the user activates the primary CTA within a collection item. The component reads key_collections[collectionIndex].related_section and
          - if related_section === 'exhibitions': navigates to exhibitions.html with contextual parameters such as from=about and collectionIndex.
          - if related_section === 'timeline': navigates to timeline.html with contextual parameters such as from=about.
          - if related_section === 'learn': navigates to learn.html with contextual parameters such as from=about.
          - if related_section has any other value or is missing: may omit the CTA or default to a generic navigation pattern defined in the component logic.
onListRendered: After the list is rendered and the highlighted collection is set, ensure the highlighted item is scrolled into view if highlight_collection is present in the URL.

components.functionality: Represents one key collection entry, displaying its title and description from getAboutPageContent and providing a context-sensitive outbound link based on related_section. It also supports an 'active' or 'highlighted' state when its index matches highlight_collection.
event_handlers: onClickHeader: Notify key-collections-list that this item should become the highlighted collection, which cascades into a highlight_collection URL update.

onClickAction: Depending on related_section value, trigger external navigation:
          - related_section='exhibitions' -> navigate to exhibitions.html with appropriate parameters.
          - related_section='timeline' -> navigate to timeline.html with appropriate parameters.
          - related_section='learn' -> navigate to learn.html with appropriate parameters.
onFocus: Similar to onClickHeader, may update highlightedCollection to improve accessibility (keyboard navigation).

components.functionality: Provides a consolidated set of outbound links from the About page to key site areas (Visit, Exhibitions, Timeline, Learn), potentially using context such as the presence of specific related_section values within key_collections, but without making any API calls beyond getAboutPageContent.
event_handlers: onClickVisit: Navigate to visit.html or visit.html?from=about.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width grid similar to a 12-column system with a main content column and an internal 4-column image grid. Each image/caption acts like one column of four within the content width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow centered within the viewport. Above, the global museum header (outside this component list) spans the full width. Below it, the about-page-controller presents a single, large white content card centered on a light grey background, occupying roughly 70–80% of the viewport width. Inside that card, the arrangement follows: (1) a strong red page title line for “About the Museum” at the very top of the card, (2) an in-page navigation row just beneath the title for quick jumps to Mission, History, and Key Collections, (3) the Mission and History sections as full-width text blocks, stacked one after the other, (4) a Key Collections section heading and short intro paragraph, followed by (5) a four-column grid of collection items (image + caption) that fills the lower half of the card width, and finally (6) a cross-links footer row that routes visitors toward Exhibitions, Timeline, Learn, and Visit. All components sit within this single central card, with no sidebars.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow centered within the viewport. Above, the global museum header (outside this component list) spans the full width. Below it, the about-page-controller presents a single, large white content card centered on a light grey background, occupying roughly 70–80% of the viewport width. Inside that card, the arrangement follows: (1) a strong red page title line for “About the Museum” at the very top of the card, (2) an in-page navigation row just beneath the title for quick jumps to Mission, History, and Key Collections, (3) the Mission and History sections as full-width text blocks, stacked one after the other, (4) a Key Collections section heading and short intro paragraph, followed by (5) a four-column grid of collection items (image + caption) that fills the lower half of the card width, and finally (6) a cross-links footer row that routes visitors toward Exhibitions, Timeline, Learn, and Visit. All components sit within this single central card, with no sidebars.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header and utility area at the top of the page occupy roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height, with the main about-page content card taking up the remaining visible space and then extending vertically as needed. Horizontally, the content card is centered and constrained to a fixed-width column (around 960–1120px or roughly 70–80% of the viewport on desktop), surrounded by light grey margins. Within the card, the upper 40% of the card's height is primarily textual: title, in-page navigation, and the Mission + History text sections, each separated by 24–32px vertical gaps. The lower 60% of the card height is dominated by visuals: the Key Collections heading and intro followed by a dense 4-column image grid where each key-collection-item occupies approximately one quarter of the content width (around 22–24% each) with 8–12px gutters. The crosslinks footer is relatively compact and sits beneath the grid with a comfortable 24–32px top margin but modest height.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density follows a moderate, editorial pattern: body text spans the full card width but is constrained so line length stays around 60–75 characters, with line spacing that feels readable but not airy. Vertical spacing between related elements (heading to paragraph, paragraph to next section heading) uses the base system of roughly 24–32px, while spacing within tight groups (caption to image, items in the cross-links footer) uses 8–16px. The Key Collections grid is visually denser, with thinner gutters (8–12px) between image cards and relatively compact captions. Overall, the layout privileges readability and efficient information presentation over dramatic whitespace, reflecting a museum-like, content-first tone.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visitor’s eye moves from the global red brand bar and logo at the top, across utilities like language and search, then down into the centered white content card. Inside the card, attention is first captured by the strong red page title at the top, then immediately by the in-page navigation links just beneath it. From there, the eye flows down the page in a linear fashion: first to the Mission section, then to the History section, both set as comfortable text blocks. The introduction to Key Collections acts as a transition, leading the eye into the more visual 4-column grid of collection items; this grid forms a strong horizontal band of imagery in the lower half of the card. Finally, the cross-links footer at the very bottom offers clear paths to Exhibitions, Timeline, Learn, and Visit, bringing the flow to a structured conclusion. Peripheral, utility-like behaviors (such as any scroll-to-top control handled elsewhere) remain visually secondary and do not interrupt the main vertical reading path.

layout.overall_description: The About the Museum page appears as a calm, centered editorial layout beneath the site’s global red museum header. The main content sits within a single large white card that floats against a light grey page background, aligned centrally and constrained to a fixed width that feels like a classic print column. At the very top of this card, a bold red page title, set in a traditional serif typeface, introduces the page. Just below it, a discrete horizontal row of in-page navigation links runs from left to right, allowing visitors to jump to Mission, History, or Key Collections without breaking the visual subtlety of the design. Beneath this navigational strip, the content is purely linear: the Mission section appears first as a full-width heading and a few short paragraphs, then the History section follows with similar structure and spacing, creating a comfortable reading rhythm. After these text-heavy blocks, the Key Collections section opens with a heading and a compact explanatory paragraph before giving way to a denser four-column grid of collection thumbnails and captions that stretches across the entire card width, echoing the multi-image row from the original mockup. Each image card appears like a small museum exhibit label: a bordered image tile above, a concise caption below, all aligned in neat vertical columns with minimal gutters, forming a strong visual band in the lower half of the page. At the very bottom of the card, a cross-links footer sits as a narrow horizontal strip, using understated text links and subtle red accents to route visitors toward Exhibitions, Timeline, Learn, and Visit. The total effect is that of a measured, trustworthy museum information page, where content flows straight downward, the visuals are organized into a clear grid, and all elements respect a consistent 8px-based spacing system and a conservative, editorial aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-page-controller acts as the main container for the page’s content area beneath the global site header. It positions a single, large white content card centered horizontally in the viewport, resting on a light grey background. The card stretches to roughly 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop, with a fixed maximum width (around 960–1120px) and equal left and right margins. Vertical margin between the bottom of the global header and the top of this card is around 32–48px, allowing the card to feel visually separated and prominent. Inside this card, the controller applies uniform padding of approximately 24–32px on all sides, enforcing the vertical stacking order: at the top edge of the card the page title is rendered, followed by the in-page navigation, then the mission-section, history-section, key-collections-section (which internally contains the key-collections-list), and finally the about-crosslinks-footer at the bottom. All these sections follow a simple block flow from top to bottom with 24–32px spacing between major sections, ensuring a consistent editorial rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block container that scrolls with the page; defines the fixed-width central column and internal vertical stacking order for all child components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-content-loader is visually minimal and sits logically at the top of the content area inside the about-page-controller, just above or integrated with the page title area. When loading states are active, it can reserve a slim horizontal strip spanning the full width of the content card, about 4–8px tall if represented as a progress bar, or a compact inline spinner aligned near the left under the title if using an indicator. In either case, it respects the 24–32px internal padding of the card and does not push content excessively; any loading indicator appears with 8–16px separation from surrounding text so it feels like a subtle utility element rather than a main content block. When data is fully loaded, it effectively collapses to zero height and becomes visually invisible, letting the editorial layout read uninterrupted.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies minimal inline or strip space at the top of the card when active; otherwise collapses and does not affect layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-in-page-navigation sits inside the main content card directly beneath the red page title, aligned to the left edge of the text column. It spans the full internal width of the card but uses only a small vertical footprint—roughly 32–48px in height including padding. The navigation items (e.g., Mission, History, Key Collections) are laid out horizontally from left to right, with each link styled as understated text or subtle pill-like buttons that echo the museum’s conservative aesthetic: modest font size, classic serif or matching sans serif, and red or dark gray text with gentle hover underline. Horizontal spacing between links is around 16–24px, giving each item breathing room. There is a 8–16px gap between the bottom of the page title and the top of this navigation row, and a 24–32px gap between this row and the mission-section below, so it reads as a distinct but integrated control strip in the upper text portion of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element flowing in the document; optionally can become sticky relative to the card on very long content, but by default scrolls with the page and maintains its position beneath the title.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mission-section is the first major textual content block inside the card, positioned immediately below the in-page navigation. It spans the full internal width of the content card and is aligned with the left text edge, maintaining consistent margins on the right. At its top, a section heading (e.g., “Our Mission”) appears in a slightly smaller but still prominent serif style than the main page title, in dark gray or red, followed by 16–24px of vertical space and then one or more paragraphs of body text. The body text is arranged in a single column with a comfortable line length (roughly 60–75 characters) and uses moderate line spacing. Vertical spacing between paragraphs is around 12–16px to maintain the editorial feel. The bottom of the mission section maintains about 24–32px of whitespace before the history-section begins, clearly separating the two while preserving a smooth reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A standard block section that fills the card’s width and scrolls naturally as part of the vertical content flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The history-section appears directly beneath the mission-section, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical space. It follows the same full-width, single-column format: a section heading at the top aligned to the left, set in the same style and size as the Mission heading, with 16–24px of space before its paragraphs. The body text may be slightly longer here to convey the museum’s background, but it still respects moderate density—line length and spacing match the Mission section for consistency. Any inline emphasis (such as dates, names, or links to timelines) uses conservative treatments like red or green text color and underlining to echo the mockup’s use of highlighted links. The bottom of this section leaves a 24–32px gap before the Key Collections section begins, maintaining the structured, stacked feel of the content card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block section within the vertical flow, occupying full card width and scrolling with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The key-collections-section starts below the history-section and introduces the more visual lower half of the card. It occupies the full internal width of the content card. At the top, a section heading such as “Key Collections” is left-aligned and visually prominent, in the same heading hierarchy as the earlier sections, with 16–24px of space below it. A short introductory paragraph or two explains the nature of the collections in a single column, using the same body text styling as above. This text ends with approximately 24px of vertical space before the key-collections-list begins. Visually, this section acts as a bridge: the heading and short text anchor the eye before it moves down into the denser grid of collection items, effectively marking the transition from purely textual sections to the image-heavy lower zone of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full-width block section that serves as a parent container for the key-collections-list; it scrolls normally and expands vertically with its content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The key-collections-list is placed immediately beneath the introductory text of the key-collections-section, occupying the full width of the content card. It is structured as a horizontal multi-column grid that echoes the mockup’s image row: on desktop, it forms a four-column layout where each key-collection-item takes approximately one quarter of the available width (around 22–24%), with uniform 8–12px gutters between items both horizontally and vertically. The top edge of the grid aligns with the left edge of the text column, and the grid extends to the right while respecting the card’s internal padding. The first row of items sits about 24px below the section intro text, forming a clear boundary between text and imagery. As the number of items grows, additional rows stack vertically with consistent row gaps (16–24px), creating a band of tightly organized thumbnails and captions that visually dominates the lower half of the page. On narrower viewports, the grid can fluidly collapse to two or one columns while maintaining consistent gutters and a centered feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A responsive grid container that flows as a block; it adjusts the number of columns based on available width but always remains centered within the content card and scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each key-collection-item is a compact image card inside the key-collections-list grid. It occupies roughly 22–24% of the content width on a four-column desktop layout, with 8–12px horizontal gutters separating it from neighboring items. Vertically, each item is composed of a simple stacked structure: at the top, a bordered image thumbnail with a slightly old-fashioned, museum-catalog feel (a thin grey border around a flat image, possibly in a 3:2 or 4:3 aspect ratio) spans the full width of the item. Beneath the image, separated by about 8px, sits a caption block: the collection title in a slightly bolder serif or small caps style, possibly in dark red or dark gray, followed by a short description or date line beneath it. Caption text is left-aligned with 4–8px spacing between lines, keeping the block compact. The overall height of each item varies with caption length but is kept visually aligned by limiting the number of lines in the description. The outer margins of each item are consistent, and the combination of thin borders, tight gutters, and stacked captions gives the grid the look of a classic museum image index.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A fixed-structure card within the grid that resizes proportionally with the column width; it flows as part of the grid and does not have independent scrolling behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-crosslinks-footer is positioned at the very bottom of the main content card, directly beneath the last row of key-collection-item cards, separated by approximately 32px of vertical space to provide a clear visual end to the collections grid. It spans the full internal width of the card, with its content aligned to the left but balanced so it does not feel cramped. A subtle top border line in light grey or a slight tint can visually distinguish it from the content above, running horizontally across the width with 16px of padding above and below. Inside, 3–4 textual cross-links (e.g., Exhibitions, Timeline, Learn, Visit) are arranged horizontally on larger screens, starting from the left, with 16–24px spacing between each link. Each link is styled as plain but prominent text, using the brand red color and possibly small arrows or separators, keeping with the conservative design. On smaller viewports, these links can wrap to a second line or stack vertically while maintaining 8–16px vertical gaps. The footer feels like a calm exit row from the page, providing clear onward paths without overwhelming the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block footer section at the bottom of the content card; it scrolls with the page and remains the last visible component in the linear vertical flow.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page where visitors can view the museum’s contact details and send questions, feedback, or special requests via a contact form.

core_features: Fetch and display current contact details (address, phone, email, brief visit information summary, and map embed) using getContactInfo.
Provide a contact form to send questions, feedback, or special requests using submitContactForm.
Prefill the contact form subject field from the optional URL parameter `subject` when present.
Offer navigation links to visitor information and directions (visit.html) and to policies (policies.html) from the Contact page.
Handle loading, error, and success states for both contact information retrieval and contact form submission without using localStorage.
user_workflows:
View contact details:
1. User opens contact.html (from footer or direct link).
2. contact-page-controller parses URL parameters (including optional `subject`).
3. contact-page-controller calls getContactInfo and enters a loading state.
4. On success, contact-info-panel receives the contact data and renders address, phone, email, visit_info_summary, and a link to open the map (via map_embed_url).
5. User can click the phone or email to initiate a call or email in their device, or use the map embed to see location and basic directions.

Send a general inquiry:
1. User navigates to contact.html without a `subject` parameter.
2. contact-page-controller initializes contact-form with an empty subject field.
3. User fills in name, email, subject, and message fields in contact-form.
4. User submits the form; contact-form validates required fields and, if valid, triggers submitContactForm with the current form values.
5. While waiting for the response, contact-form shows a submission-in-progress state (e.g., disabled submit button).
6. On success (submitContactForm.success = true), contact-form-feedback displays the success message from the API.
7. On failure (submitContactForm.success = false), contact-form-feedback displays the returned error message and allows the user to correct and resubmit.

Send a topic-specific inquiry with prefilled subject:
1. User clicks a contextual “Contact us” link from index.html or policies.html that navigates to contact.html with a `subject` URL parameter (e.g., contact.html?subject=<encoded text>).
2. contact-page-controller reads the `subject` parameter and passes it as the initialSubject prop to contact-form.
3. contact-form initializes its subject field with this value, which the user can edit.
4. User completes other fields (name, email, message) and submits the form, which calls submitContactForm with the edited subject.
5. Success or error feedback is shown via contact-form-feedback.

Navigate to visitor information and policies:
1. On contact.html, user sees a short visit_info_summary and links labeled for visitor information & directions and policies.
2. When the user clicks the visitor information link in contact-info-panel, the browser navigates to visit.html.
3. When the user clicks the policies link in contact-info-panel, the browser navigates to policies.html.
4. If those pages provide contextual contact links back (with `subject` parameter), the cycle repeats with a prefilled subject on contact.html.
interactions: Page load: Triggers parsing of URL parameters (including `subject`) and an API call to getContactInfo.
Click on phone number: Opens the device’s default call handler using the phone_number from getContactInfo.
Click on email address: Opens the device’s default mail client with the email_address from getContactInfo.
Click on map or map link: Opens the embedded map or external map view based on map_embed_url.
Click on 'Visitor information & directions': Navigates to visit.html with no additional parameters.
Click on 'View policies': Navigates to policies.html with no additional parameters.
