Form field input (name, email, subject, message): Updates the internal state of contact-form as the user types.
Form submit: Validates required fields, calls submitContactForm(name, email, subject, message), and updates UI based on the returned success and message.
Form submit success: Displays success feedback via contact-form-feedback and may reset the form fields.
Form submit failure: Displays error feedback via contact-form-feedback and keeps user-entered values for correction.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Contact page uses URL parameters and in-memory UI state (not localStorage). The recognized URL parameter is `subject` (string). On page load, contact-page-controller reads `subject` from the query string, decodes it, and passes it as the initial subject value to contact-form. If `subject` is absent or empty, the subject field is initialized empty. This URL-driven initial state is used only on first render; if the user edits the subject, the form’s internal state takes precedence and is not written back to the URL. Contact information state includes: loading (before getContactInfo resolves), loaded (after successful response), and error (if getContactInfo fails). Contact form state includes: idle (no submission yet), submitting (awaiting submitContactForm result), success (submitContactForm.success = true, with associated message), and error (submitContactForm.success = false, with associated message). These states are held in component state and are not persisted across page reloads; reloading the page re-applies URL-based initialization for the subject and re-fetches contact data using getContactInfo.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading, URL parameter parsing, and inter-component communication for the Contact page.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse the current page URL to read the `subject` query parameter (if present) and store it as initialSubject for the contact form.
onInit: Call getContactInfo() once on page load, manage loading and error state, and pass the resulting contact data to child components.
onContactInfoLoadSuccess: Provide address, phone_number, email_address, visit_info_summary, and map_embed_url to contact-info-panel and contact-map-embed.
onContactInfoLoadError: Set an error state that contact-info-panel can use to display a non-data-specific error message.
onFormSubmitResult: Receive success and message from contact-form after submitContactForm resolves, and pass them to contact-form-feedback for display.

components.functionality: Displays current contact details and key navigation links using data from getContactInfo.
event_handlers: onClickPhone: Trigger the browser to initiate a call via a tel: link using phone_number from getContactInfo.
onClickEmail: Trigger the browser to open the default mail client via a mailto: link using email_address from getContactInfo.
onClickVisitInfoLink: Navigate to visit.html (no additional parameters) for detailed visitor information and directions.
onClickPoliciesLink: Navigate to policies.html (no additional parameters) to view formal policies.
onContactInfoError: When contact-page-controller signals an error state, display a generic error message instead of contact details.

components.functionality: Shows the museum’s location using the map_embed_url from getContactInfo, allowing the user to view the map and optionally open it in an external map application.
event_handlers: onMapLoad: Initialize the embedded map or iframe using map_embed_url when available.
onMapError: If the map embed cannot be loaded, display a non-data-specific fallback message and keep navigation links available.
onClickOpenInMaps: If applicable, open the map_embed_url in a new browser tab or window for full-screen viewing or directions.

components.functionality: Collects the visitor’s name, email, subject, and message, and submits them via submitContactForm.
event_handlers: onInit: Receive initialSubject from contact-page-controller (derived from URL `subject`) and initialize the subject field with it (or empty if not provided).
onChangeName: Update internal state for the name field when the user types.
onChangeEmail: Update internal state for the email field and optionally mark it invalid if it does not match basic email format rules.
onChangeSubject: Update internal state for the subject field as the user edits it, overriding the initial URL-derived value.
onChangeMessage: Update internal state for the message field as the user types.

onValidate: Before submission, check that required fields (name, email, subject, message) are not empty and that email has a valid format; if validation fails, prevent submission and surface validation messages.

onSubmit: Prevent default browser form submission, call submitContactForm(name, email, subject, message) with current field values, set a submitting state to disable the submit button, and pass the asynchronous result to contact-page-controller.
onSubmitSuccess: When submitContactForm returns success = true, reset the submitting state, optionally clear the form fields, and trigger contact-form-feedback with the returned message.

onSubmitError: When submitContactForm returns success = false or the call fails, reset the submitting state while retaining field values for correction, and trigger contact-form-feedback with the error message.

components.functionality: Displays success or error messages after contact form submission based on the response from submitContactForm.
event_handlers: onFeedbackUpdate: When contact-page-controller provides a new submitContactForm result, update the display to show the corresponding message and styling (success or error).
onDismissFeedback: Allow the user to dismiss the message (e.g., by clicking a close control), which clears the displayed status but does not alter any stored data.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width grid similar to a 12-column system with a main content column and an internal 4-column image grid. Each image/caption acts like one column of four within the content width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom structure derived from the mockup: a global branding header at the very top, then a single centered white content card for the Contact page. Inside this card, the top portion is reserved for the page title and introductory explanatory text. Directly beneath this narrative block, a horizontal content band uses the internal 4-column grid: the contact-info panel occupies the left quarter (1 of 4 columns) and the map embed spans the remaining three quarters (3 of 4 columns). Below this band, the contact form runs full width across the card. The form feedback indicator sits tightly coupled to the form, just below the form title, so it is perceived as part of the form rather than an independent section.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom structure derived from the mockup: a global branding header at the very top, then a single centered white content card for the Contact page. Inside this card, the top portion is reserved for the page title and introductory explanatory text. Directly beneath this narrative block, a horizontal content band uses the internal 4-column grid: the contact-info panel occupies the left quarter (1 of 4 columns) and the map embed spans the remaining three quarters (3 of 4 columns). Below this band, the contact form runs full width across the card. The form feedback indicator sits tightly coupled to the form, just below the form title, so it is perceived as part of the form rather than an independent section.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header area consumes about 15–20% of the initial viewport height. The centered main content card occupies roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, with light grey margins on both sides. Within the card, the top narrative zone (page title + short descriptive paragraph) takes roughly 30–40% of the card height on a typical desktop view. The contact-info + map row occupies another ~30–40%, appearing as a strong horizontal band beneath the text. The full-width contact form fills the remaining lower section of the card, around 30–40% of the card height depending on the number of fields. Internal padding around the card content is about 24–32px on all sides; vertical gaps between sections (title to paragraph, paragraph to info/map band, info/map band to form) are also in the 24–32px range, following the base 8px scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains moderate and editorial, mirroring the mockup: text lines are comfortably readable with traditional serif typography, and spacing is neither sparse nor cramped. The contact-info panel uses compact but legible line spacing so multiple contact methods fit in a relatively small vertical area. The map embed is sized to feel prominent but not overwhelming. Form fields are stacked in a single column with modest vertical spacing (8–16px) between labels and inputs, ensuring efficient use of space without sacrificing clarity. Overall, the page communicates that it is an informational resource first, with the form integrated cleanly rather than dominating the layout.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye begins at the red brand bar and logo in the header, moves horizontally to the right to notice utilities like language selection and search, then drops to the large red 'Contact' page title in the content card. From there it flows down through a short explanatory paragraph, reading left to right. The gaze then transitions to the left-aligned contact-info panel in the next row and continues across to the larger map on the right, similar to how the original mockup moves from text to a band of images. After absorbing where and how to reach the museum, the eye naturally descends to the centered contact form, starting at its form title and any active feedback state, then scanning down the stacked fields toward the submit button. A subtle scroll-to-top affordance remains a peripheral, tertiary element near the bottom-right of the viewport, echoing the mockup’s secondary navigation cue.

layout.overall_description: The Contact page sits on a light grey background with a conservative editorial feel. At the very top, a full-width red brand bar with the museum logo on the left and small utility elements on the right anchors the page. Below it, centered on the screen, a large white content card spans about three-quarters of the viewport width, framed by equal grey margins on both sides. Inside this card, generous but not extravagant padding creates a clean border of white space. The top of the card presents a bold red serif page title—“Contact”—followed by a compact, two-to-three-line paragraph explaining how visitors can reach the museum or send questions, with occasional colored links to emphasize key actions. Beneath this text, a horizontal content band uses the internal grid: on the left, a narrow vertical block lists address, phone, email, and visiting hours; to its right, a larger rectangular map preview fills the rest of the row, feeling similar in visual weight to the mockup’s strip of images. Under this band, separated by a consistent vertical gap, a full-width contact form stretches edge to edge within the card’s inner padding, presenting fields in a single column with clear labels and a prominent red submit button. A slim status message area tied to the form appears just below the form title, changing to green or red when feedback is needed. As users scroll, the header remains at the top of the page flow, the main card scrolls naturally, and a small scroll-to-top arrow in the lower-right corner quietly mirrors the mockup’s secondary navigation behavior, ensuring the layout feels stable, orderly, and content-first.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the overall canvas. It places a full-width header bar at the very top of the viewport, approximately 64–96px tall (about 15–20% of the initial viewport height). The header uses a red background with the museum logo left-aligned, and utility items—language selection pills and a subtle search input—grouped on the right. Below the header, after a vertical gap of about 32–48px, the controller centers a single main content card on the page. This card sits on a light grey page background and spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, capped at a fixed max-width (e.g., 1040–1200px) to maintain a classic editorial column feel. The content card has uniform padding of about 24–32px on all sides. Within the card, the controller arranges the internal components in a strict top-to-bottom order: at the top, the page-level heading and introductory text area; beneath that, a single horizontal row that houses the contact-info panel on the left and the embedded map on the right; and finally the full-width contact form at the bottom. The controller also ensures consistent vertical spacing of approximately 24–32px between these internal sections and keeps the entire structure centered as the page scrolls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The page controller’s header is statically positioned at the top of the document flow in this description (it appears at the top when the page loads and scrolls away with the content), though it can support a simple sticky behavior if desired. The centered content card scrolls normally within the document; it is not fixed. The controller is also responsible for positioning a small scroll-to-top button fixed to the bottom-right corner of the viewport, offset by about 24–32px from the edges, echoing the mockup’s peripheral navigation cue.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the main content card, the contact-info panel is placed in the middle section of the card, directly beneath the introductory text and aligned to the left edge of the card’s content area. It is part of a horizontal row that uses a 4-column internal grid: the contact-info panel spans 1 of these 4 columns, effectively occupying about 25% of the card’s inner width. To its right, separated by an 8–12px gutter, sits the map embed spanning the remaining 75%. The top edges of the panel and map align horizontally, creating a cohesive band across the card. Vertically, the panel is sized to roughly match the map’s height (around 260–320px) so the row reads as a unified strip, similar in presence to the mockup’s line of images with captions. Inside the panel, content is stacked vertically: a small section title (e.g., “Visit us” in a red or bold serif style), followed by neatly spaced lines for address, phone number, email, and opening hours. Internal padding of about 16–24px on all sides ensures the text doesn’t feel cramped. A thin, light grey border or a subtle bottom divider can echo the card-like feel of the original image tiles, but the panel remains visually restrained and informational.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The contact-info panel is statically positioned within the content card and scrolls with the rest of the page. It has no sticky or floating behavior; it simply occupies its portion of the horizontal row next to the map. Its height may grow slightly if more contact details are added, but should aim to stay visually balanced with the map embed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-map embed is positioned on the same horizontal row as the contact-info panel, immediately to its right. Within the internal 4-column grid, the map spans 3 columns—about 75% of the content card’s inner width—making it the dominant element of this row. An 8–12px horizontal gutter separates it from the contact-info panel. The top of the map aligns with the top of the contact-info panel, and its height ranges around 260–320px, forming a broad horizontal rectangle that visually recalls the photo strip from the mockup. The map’s left edge aligns with the right edge of the info panel, and its right edge aligns with the card’s inner padding boundary. This placement makes the row feel like a continuous band of visual content anchored by text on the left. The map container may include a subtle 1px grey border and slightly rounded corners consistent with any card styling present in the design, but remains flat and simple, in line with the conservative visual style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The map embed is static within the content card and scrolls naturally with the page. It does not stick or float as the user scrolls. On narrower viewports, this element should stack below the contact-info panel while retaining a comfortable vertical gap (about 16–24px), but in the desktop layout described here it remains side-by-side in a single row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact form occupies the entire width of the content card below the contact-info and map row. A vertical gap of about 24–32px separates it from the row above, clearly marking it as a new section while maintaining the overall vertical rhythm. The form’s left and right edges align with the inner padding boundaries of the card, so it spans 100% of the card’s content width. At the top of the form section, a concise heading such as “Send us a message” is set in the same red serif style as other section headings, left-aligned with the page title and intro text above. Directly beneath this heading, the form feedback area (status indicator) is reserved in a low-profile horizontal strip before the first field begins. Below that strip, fields are stacked in a single column: name, email, subject, message, and any additional options (e.g., visit type, preferred date). Each field has its label above the input, with 4–8px spacing between label and input and 8–16px between each field group, preserving the page’s moderate density. The bottom of the form features a left-aligned red submit button with compact padding (around 8px vertical and 16px horizontal), matching the button and pill style from the mockup. A final margin of about 32–48px below the form separates it from any page footer content, giving the layout a deliberate, finished feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The contact form is statically positioned as the last major section inside the main content card and scrolls with the page. It does not float or stick. Its height expands as needed based on the number of fields and validation messages, but the left/right alignment and spacing relative to the info/map row above remain fixed to preserve the editorial column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form-feedback component is visually integrated into the top of the contact form section rather than treated as a separate block. Within the form’s full-width area, it appears directly under the form heading and above the first input field, spanning the same width as the form content. In its active state, it takes the form of a low-height horizontal bar (approximately 40–56px tall) with a colored background—green for success, red for errors—subtly inset within the card’s white background. Text is left-aligned, concise, and set in the same serif or a compatible sans-serif as the rest of the page, with an icon (e.g., checkmark or exclamation) aligned at the left for quick scanning. The feedback area maintains consistent vertical padding (8–12px) so it feels integrated but not bulky. When inactive, the component either collapses to a minimal spacer (4–8px) or becomes visually transparent while preserving a small reserved space to prevent the form fields from jumping, maintaining the conservative, stable feel of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The form feedback component is static and anchored to the contact form; it scrolls with the form and does not float independently. It appears and disappears (or changes height) in place as the user submits the form or triggers validation, but its horizontal alignment and relationship to the form heading and first field remain fixed.

name: Policies
title: Policies
description: Displays all museum policies for the website, ticketing, and shop, and lets users navigate directly to specific sections or contact the museum with policy-related questions.

core_features: Load all policy sections dynamically via getPoliciesContent and render them in a structured list.
Support deep linking to a specific policy section using the section URL parameter, matching either a section id or category from getPoliciesContent.
Provide an in-page table of contents that lists all policy section titles and allows users to jump between sections while updating the URL.
Within each policy section, provide a "Contact us about this policy" link that navigates to contact.html with a prefilled subject parameter.
Handle loading and error states for the getPoliciesContent interface, with an option to retry loading.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View all policies
1. User opens policies.html without any URL parameters.
2. The page controller parses the URL (no section parameter) and triggers getPoliciesContent.
3. getPoliciesContent returns an object containing an array of sections.
4. The page displays a table of contents built from the returned sections, then renders each section with its title and content.
5. The first section in the returned list is treated as the default active section for highlighting, if highlighting is implemented.

Workflow: Open policies from another page focusing on a specific policy category
1. User clicks a policies link on cart.html, visit.html, or shop.html that points to policies.html with a section parameter (for example, a value aligned with a section id or section category).
2. policies.html loads and parses the section URL parameter.
3. The page triggers getPoliciesContent and waits for the sections.
4. After sections are loaded, the page tries to find a target section:
   - First, it searches for a section where section.id equals the section URL parameter.
   - If not found, it searches for a section where section.category equals the section URL parameter.
   - If still not found, it falls back to the first section in the list.
5. The page scrolls to the resolved target section and marks it as the active section in the UI state.
6. The browser URL remains policies.html with the section query parameter so the view is shareable/bookmarkable.

Workflow: Navigate between policy sections using the table of contents
1. User is on policies.html with all sections loaded via getPoliciesContent.
2. User clicks on a section entry in the table of contents.
3. The click handler identifies the corresponding section object (using its id from getPoliciesContent) and updates the URL to policies.html?section=<section.id> using the History API.
4. The activeSectionId in page state is updated to the selected section id.
5. The scroll manager scrolls the page to the selected section’s anchor.
6. If the user uses the browser Back or Forward buttons, the URL changes and the page reacts by updating activeSectionId and scrolling to the appropriate section again.

Workflow: Ask a question about a specific policy
1. User is reading a particular policy section.
2. User clicks the "Contact us about this policy" link within that section.
3. The click navigates the browser to contact.html with the subject query parameter set to the specified value (e.g., contact.html?subject=Policy%20question), as defined in the outgoing navigation contract.
4. The contact page handles the subject parameter and can prefill the relevant form field; further interactions happen on contact.html.

Workflow: Handle loading or error states for policies
1. User opens policies.html.
2. The data loader component calls getPoliciesContent and enters a loading state.
3. If the call succeeds, loading state ends and the sections are passed to dependent components (table of contents, section list, scroll manager).
4. If the call fails, the page enters an error state and shows a non-hardcoded error message container with a retry action.
5. When the user activates the retry action, getPoliciesContent is called again.
6. On success after retry, the normal rendering flow resumes (build table of contents, render sections, scroll to target section if any).
interactions: On page load: Parse the section URL parameter (if present), trigger getPoliciesContent, and store the requested section parameter in page state for later resolution once data arrives.

On getPoliciesContent success: Store the returned sections array in state, derive the active section based on the section URL parameter (matching id, then category), set a default if necessary, and notify the scroll manager and table of contents components.
On getPoliciesContent error: Set an error flag in state and display an error panel with a retry control; do not render the table of contents or sections until data is available.
On retry click: Clear the error state, re-enter loading state, and call getPoliciesContent again.

On table-of-contents item click: Prevent default anchor behavior if needed, update activeSectionId in state, push a new URL with ?section=<targetSectionId>, and instruct the scroll manager to scroll to that section’s anchor.

On in-section contact link click: Navigate to contact.html with the subject query parameter set according to the outgoing navigation specification; no additional data from getPoliciesContent is appended to the query.

On URL change via browser navigation (Back/Forward): Detect the new section query parameter value, update the activeSectionId accordingly based on already-loaded sections, and scroll to the resolved section or to the default section if there is no match.
On window resize or layout changes: Optionally re-run scroll adjustments if necessary (e.g., to account for fixed headers) using the current activeSectionId.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary page state consists of: (1) sections: the array returned by getPoliciesContent; (2) isLoading: whether a call to getPoliciesContent is in progress; (3) loadError: whether the last call failed; (4) requestedSectionParam: the raw value of the section URL parameter as parsed from window.location; (5) activeSectionId: the id of the section currently considered active/highlighted; (6) hasInitialScrollCompleted: a flag to prevent repeated automatic scrolling.

On initial load, the page reads the section query parameter from the URL (e.g., policies.html?section=<value>) and stores it as requestedSectionParam. It sets isLoading to true and calls getPoliciesContent. When getPoliciesContent resolves, isLoading becomes false, sections is populated, and the page computes activeSectionId as follows:
1) If requestedSectionParam is defined and there exists a section where section.id equals requestedSectionParam, activeSectionId is set to that section.id.
2) Otherwise, if requestedSectionParam is defined and there exists a section where section.category equals requestedSectionParam, activeSectionId is set to that section.id.
3) Otherwise, if sections is non-empty, activeSectionId is set to the id of the first element in sections.
4) If sections is empty, activeSectionId remains undefined.

After activeSectionId is resolved for the first time and if hasInitialScrollCompleted is false, the scroll manager scrolls to the DOM element whose anchor maps to activeSectionId and then sets hasInitialScrollCompleted to true.

When a user clicks a table-of-contents item, the handler updates activeSectionId to the chosen section.id and uses the History API (pushState/replaceState) to set the URL query parameter section to that same id, without reloading the page. requestedSectionParam is updated to keep it in sync, and the scroll manager scrolls to the new section.

When the URL changes via browser Back/Forward navigation, a popstate listener re-parses the section parameter from the updated URL into requestedSectionParam and recomputes activeSectionId using the same matching logic (id first, then category, then default). The scroll manager then scrolls to the new active section.

No localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used; all state is in memory for the current page lifecycle and synchronized with the URL via the section query parameter.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page setup: reads the section URL parameter, triggers getPoliciesContent, manages overall page state (sections, loading, error, requestedSectionParam, activeSectionId), and connects data to subcomponents.
Executes getPoliciesContent on initialization or retry, exposes loading and error states, and passes the sections array and any errors to the page controller and dependent components.

Displays a list of all policy sections returned by getPoliciesContent as a clickable table of contents. Each entry reflects the section’s title and whether it is currently active, and clicking an entry updates the URL section parameter and active section state.
Renders all policy sections as discrete blocks with anchors derived from their ids. Each block shows the section title and content from getPoliciesContent and includes a policy-specific contact link.

Represents a single policy section. Binds a DOM anchor to section.id, outputs the section.title and section.content, and visually indicates when it is the active section according to activeSectionId from the controller.
Within each section, generates a clickable element that navigates to contact.html with the subject query parameter set for policy-related questions, following the outgoing navigation contract.
Listens for changes to activeSectionId and URL popstate events, and performs scrolling to the corresponding section anchor. Ensures initial automatic scrolling happens only once per page load.
Shows when getPoliciesContent fails. Presents a non-hardcoded error message container and a control that triggers a retry via the data loader.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width grid similar to a 12-column system with a main content column and an internal 4-column image grid. Each image/caption acts like one column of four within the content width.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s simple, linear, top‑to‑bottom structure. At the top, outside these components, the existing global red brand bar and utilities remain unchanged. Directly beneath that, a single centered white content card contains everything for the Policies page. Inside the card, the order is: (1) the Policies page title (large red serif heading) and a short introductory paragraph (handled by the page controller/layout template), (2) the policies-toc as a full-width, internal grid of navigation items that visually echoes the 4-column image row from the mockup, and (3) the policies-section-list as a vertical stack of detailed policy sections. The policies-contact-link appears at the bottom of the card, after all sections, as a clearly separated call-to-contact block. Non-visual helpers (policies-page-controller, policies-data-loader, policies-scroll-manager) orchestrate this arrangement, and the policies-error-panel can appear above the card content when needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s simple, linear, top‑to‑bottom structure. At the top, outside these components, the existing global red brand bar and utilities remain unchanged. Directly beneath that, a single centered white content card contains everything for the Policies page. Inside the card, the order is: (1) the Policies page title (large red serif heading) and a short introductory paragraph (handled by the page controller/layout template), (2) the policies-toc as a full-width, internal grid of navigation items that visually echoes the 4-column image row from the mockup, and (3) the policies-section-list as a vertical stack of detailed policy sections. The policies-contact-link appears at the bottom of the card, after all sections, as a clearly separated call-to-contact block. Non-visual helpers (policies-page-controller, policies-data-loader, policies-scroll-manager) orchestrate this arrangement, and the policies-error-panel can appear above the card content when needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header continues to occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport height. Below it, the Policies content card sits centered, using about 70–80% of the viewport width (fixed max width, e.g., ~1040px), with light grey page background margins on left and right. The card has 24–32px padding on all sides, matching the mockup. Within the card, the top third is given to the title and intro (roughly 25–35% of card height depending on content length), the middle portion (around 20–30%) is reserved for the dense but compact policies-toc grid, and the remaining lower half of the card is taken up by the full policies-section-list. The contact link block occupies a slim band at the very bottom but is visually separated with generous top margin. The error panel, when shown, uses the same centered width as the card, placed above it and consuming as much vertical space as its message needs.
layout_strategies.content_density: Typography and spacing mirror a moderate, editorial density. Paragraphs in sections have comfortable line lengths (approximately 60–75 characters) with line-height around 1.4–1.6, and vertical spacing of roughly 16–24px between paragraphs. Section blocks are separated from each other by 24–32px, with slightly larger 32–48px gaps between major regions (title and intro vs. TOC, TOC vs. first section, last section vs. contact link). The policies-toc is visually denser, similar to the photo thumbnail row in the mockup: items are placed in a multi-column grid with narrow 8–12px gutters and minimal vertical padding, emphasizing information volume while still leaving thin breathing room and subtle borders. Overall, the page feels information-rich but not cramped.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows a strictly vertical narrative flow, mirroring the mockup. It starts at the familiar red global header and logo, then drops into the large red 'Policies' heading at the top of the white content card. From there, it moves down to the introductory paragraph, then is pulled into the horizontally structured policies-toc grid, which forms a visual band similar to the historical photo row in the mockup. Each TOC item offers a quick entry point to a policy section. When a user clicks or scrolls, the visual flow continues down the page through the stacked policy sections, each clearly separated and easy to scan. The contact link at the bottom acts as a final call-to-action endpoint. The policies-scroll-manager quietly supports this flow by synchronizing scroll position and TOC highlighting, possibly enabling a subtle sticky behavior for the top area of the card on scroll, without introducing sidebars or competing focal elements.

layout.overall_description: The Policies page sits beneath the existing museum header: a strong red brand bar with the logo on the left and utilities (language options, search) on the right, occupying the top fifth of the viewport. Below this, centered on a light grey background, a single prominent white content card stretches across about three quarters of the screen width, following a fixed editorial grid. The card has generous 24–32px padding inside and gently rounded or squared corners, consistent with the conservative museum aesthetic. At the very top of the card, aligned to the left, a large red serif 'Policies' title sets the tone, followed by a short, calm explanatory paragraph in dark grey serif text with occasional green inline links. After a comfortable vertical gap, a full-width band of navigation appears: the policies-toc. It is organized as a simple multi-column grid of evenly sized flat TOC items, each framed by thin grey borders and separated by narrow gutters, evoking the mockup’s row of historical photo cards. These items wrap as needed but are intended to show up to four columns on wider screens, forming a horizontal field of options that invites scanning. Below the TOC, a subtle divider or extra spacing leads into the policies-section-list: a vertical stack of policy sections, each beginning with a slightly larger, often red or dark heading and followed by multiple paragraphs or bullet lists. Sections fill the width of the card’s content area and are separated by clear vertical rhythm, enabling users to read sequentially or jump between them via the TOC. At the bottom of this stack, separated by a slightly larger gap, the policies-contact-link appears as a distinct left-aligned text-and-icon link, inviting visitors to contact the museum about policy questions. Throughout, the layout remains firmly single-column, centered, and textual, emphasizing clarity and trust. The scroll-to-top behavior and TOC highlighting are handled unobtrusively by the scroll manager, while the error panel, when active, surfaces above the card as a red-accented message box that temporarily becomes the primary focal point without disturbing the overall structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the overall page frame beneath the global museum header. It places a centered content wrapper on a light grey background, spanning approximately 70–80% of the viewport width with a fixed maximum width and equal left/right margins. Inside this wrapper, it renders a single white content card that begins roughly 32–48px below the bottom edge of the red brand bar. The card runs as tall as needed for all policy content and is padded 24–32px on all sides. Within the card, the controller arranges top-level content in a vertical stack: title and introductory text at the top, a block reserved for the policies-toc directly beneath, followed by the policies-section-list, and finally the policies-contact-link at the very bottom. The policies-error-panel, when active, is placed just above the card but centered to the same width, while the policies-data-loader and policies-scroll-manager are wired into this structure invisibly to manage loading and scroll synchronization. All elements align to the left inside the card, maintaining a straight, editorial text column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level container centered in the page; it grows vertically with content but remains fixed relative to the global header. It orchestrates the vertical stacking of all visible components without any floating or overlapping.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader occupies the same spatial footprint as the main content card’s interior. While data is loading, it fills the inside of the white card with skeleton placeholders arranged to match the eventual layout: a wide, low rectangle near the top simulates the page title, a couple of full-width horizontal bars beneath hint at the intro paragraph, then a band of four smaller side-by-side rectangles mimics the policies-toc grid, and a vertical series of wider grey bars suggest the policy sections. All placeholders respect the card’s 24–32px internal padding and the 16–24px gaps between elements, so the loading state visually aligns with the final design. Once loading completes, these placeholders are removed and replaced by the actual toc, sections, and contact link.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces the card’s content area while preserving the card’s position and dimensions; appears and disappears based on loading state without shifting surrounding layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policies-toc sits inside the content card, directly beneath the page title and introductory paragraph, separated from them by approximately 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the card’s content area (minus the card’s side padding) and is visually grouped as a single band, echoing the image row in the mockup. Within this band, TOC items are laid out in a simple multi-column grid: on wide screens, up to four items per row, each occupying roughly one quarter of the usable width; on medium screens, they naturally fall back to two columns, and on small screens to a single column. Each TOC item is a low-profile flat card or pill with a thin grey border, 8–12px horizontal padding, 4–8px vertical padding, and minor 8–12px gutters between items both horizontally and vertically. The items are all equal height within a row and align to a shared baseline, forming a dense but orderly block. Text inside each TOC item is left-aligned, set in a slightly smaller serif font, and uses dark grey with red accent states (hover/active) that match the brand. The entire TOC block sits visually in the upper middle of the card, acting as a navigation strip before the deeper content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card but aligned with scroll behavior: clicking an item triggers a smooth scroll to the corresponding section below, coordinated by the scroll manager. Optionally, on taller viewports, the top edge of the TOC band can become subtly sticky to the top of the viewport once the title scrolls away, but it never detaches from the main content column or becomes a sidebar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policies-section-list is placed immediately below the policies-toc inside the card, with a clear 24–32px vertical gap and optionally a thin, light grey horizontal rule to mark the transition from navigation to content. It occupies the full internal width of the card and is structured as a vertical stack of policies-section-item components. Each section item is full-width within this column and separated from the next by a 24–32px gap, creating a clear rhythm down the page. The list begins roughly mid-card and extends to the bottom as needed, becoming the dominant vertical content area. The left edges of all section titles and paragraphs align with the left edge of the TOC items, maintaining a consistent column. No sidebars or parallel columns appear; all policy content flows in a single readable column, reinforcing the editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical stack that grows with content length. It scrolls naturally within the page; sections are used as anchor targets for TOC interactions coordinated by the scroll manager.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each policies-section-item occupies a full-width block within the section list’s single column. At the top of the item, aligned to the left, is the section heading: a serif title slightly smaller than the main page heading but larger than body text, often using the branded red or a strong dark grey. This heading has 4–8px of space above it (except the first section) and 8–12px of space below before the body content begins. Below the heading, one or more paragraphs, bullet lists, or short subsections flow vertically with 16–24px spacing between them. Text wraps within the same content width as the TOC items, maintaining the same line length. If the section contains key links (for example, specific policy documents or internal references), they appear inline as subtle green text links that punctuate the paragraphs similarly to the mockup’s inline emphasis. Each section item has its own anchor position near the top, used by the TOC for scrolling. Visually, sections remain simple, flat, and text-first: no heavy boxes, just content separated by consistent white space and occasional light dividers.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level content. Each section serves as a scroll anchor; when navigated to, it may receive a brief visual emphasis (e.g., a soft highlight background that fades) coordinated with the scroll manager, without affecting its size or position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policies-contact-link is positioned at the very bottom of the content card, directly after the last policies-section-item. There is a slightly larger vertical gap above it—around 32–48px—to distinguish it as a closing action rather than another policy section. It is left-aligned with the main text column and may be presented as a short sentence with an embedded green or red-accent link ('Contact us about policy questions'), or as a compact flat button-style link with a thin border and 8–12px horizontal and 4–8px vertical padding. The link text uses the same serif font but may be slightly bolder or colored to stand out. A small, simple icon (like an outline envelope) can sit to the left of the text, reinforcing its purpose without disrupting the conservative style. The area around this link remains relatively open, giving it visibility as the last element users encounter when scrolling to the bottom of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and anchored at the bottom of the content card; it scrolls with the rest of the page and does not stick or float. It serves as a final call-to-action once users have finished reading the policies.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The scroll manager has no direct visual footprint but conceptually inhabits the space between the policies-toc and the policies-section-list. It manages the relationship between TOC items and section anchors: when users click a TOC item, the viewport smoothly scrolls the corresponding policies-section-item so its heading appears just below the global header and any optional sticky TOC band. As users scroll manually, the scroll manager tracks which section is currently in view and highlights the corresponding TOC item (e.g., by switching its border or text color to red). If sticky behavior is used, it ensures that once the top of the TOC reaches the lower edge of the global header, the TOC band remains pinned there, preserving a predictable navigation strip while the sections continue to scroll underneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible behavioral layer. It may cause the TOC band to adopt a sticky position at the top of the viewport after a certain scroll threshold, while keeping the TOC aligned to the main content column and not introducing any sidebars or overlays.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error panel appears only when there is a problem loading or displaying policy content. When active, it is inserted into the layout directly below the global header and above the main content card, centered to the same width as the card. It is a wide, low rectangular panel with 16–24px padding, spanning the card’s width and aligned to its left and right edges, creating the impression of a stacked card above the main one. The panel background is a very light red or off-white with a strong red border or left accent bar, and the message text is dark, readable serif set in the same style as the rest of the site. There is 16–24px of vertical space separating the bottom of the error panel from the top of the content card, so the two are visually distinct. If the error prevents content from loading entirely, the panel may replace the inside of the content card instead, filling the card’s interior with a similar styled message. In either case, it appears in the main reading column, maintaining the conservative editorial layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: When displayed, it pushes the content card downward but does not overlay it. It remains fixed in the document flow at the top of the main content area and scrolls away normally as the user scrolls down the page.
